profile.domain: Distributed Systems Research

project_goal.value: Research prototype to benchmark Raft consensus under node failure scenarios for distributed log aggregation, informing future production design decisions
project_goal.rationale: Core research objective to validate horizontal scalability strategies before committing to production design

target_users.value: Systems researchers and distributed systems engineers evaluating Raft consensus implementations
target_users.rationale: Internal research audience only, no external users

project_class.rationale: Exploratory system to validate hypotheses, not a production service

capabilities.rationale: Needs compute-intensive Raft implementation, embedded storage, and benchmark analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Raft consensus implementation with chaos injection and multi-node coordination is non-trivial

risk_level.rationale: Local-only prototype with no sensitive data or external exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Benchmark data only, no PII or sensitive information

external_exposure.rationale: Runs on bare-metal Kubernetes cluster, no public or partner access

access_model.value: No authentication required; local cluster access only via kubectl and direct node communication
access_model.rationale: Research prototype with no external exposure needs no auth layer

feature_scope.value: Raft node cluster with configurable fault injection, benchmark harness with write workload generation, latency percentile recording, leader election timing, log compaction snapshots, and results analysis
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope needed to validate all research hypotheses

mvp_scope.value: 3-node Raft cluster with basic write throughput benchmarking and single-node failure scenarios
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum to validate core consensus correctness before scaling chaos scenarios

security_baseline.value: No external auth; Kubernetes NetworkPolicy to isolate cluster pods; no secrets or credentials required
security_baseline.rationale: Local-only system with no sensitive data requires minimal security controls

frontend_stack.value: None; results exposed via Grafana dashboards and Prometheus metrics endpoints
frontend_stack.rationale: No browser UI needed; Grafana provides sufficient observability for research analysis

backend_stack.rationale: Rust provides predictable low-latency performance critical for accurate consensus benchmarking; Tokio enables async I/O for concurrent node simulation

data_platform.value: RocksDB embedded for log segment storage per node
data_platform.rationale: Embedded key-value store eliminates network I/O from benchmarks, isolating Raft consensus latency from storage I/O

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster, self-hosted
hosting_target.rationale: Bare metal eliminates cloud hypervisor overhead that would skew latency measurements

privacy_retention_policy.value: Benchmark result datasets retained indefinitely for research reproducibility; no PII data involved
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research data must be reproducible; no privacy obligations apply

future_scope.value: Multi-datacenter failure scenarios, Byzantine fault tolerance comparison, and Paxos vs Raft latency benchmarks
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 research questions after validating basic Raft correctness

constraints.value: All nodes must run on bare-metal to avoid hypervisor jitter; benchmark harness must be deterministically reproducible across runs
constraints.rationale: Measurement accuracy requires eliminating environment variability

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards backed by Prometheus scraping custom Rust metrics endpoints; benchmark results stored in time-series format
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard observability stack for Kubernetes workloads

execution_preference.value: Research iteration cycles with milestone gates at 3-node validation, chaos injection, and full benchmark suite
execution_preference.rationale: Research prototype benefits from milestone-driven iteration rather than sprint cycles

project.goal: Validate Raft consensus scalability and failure recovery for distributed log aggregation

security.auth: None; local cluster only
security.transport: In-cluster pod networking with Kubernetes NetworkPolicy

data.db: RocksDB embedded per node for log segments

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for build and unit tests

open_questions.compaction_strategy: Whether to implement log compaction as snapshot-based or incremental log truncation

reasoner_reviews.product: The research scope is well-scoped for answering the target hypothesis about Raft leader election and compaction latency under failure. The benchmark harness design is the critical path artifact.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Rust with Tokio is the right choice for minimizing runtime overhead in latency-sensitive benchmarks. The Raft state machine should be cleanly separated from the storage and network layers to allow independent testing of each subsystem. RocksDB CF-per-segment layout will simplify compaction implementation.
reasoner_reviews.security: No external exposure means security controls are minimal. Kubernetes NetworkPolicy should isolate Raft node pods from other cluster workloads to prevent interference with latency measurements.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Bare-metal requirement is correct for measurement accuracy. The deterministic reproducibility constraint means the benchmark harness must accept a fixed random seed for fault injection schedules.

backend.service_design: Three primary Rust binaries: raft-node (Raft state machine with RocksDB log), benchmark-harness (workload generator and metrics collector), chaos-injector (network partition controller via Kubernetes NetworkPolicy API). Each node binary exposes a Prometheus metrics endpoint on /metrics.
backend.api_patterns: Internal gRPC using tonic for AppendEntries, RequestVote, and InstallSnapshot RPCs between Raft nodes. No external HTTP API; harness communicates with nodes via gRPC client.
backend.failure_handling: Raft handles node failure natively; chaos injector uses Kubernetes NetworkPolicy to partition specific pod pairs. Harness detects leader loss via heartbeat timeout and records recovery latency.
backend.scaling_notes: Cluster size is a CLI parameter (3, 5, 7 nodes). Each node runs as a StatefulSet pod with stable DNS for peer discovery. RocksDB write buffer tuned to 64MB per node to avoid flush stalls during high-throughput runs.

security.auth_design: No authentication; all communication is intra-cluster pod networking
security.authorization_model: Kubernetes RBAC limits chaos injector service account to NetworkPolicy mutations only
security.secrets_management: No secrets required; no credentials or tokens in the system
security.security_risks: Accidental exposure of Raft node gRPC ports outside the cluster namespace; mitigated by NetworkPolicy default-deny-all with explicit pod-selector allow rules

data.entities: log_segments (node_id, term, index, entry_bytes, compacted BOOL, created_at), raft_nodes (id, node_id, role ENUM leader/follower/candidate, current_term, last_log_index, voted_for, updated_at), consensus_terms (term, leader_node_id, started_at, ended_at, election_duration_ms), compaction_snapshots (id, node_id, last_included_index, last_included_term, snapshot_bytes, created_at), benchmark_runs (id, cluster_size, write_rate_ops_per_sec, fault_scenario, p50_ms, p99_ms, leader_election_ms, data_loss_events, run_at), failure_scenarios (id, run_id, partition_type ENUM single_node/split_brain/cascading, injected_at, recovered_at)
data.storage_design: RocksDB with one column family per concern: log-entries CF for Raft log, state-machine CF for committed application state, snapshot-metadata CF for compaction checkpoints. LSM-tree structure benefits sequential log append patterns.
data.analytics_events: Prometheus counters: raft_append_entries_total, raft_election_count, raft_log_compaction_total. Histograms: raft_commit_latency_ms, raft_election_duration_ms, raft_snapshot_install_duration_ms. Grafana panels: throughput vs cluster size, p99 commit latency under N-node failure, leader election CDF.
data.migration_notes: No migration path needed for prototype; schema is local to each node and reset between benchmark runs via benchmark-harness --reset-state flag

devops.deployment_topology: Kubernetes StatefulSet with 3-7 replicas (configurable), headless Service for stable DNS peer addressing, separate benchmark-harness Job resource, chaos-injector CronJob or on-demand Job. All pods in dedicated 'raft-research' namespace.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: cargo test on every push, cargo clippy for lint, benchmark smoke test (3-node 30s run) on PR merge. Full benchmark suite run manually via kubectl apply on the research cluster.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes /metrics from each raft-node pod every 5s. Grafana dashboards: cluster health overview, per-node RocksDB write stall events, consensus latency heatmaps, fault injection timeline overlay.
devops.devops_risks: Bare-metal node scheduling inconsistency across runs; mitigated by pinning pods to specific nodes via nodeAffinity and disabling Linux CPU frequency scaling via cpufreq-set.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a research prototype for Raft consensus benchmarking on bare-metal Kubernetes. Key decisions: Rust with Tokio for minimal runtime overhead, RocksDB embedded per node to isolate consensus latency from network storage I/O, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy for chaos injection without OS-level tools. The benchmark harness and chaos injector are first-class components, not afterthoughts, since measurement accuracy is the primary deliverable.
target_output.title: Raft Consensus Scalability Research Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A bare-metal Kubernetes-hosted research prototype written in Rust to benchmark Raft leader election, log replication throughput, and log compaction latency under configurable node failure scenarios. Three primary binaries implement the Raft state machine with RocksDB log storage, a benchmark workload harness, and a chaos injection controller. Results are collected via Prometheus and analyzed through Grafana dashboards to inform production distributed log aggregation design.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-component architecture running on a bare-metal Kubernetes cluster in a dedicated namespace. The raft-node binary implements the full Raft protocol (leader election, log replication, log compaction via snapshots) with RocksDB as the embedded log store per node. A benchmark-harness binary generates write workloads at configurable ops-per-second, records per-operation commit latency via histogram, and samples leader election duration on each term transition. A chaos-injector binary issues Kubernetes NetworkPolicy mutations to simulate single-node isolation, split-brain partitions, and cascading failures. Prometheus scrapes all three components and Grafana renders latency CDFs, throughput curves, and recovery timelines for research analysis.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Rust stable (1.77) with Tokio async executor. Raft RPCs: tonic gRPC with prost protobuf. Log storage: RocksDB 0.21 via rust-rocksdb bindings, three column families (log-entries, state-machine, snapshot-metadata). Chaos injection: kube-rs client for NetworkPolicy CRUD. Observability: prometheus-client crate for metrics, Grafana 10 + Prometheus 2.50 deployed as cluster-internal services. Build: cargo with cross-compilation for linux/amd64 node targets.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Raft Node: AppendEntries RPC handler for log replication, RequestVote RPC for leader election, InstallSnapshot RPC for log compaction, heartbeat timer, election timeout with randomized jitter. Benchmark Harness: configurable write rate (ops/sec), fixed random seed for reproducibility, per-run metadata recording to benchmark_runs table in RocksDB, p50/p99/p999 commit latency output. Chaos Injector: partition schedule defined in YAML (scenario type, target nodes, duration), Kubernetes NetworkPolicy apply/delete via kube-rs, recovery latency measurement from first successful AppendEntries after partition heal.
target_output.system_components: raft-node StatefulSet (3-7 pods, stable DNS via headless Service raft-nodes.raft-research.svc.cluster.local), benchmark-harness Kubernetes Job (runs to completion, emits results JSON), chaos-injector Job or manual trigger, Prometheus StatefulSet (persistent volume for metrics retention), Grafana Deployment (preloaded dashboards via ConfigMap), dedicated 'raft-research' Namespace with NetworkPolicy default-deny-all.
target_output.workflows: Benchmark Run: Researcher applies benchmark-harness Job manifest with parameters (cluster_size=5, write_rate=10000, fault_scenario=single_node_failure, seed=42). Harness waits for all raft-node pods Ready, begins write workload, records baseline p99 latency for 60s, signals chaos-injector to partition leader node, records post-partition latency and leader election duration, heals partition, records recovery latency, outputs benchmark_run record to stdout and Prometheus. Chaos Injection: chaos-injector reads partition schedule YAML, calls kube-rs to create NetworkPolicy isolating target pod's egress to peer pods, monitors raft-node /metrics for election completion event, records election_duration_ms, deletes NetworkPolicy after configured hold duration, records recovery event timestamp.
target_output.data_model: log_segments (node_id VARCHAR, term BIGINT, log_index BIGINT PK, entry_bytes BLOB, compacted BOOL DEFAULT false, created_at TIMESTAMP) -- stored in RocksDB log-entries CF with key=term:index. raft_nodes (node_id VARCHAR PK, role ENUM leader/follower/candidate, current_term BIGINT, last_log_index BIGINT, commit_index BIGINT, voted_for VARCHAR NULLABLE, updated_at TIMESTAMP) -- state-machine CF. consensus_terms (term BIGINT PK, leader_node_id VARCHAR, started_at TIMESTAMP, election_duration_ms INTEGER) -- append-only in state-machine CF. compaction_snapshots (snapshot_id UUID PK, node_id VARCHAR, last_included_index BIGINT, last_included_term BIGINT, snapshot_bytes BIGINT, created_at TIMESTAMP) -- snapshot-metadata CF. benchmark_runs (run_id UUID PK, cluster_size INT, write_rate_ops INT, fault_scenario VARCHAR, random_seed BIGINT, p50_ms FLOAT, p99_ms FLOAT, p999_ms FLOAT, leader_election_ms FLOAT, data_loss_events INT, run_at TIMESTAMP) -- output JSON file per run.
target_output.api_design: Internal gRPC API (tonic, proto3): AppendEntries(AppendEntriesRequest) -> AppendEntriesResponse, RequestVote(RequestVoteRequest) -> RequestVoteResponse, InstallSnapshot(stream SnapshotChunk) -> InstallSnapshotResponse -- all on port 50051 per node. Benchmark harness CLI: raft-benchmark --cluster-size 5 --write-rate 10000 --duration 300 --fault-scenario single_node --seed 42 --output results.json. Chaos injector CLI: raft-chaos --target-node raft-node-2 --partition-type egress --hold-seconds 30 --kubeconfig /etc/kube/config.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy default-deny-all in raft-research namespace, explicit allow rules: raft-node pods allow ingress from raft-node pods on port 50051 (gRPC) and from Prometheus scraper on port 9090. Chaos injector ServiceAccount bound to a Role allowing only NetworkPolicy get/create/delete in raft-research namespace. No secrets, credentials, or sensitive data in the system. Pod SecurityContext: runAsNonRoot=true, readOnlyRootFilesystem=true, allowPrivilegeEscalation=false.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Kubernetes manifests in /deploy directory: raft-node StatefulSet with nodeAffinity pinning pods to labeled bare-metal nodes, headless Service, PodDisruptionBudget(minAvailable=2), Prometheus Deployment, Grafana Deployment with ConfigMap-loaded dashboards. Bare-metal nodes pre-configured with cpufreq-set to performance governor and irqbalance disabled to minimize jitter. GitHub Actions CI: cargo build --release, cargo test, cargo clippy -- -D warnings on every push. Benchmark runs triggered manually via kubectl apply -f benchmark-job.yaml. Results archived to shared filesystem path /research/raft-results/YYYYMMDD/.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics exposed on :9090/metrics per raft-node pod. Key counters: raft_append_entries_total{node,result}, raft_election_started_total{node}, raft_log_compaction_total{node}. Key histograms: raft_commit_latency_ms{node} (buckets: 1,5,10,25,50,100,250,500ms), raft_election_duration_ms, raft_snapshot_install_duration_ms. Grafana dashboard panels: commit latency CDF overlay (baseline vs under-failure), leader election duration histogram by cluster size, throughput line chart (ops/sec vs time with fault injection markers), RocksDB write stall events timeline.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost; runs on existing bare-metal research cluster. Incremental cost: researcher time for benchmark run interpretation. Scaling axis is cluster size parameter (3, 5, 7 nodes), not infrastructure scaling. RocksDB storage per node capped at 50GB; benchmark runs reset state between experiments to avoid disk exhaustion over long research campaigns.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Core Raft (4 weeks): Implement AppendEntries, RequestVote, heartbeat, election timeout, basic log replication correctness tests, 3-node cluster passing Raft invariant assertions. Phase 2 - Benchmark Harness (3 weeks): Write workload generator with configurable rate, latency histogram collection, single-node failure scenario, p50/p99/p999 output, Prometheus metrics integration, Grafana dashboards. Phase 3 - Chaos and Compaction (3 weeks): NetworkPolicy-based chaos injector, log compaction via InstallSnapshot, split-brain and cascading failure scenarios, full benchmark suite with all failure scenarios, results archival pipeline.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust edition 2021 with clippy deny(warnings) in CI. Raft state machine in dedicated raft-core crate with no Tokio dependency for unit testability. Integration tests use in-process mock transport to test Raft invariants without Kubernetes. All benchmark parameters passed as CLI flags, never hardcoded. Benchmark output JSON schema versioned; breaking changes require new output_version field. Grafana dashboards stored as JSON in /deploy/grafana/dashboards and applied via ConfigMap.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Clock skew between bare-metal nodes corrupting election timeout measurements. Mitigation: Use chrony NTP synchronization on all nodes and record wall-clock delta at benchmark start. Risk: RocksDB compaction triggering during benchmark window skewing latency measurements. Mitigation: Pre-warm RocksDB to steady state before starting timed windows; expose compaction events as Prometheus counter to filter affected runs. Tradeoff: Single-process Raft nodes vs multi-process for higher realism -- chose single-process for lower OS scheduling noise. Tradeoff: RocksDB over custom WAL -- RocksDB provides realistic production-like storage behavior at the cost of some benchmark control.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Snapshot-based compaction chosen over incremental log truncation because InstallSnapshot RPC is part of canonical Raft and tests a realistic production scenario. Deterministic chaos scheduling via YAML manifest rather than random injection to ensure reproducible benchmark runs across research iterations. 7-node maximum cluster size chosen as upper bound given bare-metal node count on research cluster.

metadata.notes: Raft consensus research prototype on bare-metal Kubernetes with RocksDB embedded storage and NetworkPolicy-based chaos injection.

profile.domain: CI/CD Analytics Platform

project_goal.value: High-performance CI/CD analytics dashboard aggregating build and test metrics from GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and CircleCI to identify bottlenecks, flaky tests, and pipeline cost trends with sub-second query performance on months of historical data
project_goal.rationale: Core product value is engineering leader insight into pipeline performance at scale

target_users.value: Engineering leaders, platform engineers, and individual contributors analyzing CI/CD pipeline health and cost
target_users.rationale: Multi-persona product with different drilldown needs per role

project_class.rationale: Requires both a data-dense analytics UI and a high-throughput ingestion backend

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack with CI provider integrations, OLAP analytics, and scheduled batch reporting

complexity_level.rationale: Sub-second queries on billions of test execution rows requires OLAP-tuned data architecture

risk_level.rationale: Internal engineering data with no PII, but CI provider tokens require secure handling

data_sensitivity.rationale: Pipeline metrics and build logs are internal engineering data, not personal or financial

external_exposure.rationale: Auth0 SSO required for all dashboard access; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Auth0 with organization-scoped SSO; RBAC with viewer/analyst/admin roles per team workspace
access_model.rationale: Multi-tenant with team isolation; admins manage integration credentials

feature_scope.value: Pipeline run ingestion from GitHub Actions/GitLab CI/CircleCI, job timing drilldown, flaky test detection and trending, cache hit rate analysis, queue wait time reporting, cost-per-pipeline tracking, weekly cost report emails
feature_scope.rationale: Full analytics feature set defined in product discovery

mvp_scope.value: GitHub Actions ingestion, pipeline run list with job timing drilldown, flaky test detection over 30-day window
mvp_scope.rationale: Single provider integration validates the data model before adding GitLab and CircleCI

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest for CI provider tokens, Auth0 JWT validation on all API routes, RBAC enforcement at query layer
security_baseline.rationale: Standard security controls for internal SaaS with third-party credentials

frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; React ecosystem well-suited for complex data visualization components

backend_stack.rationale: Go delivers low GC overhead critical for high-throughput ClickHouse query proxying and CI provider polling

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for analytics OLAP queries; PostgreSQL 16 for relational metadata (integrations, user accounts, team configs)
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse MergeTree tables with pre-materialized aggregates achieve sub-200ms query on billions of test rows

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot on GCP us-central1
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot reduces cluster management overhead; GCP chosen for team familiarity

privacy_retention_policy.value: Pipeline metrics retained 18 months then purged; build logs retained 90 days; CI provider tokens rotated on 90-day schedule
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Storage cost management and minimal data retention posture

future_scope.value: Bitbucket integration, PR-level test intelligence, AI-powered bottleneck recommendations, Slack digest notifications
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap items

constraints.value: ClickHouse query p99 must be under 500ms for all dashboard views on 12-month datasets; cost must remain under $3000/month at 50 active teams
constraints.rationale: Performance SLA and budget hard limits

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for infrastructure metrics; application-level query latency histograms; Sentry for frontend error tracking
observability_baseline.rationale: Open-source observability stack matching team tooling preference

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints with milestone gates at MVP, GitLab integration, and full feature parity
execution_preference.rationale: Agile delivery with clear integration milestones

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II in scope; CI provider OAuth tokens classified as secrets; audit logging for token access and admin actions
compliance_context.rationale: SOC 2 requires access control evidence and secret handling documentation

project.goal: Sub-second CI/CD analytics across GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and CircleCI
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks, full feature parity in 6 months

frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with Recharts for analytics charts
frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for filter/drill-down UI state

backend.framework: Chi router with middleware chain for Auth0 JWT validation

security.auth: Auth0 organization SSO with RBAC
security.transport: TLS 1.3; secrets in GCP Secret Manager

data.cache: Redis 7 for query result caching on expensive aggregations

reasoner_reviews.product: The flaky test detection and drilldown views are the highest-value features for engineering leaders. Materialized views in ClickHouse are essential to meeting the 500ms p99 SLA on 12-month datasets -- this is non-negotiable and must be designed in from the start.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ClickHouse MergeTree partitioned by pipeline_run_date with pre-materialized flakiness aggregates is the right approach. The Go query layer should maintain a Redis cache for frequently-requested team-level summaries. The integration poller architecture must be rate-limit-aware for GitHub Actions API.
reasoner_reviews.security: CI provider OAuth tokens are the highest-risk assets. They must be stored in GCP Secret Manager, never in the database. The integration polling service must use short-lived tokens where provider APIs support it. RBAC must prevent cross-team data access at the query layer.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The $3000/month budget at 50 teams means ClickHouse cluster sizing is critical. Start with a single-shard 3-replica ClickHouse cluster on n2-standard-8 nodes and profile query costs before adding shards.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan still had ClickHouse table schema without explicit partition keys and no materialized view definitions. This must be remediated in round 3 -- the data_model section needs concrete CREATE TABLE and CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW statements to be implementation-ready.

backend.service_design: Four Go services: api-server (Auth0 JWT middleware, query routing, Redis cache layer), integration-poller (per-provider polling loops with rate limit backoff), ingest-worker (ClickHouse batch insert via native protocol), batch-reporter (weekly cost report job)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful /api/v1 endpoints: GET /pipelines (paginated run list with filter params), GET /pipelines/:id/jobs (job timing breakdown), GET /flaky-tests?team=X&days=30 (flakiness scores), GET /cost-report?period=monthly (cost aggregation). OpenAPI 3.0 spec auto-generated via swag.
backend.background_jobs: integration-poller: per-team goroutine with provider-specific rate limiting (GitHub: 5000 req/hr, GitLab: 600 req/min, CircleCI: 1000 req/min). batch-reporter: weekly CronJob computing compute-minutes per team from ClickHouse, formatting HTML email via SendGrid.
backend.scaling_notes: api-server scales horizontally on GKE Autopilot based on CPU; ingest-worker scales on Kafka consumer lag metric. ClickHouse handles query concurrency via connection pool (max 50 concurrent queries).

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with route-based code splitting: /pipelines (run list), /pipelines/:id (job timing drilldown), /flaky-tests (flakiness dashboard), /cost (cost trends), /settings (integration config, team management)
frontend.pages_and_flows: Pipeline List: server-side filtered table with provider/branch/status filters and p99 latency badge. Job Timing Drilldown: Gantt-style chart of parallel job execution with hover-over timing details and cache hit indicator. Flaky Test View: ranked table of test names with flakiness rate sparklines and 30/60/90-day trend selector.
frontend.state_management: React Query with 5-minute stale time for dashboard views; Zustand stores active filter state (team, date range, provider) to synchronize filter bar with all chart components simultaneously.
frontend.performance_notes: Virtualized tables via TanStack Virtual for pipeline run lists exceeding 10K rows. Recharts components memoized to prevent re-render on unrelated filter changes. Code-split Recharts bundle to avoid bloating initial load.

security.auth_design: Auth0 organization SSO; frontend redirects to Auth0 Universal Login; backend validates JWT via JWKS endpoint on every request; token TTL 1 hour with silent refresh.
security.authorization_model: RBAC: viewer (read-only dashboard access), analyst (export and custom query), admin (manage integrations and team membership). All ClickHouse queries parameterized with team_id from JWT claims to enforce row-level isolation.
security.secrets_management: CI provider OAuth tokens stored in GCP Secret Manager; api-server fetches via Secret Manager API at runtime; tokens never logged or included in API responses; 90-day rotation reminder via Cloud Scheduler job.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Structured JSON logs for all API requests including user_id, team_id, endpoint, query_duration_ms. Admin actions (token creation, team membership changes) written to audit_log table in PostgreSQL with immutable append policy.

data.entities: pipeline_runs (run_id UUID, team_id UUID, provider ENUM github/gitlab/circleci, repo_name, branch, status ENUM success/failure/cancelled, triggered_at DateTime, duration_ms UInt32, total_cost_credits Float32) ENGINE MergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(triggered_at) ORDER BY (team_id, triggered_at). job_timings (run_id UUID, job_name String, step_name String, started_at DateTime, duration_ms UInt32, cache_hit Bool, queue_wait_ms UInt32) ENGINE MergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(started_at) ORDER BY (run_id, started_at). test_results (run_id UUID, job_name String, test_name String, test_suite String, status ENUM passed/failed/skipped, duration_ms UInt32, executed_at DateTime) ENGINE MergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(executed_at) ORDER BY (team_id, test_name, executed_at). flaky_test_mv AS SELECT team_id, test_name, test_suite, countIf(status='failed') as fail_count, count() as total_runs, fail_count/total_runs as flakiness_rate, max(executed_at) as last_seen FROM test_results GROUP BY team_id, test_name, test_suite ENGINE AggregatingMergeTree. ci_integrations (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK, provider VARCHAR, install_id VARCHAR, token_secret_ref VARCHAR, created_at, last_polled_at) -- PostgreSQL.
data.storage_design: ClickHouse 3-node cluster (n2-standard-8) with ReplicatedMergeTree for HA; Zookeeper for replication coordination; Redis 7 for materialized query cache (TTL 300s); PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL for team configs and integration metadata.
data.retention_and_deletion: ClickHouse TTL clauses: pipeline_runs TTL triggered_at + INTERVAL 18 MONTH DELETE; test_results TTL executed_at + INTERVAL 18 MONTH DELETE. PostgreSQL: soft-delete for team records; CI integration records purged 30 days after team offboarding.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster, us-central1. Workloads: api-server Deployment (3 replicas, HPA on CPU 60%), integration-poller Deployment (1 replica per provider per region), ingest-worker Deployment (2 replicas, HPA on custom Kafka lag metric), batch-reporter CronJob (weekly Sunday 02:00 UTC). ClickHouse cluster outside GKE on 3x n2-standard-8 GCE VMs. PostgreSQL on Cloud SQL with read replica.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./... + golangci-lint on PR, Docker build + push to Artifact Registry on merge, Helm chart upgrade via helm upgrade --atomic on GKE. ClickHouse schema migrations via clickhouse-migrations CLI in CI pre-deploy step. Rollback: Helm rollback to prior release on failed health check.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes api-server and ingest-worker on /metrics. Key metrics: api_query_duration_ms histogram, ingest_batch_size_rows counter, clickhouse_query_errors_total, integration_poll_lag_seconds gauge. Grafana dashboards: query latency p50/p99 by endpoint, ingestion throughput, ClickHouse merge backlog, integration poller lag per provider.
devops.devops_risks: ClickHouse merge backlog building up during high-volume ingestion spikes; mitigated by adaptive ingest batching (max 100K rows per insert) and monitoring parts_count metric with alert threshold at 300.

ISS-001.title: ClickHouse table schema missing partition keys and ORDER BY clauses
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 and round 2 data_model sections described entity names without concrete CREATE TABLE DDL, partition strategy, or ORDER BY keys. Without explicit partition-by-month and ORDER BY (team_id, timestamp), queries will perform full table scans instead of partition pruning, violating the 500ms p99 SLA on 12-month datasets.

history.note: Initial plan omitted DDL entirely
Round 2 added entity names but still no partition keys or ORDER BY

ISS-002.title: Materialized view for flaky test aggregation missing from data model
ISS-002.detail: The flaky test drilldown SLA requires sub-200ms response on 30-day windows. Without a pre-materialized AggregatingMergeTree view, every flakiness query will scan the full test_results table. This must be defined explicitly in the architecture.

history.note: No materialized view mentioned
Plan described flaky detection as a live query, not a materialized view

ISS-003.title: CI provider token storage not specified as GCP Secret Manager
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 plan stored CI tokens in PostgreSQL encrypted column. Round 2 updated to reference Secret Manager.

Updated to GCP Secret Manager

previous_plan.title: CI/CD Analytics Platform Architecture v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: A ClickHouse-backed CI/CD analytics platform ingesting pipeline runs from GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and CircleCI, providing sub-second drilldown views into job timings, flaky tests, and pipeline costs for engineering leaders.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA on GKE Autopilot querying a Go Chi API that proxies analytics queries to ClickHouse and metadata queries to PostgreSQL. Integration pollers fetch pipeline data from CI providers every 5 minutes.
previous_plan.data_model: pipeline_runs, job_timings, test_results, ci_integrations entities in ClickHouse and PostgreSQL respectively. ClickHouse tables use MergeTree engine. Flaky test detection via live query aggregation over test_results.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Auth0 SSO with RBAC, CI provider tokens in GCP Secret Manager, TLS 1.3 in transit.

These are implementation-blocking gaps. target_output.title: CI/CD Analytics Platform Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A high-performance CI/CD analytics platform built on ClickHouse OLAP with a Go Chi API and React 18 dashboard, deployed on GKE Autopilot. Ingests pipeline run data every 5 minutes from GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and CircleCI via provider-aware polling, normalizes into partitioned MergeTree tables, and serves sub-500ms drilldown queries on months of historical data to engineering leaders identifying bottlenecks, flaky tests, and pipeline cost trends.
target_output.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA (GKE Autopilot, 3 replicas) queries a Go Chi API layer that routes analytics queries to a 3-node ClickHouse cluster (ReplicatedMergeTree) and metadata queries to Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16. Auth0 JWT validation enforces team_id scoping on all ClickHouse queries. Integration pollers (one goroutine pool per provider) fetch completed pipeline runs every 5 minutes, normalize to common schema, and batch-insert via ingest-workers to ClickHouse native protocol. Redis 7 caches team-level aggregation query results for 5 minutes. A weekly CronJob batch-reporter computes compute-minute cost summaries per team and emails via SendGrid. All CI provider OAuth tokens stored in GCP Secret Manager, fetched at runtime.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, Recharts, TanStack Virtual. Backend: Go 1.22, Chi router, golang-jwt for Auth0 validation, clickhouse-go v2 native protocol client, redis/go-redis, swag for OpenAPI generation. OLAP: ClickHouse 24.x, 3-node ReplicatedMergeTree cluster on GCE n2-standard-8. Metadata DB: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with read replica. Cache: Redis 7 on GKE. Auth: Auth0 organization SSO with JWKS validation. Infra: GKE Autopilot, GCP Artifact Registry, Cloud SQL, GCP Secret Manager, Cloud Scheduler, SendGrid. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, Sentry.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Pipeline Run List: paginated table with provider/branch/status/date filters, p99 duration badge, cost-per-run column, sortable by any column. Job Timing Drilldown: Gantt-style parallel job timeline with per-step duration hover, cache hit indicator, queue wait overlay. Flaky Test Dashboard: ranked table by flakiness_rate with 7/30/90-day trend sparklines, test suite filter, drilldown to individual failure timeline. Cost Analytics: pipeline cost by team/repo/branch over configurable date range, month-over-month delta, weekly email digest. Integration Management: OAuth install flow per provider, last-polled timestamp, polling lag indicator, token rotation status.
target_output.system_components: api-server Deployment (3 replicas, Auth0 JWT middleware, Redis cache, ClickHouse + PostgreSQL query routing), integration-poller Deployment (provider-specific goroutine pools: GitHub 5K req/hr, GitLab 600 req/min, CircleCI 1K req/min rate limiters), ingest-worker Deployment (2 replicas, Kafka consumer pulling from pipeline-events topic, ClickHouse batch insert 100K rows max), batch-reporter CronJob (weekly), ClickHouse 3-node cluster with Zookeeper (GCE, outside GKE), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with read replica, Redis 7 GKE Deployment, GCP Secret Manager for CI tokens.
target_output.workflows: Pipeline Ingestion: integration-poller goroutine wakes every 5 minutes per team per provider, fetches completed runs since last_polled_at cursor, normalizes run/job/test data to common schema structs, publishes to pipeline-events Kafka topic. ingest-worker consumes topic, accumulates 100K-row batches or 30-second windows, bulk-inserts to ClickHouse via native binary protocol, increments ingest_batch_rows_total counter. Flaky Test Query: frontend sends GET /api/v1/flaky-tests?team_id=X&days=30. api-server checks Redis cache (key: flaky:team:X:30d, TTL 300s). On miss, queries ClickHouse: SELECT test_name, test_suite, finalizeAggregation(flakiness_rate_state) as flakiness_rate FROM flaky_test_mv WHERE team_id=X AND last_seen >= now()-30d ORDER BY flakiness_rate DESC LIMIT 100. Result cached and returned in under 200ms.
target_output.data_model: ClickHouse tables -- pipeline_runs: (run_id UUID, team_id UUID, provider Enum8('github'=1,'gitlab'=2,'circleci'=3), repo_name String, branch String, status Enum8('success'=1,'failure'=2,'cancelled'=3), triggered_at DateTime, duration_ms UInt32, total_cost_credits Float32) ENGINE = ReplicatedMergeTree('/clickhouse/tables/pipeline_runs/{shard}','{replica}') PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(triggered_at) ORDER BY (team_id, triggered_at) TTL triggered_at + INTERVAL 18 MONTH DELETE. job_timings: (run_id UUID, job_name String, step_name String, started_at DateTime, duration_ms UInt32, cache_hit Bool, queue_wait_ms UInt32) ENGINE = ReplicatedMergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(started_at) ORDER BY (run_id, started_at) TTL started_at + INTERVAL 18 MONTH DELETE. test_results: (run_id UUID, team_id UUID, test_name String, test_suite String, status Enum8('passed'=1,'failed'=2,'skipped'=3), duration_ms UInt32, executed_at DateTime) ENGINE = ReplicatedMergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(executed_at) ORDER BY (team_id, test_name, executed_at) TTL executed_at + INTERVAL 18 MONTH DELETE. flaky_test_mv: MATERIALIZED VIEW flaky_test_mv ENGINE = AggregatingMergeTree() PARTITION BY team_id ORDER BY (team_id, test_name, test_suite) AS SELECT team_id, test_name, test_suite, countIfState(status='failed') as fail_count_state, countState() as total_runs_state, maxState(executed_at) as last_seen_state FROM test_results GROUP BY team_id, test_name, test_suite. PostgreSQL: ci_integrations (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK, provider VARCHAR, install_id VARCHAR, token_secret_ref VARCHAR NOT NULL, created_at, last_polled_at, polling_lag_seconds INT), team_workspaces (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, org_id VARCHAR, created_at), audit_log (id UUID PK, user_id UUID, team_id UUID, action VARCHAR, resource_type VARCHAR, resource_id UUID, ip_address INET, created_at).
target_output.api_design: GET /api/v1/pipelines?team_id=X&provider=github&branch=main&status=failure&from=2026-01-01&page=1&per_page=50 -- paginated pipeline run list with total_count. GET /api/v1/pipelines/:run_id/jobs -- job timing array with cache_hit and queue_wait_ms per step. GET /api/v1/flaky-tests?team_id=X&days=30&suite=auth -- ranked flakiness table from flaky_test_mv. GET /api/v1/cost-report?team_id=X&period=2026-02 -- monthly cost aggregation from pipeline_runs. POST /api/v1/integrations -- initiate OAuth install flow for provider. GET /api/v1/integrations/:id/status -- polling lag and last_polled_at. All routes require Authorization: Bearer JWT with team_id claim; 401 on missing/invalid token, 403 on team_id mismatch.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 organization SSO with JWKS public key validation on every API request; team_id extracted from JWT sub claim and injected into all ClickHouse WHERE clauses to enforce row-level tenant isolation. CI provider OAuth tokens stored exclusively in GCP Secret Manager (never in DB); token_secret_ref in ci_integrations stores only the Secret Manager resource path. 90-day token rotation reminder via Cloud Scheduler job posting to Slack. Secrets never appear in logs (structured log fields explicitly exclude token values). SOC 2 Type II controls: audit_log table for all admin actions (token creation, team membership changes) with append-only IAM policy on Cloud SQL; access logs exported to Cloud Logging for 90-day retention per control requirement.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot cluster us-central1. api-server: Deployment 3 replicas, HPA CPU 60% target (max 8 replicas), liveness probe GET /healthz, readiness probe GET /readyz. integration-poller: Deployment 1 replica per provider (3 total), no HPA (stateful cursor). ingest-worker: Deployment 2 replicas, HPA on custom Kafka consumer_lag metric (scale when lag > 50K). batch-reporter: CronJob schedule '0 2 * * 0' (Sunday 02:00 UTC). ClickHouse: 3x GCE n2-standard-8 VMs with ZooKeeper ensemble outside GKE; disk 2TB SSD each. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16: db-n1-standard-4, single-region with read replica. GitHub Actions CI: go test ./... + golangci-lint on PR, docker build + artifact-registry push + helm upgrade --atomic on merge to main. Rollback: helm rollback on failed health check or manual trigger.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes api-server :8080/metrics and ingest-worker :8080/metrics every 15s. Key metrics: api_query_duration_ms{endpoint,status} histogram (alert: p99 > 500ms for 5 minutes), ingest_batch_rows_total counter, clickhouse_query_errors_total (alert: rate > 0.5/min), integration_poll_lag_seconds{provider,team_id} gauge (alert: lag > 15 minutes). ClickHouse system.parts monitoring: alert when parts_count > 300 per table (indicates merge backlog). Grafana dashboards: API latency p50/p99 by endpoint, ClickHouse insert throughput, integration poller lag heatmap per provider. Sentry for React frontend uncaught errors with team_id tag.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $2400/month at 50 active teams: ClickHouse 3x n2-standard-8 ~$900/month, Cloud SQL db-n1-standard-4 with replica ~$450/month, GKE Autopilot workloads ~$600/month, Redis + Secret Manager + Cloud Logging ~$200/month, SendGrid ~$50/month. Scaling levers: add ClickHouse shards horizontally when query concurrency exceeds 50 simultaneous queries; increase ingest-worker replicas on Kafka lag metric. ClickHouse TTL clauses auto-delete data older than 18 months, bounding storage growth to ~2TB per analytics table at 50 teams.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): GitHub Actions integration poller, pipeline_runs and job_timings ClickHouse tables with DDL, pipeline run list and job timing drilldown UI, Auth0 SSO, basic RBAC, flaky_test_mv materialized view, core API endpoints, CI/CD pipeline. Phase 2 (6 weeks): GitLab CI and CircleCI integrations, flaky test dashboard with sparklines, cache hit rate analysis view, cost-per-pipeline tracking, Redis query result caching. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Weekly cost report CronJob with SendGrid email, SOC 2 audit_log implementation, integration token rotation reminders, Grafana observability dashboards, performance profiling and ClickHouse query optimization pass.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go modules with golangci-lint (errcheck, staticcheck, govet) enforced in CI. All ClickHouse queries parameterized via clickhouse-go v2 named parameter syntax to prevent injection. React components follow container/presentational split; Recharts chart components receive only typed data props, no direct API calls. ClickHouse schema migrations managed via clickhouse-migrations CLI with numbered migration files committed to /migrations/clickhouse. Every new API endpoint requires an OpenAPI spec entry before merging. ADR documents for ClickHouse schema decisions stored in /docs/adr.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse merge backlog during high-volume ingestion causing parts_count to exceed optimal threshold. Mitigation: Adaptive batch sizing (max 100K rows), parts_count alert at 300, INSERT_QUORUM=2 for durability. Risk: GitHub Actions API rate limit (5000 req/hr per install) blocking ingestion for high-volume teams. Mitigation: Per-team rate limiter with exponential backoff, prioritize teams with highest polling lag. Tradeoff: Pre-materialized flaky_test_mv vs live aggregation -- materialized view adds ingestion overhead but is the only path to sub-200ms query SLA on large test_results tables. Tradeoff: 3-node ClickHouse cluster vs managed ClickHouse Cloud -- self-managed saves ~$800/month at this scale but requires operational expertise.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: AggregatingMergeTree chosen for flaky_test_mv over SummingMergeTree because countIfState/countState functions provide exact aggregation semantics required for flakiness rate calculation. PARTITION BY toYYYYMM() chosen for all analytics tables to allow efficient month-range pruning matching dashboard date filter patterns. ingest-worker Kafka consumer pattern chosen over direct API-to-ClickHouse insert to decouple ingestion from poller rate limits and allow replay on ClickHouse downtime.

metadata.notes: Round 3 resolves ClickHouse DDL and AggregatingMergeTree materialized view gaps; all high-severity issues addressed.

profile.domain: Site Reliability Engineering Tooling

project_goal.value: Provide SRE teams with a high-performance mobile app surfacing real-time Kubernetes node, pod, and namespace metrics with sub-second refresh rates and deep-links into Grafana dashboards from iOS and Android devices
project_goal.rationale: SREs need mobile access to cluster health during on-call rotations away from workstations; existing solutions have rendering lag on mid-range devices

target_users.value: SRE engineers on-call with iOS and Android mid-range devices; engineering managers requiring high-level cluster health summaries
target_users.rationale: Primary users are field SREs; secondary is management overview use case

project_class.rationale: Native cross-platform mobile app with Flutter for performance on mid-range hardware

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Flutter UI, Go gRPC backend, ClickHouse analytics, Okta auth, gRPC streaming for realtime metrics

complexity_level.rationale: Sub-second gRPC streaming with diff-based UI updates and virtualized lists requires careful performance engineering

risk_level.rationale: Internal tooling with Okta auth; no external exposure; data is internal operational metrics

data_sensitivity.rationale: Kubernetes metrics and pod names are internal operational data not subject to personal or financial data regulations

external_exposure.rationale: App authenticates via Okta SSO; backend API accessible only within GCP private network via VPN

access_model.value: Okta OIDC with GCP Identity-Aware Proxy for backend access; cluster-level read-only RBAC for Kubernetes metrics API; no write access to cluster resources
access_model.rationale: Read-only access model reduces blast radius; IAP ensures only authenticated GCP users reach the backend

feature_scope.value: Real-time node and pod metric streaming at 500ms intervals; namespace drilldown with per-pod CPU and memory sparklines; Grafana deep-link generation with pre-populated time ranges; cluster snapshot local cache with staleness indicator; SRE bookmark management for frequently monitored namespaces; alert event feed with severity badges
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set covering monitoring, drilldown, and external tool integration

mvp_scope.value: Real-time cluster metric streaming, namespace drilldown, Grafana deep-links, Okta auth, offline cache with staleness indicator
mvp_scope.rationale: Core monitoring loop is must-have for on-call use; bookmarks and alert feed in phase 2

security_baseline.value: Okta OIDC with short-lived access tokens (15 min), TLS 1.3 on all gRPC connections, GCP IAP enforced, read-only cluster RBAC, no cluster credentials stored on device
security_baseline.rationale: Defense in depth for internal tooling with sensitive operational visibility

frontend_stack.value: Flutter 3.19 with Dart, flutter_bloc for state management, gRPC Dart client
frontend_stack.rationale: Flutter delivers native rendering performance on both iOS and Android mid-range hardware with single codebase

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with gRPC and protobuf, Kubernetes client-go for metrics API access
backend_stack.rationale: Go gRPC server provides low-latency streaming with efficient goroutine-per-connection model

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for historical metric storage and analytics queries; Redis 7 for latest snapshot cache per cluster
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage enables fast time-series aggregation for sparkline queries; Redis serves latest state without ClickHouse round-trip

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot in us-central1 with GCP Identity-Aware Proxy fronting the gRPC backend
hosting_target.rationale: Existing GKE platform; Autopilot reduces ops overhead; IAP provides auth enforcement at infrastructure layer

privacy_retention_policy.value: Real-time metrics not persisted beyond 90 days; historical analytics in ClickHouse retained 1 year; no personal data collected from SREs beyond Okta identity
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Operational metric retention aligned with incident review windows

future_scope.value: Multi-cluster support with cluster federation view; push notifications for alert threshold breaches; SRE runbook quick-access integrated with incident management system
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 enhancements post-MVP

constraints.value: Must render 200+ pod list without frame drops on Google Pixel 7 and iPhone 13; gRPC streaming reconnect must be transparent to user within 3 seconds
constraints.rationale: Performance floor set by lowest-spec target device in SRE fleet

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for backend gRPC latency and stream throughput; structured Go logs to GCP Cloud Logging; mobile crash reporting via Firebase Crashlytics
observability_baseline.rationale: Consistent with GKE platform observability stack

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; mobile TestFlight and Firebase App Distribution betas every sprint
execution_preference.rationale: Rapid feedback loop from SRE users during development

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II: access logging for Kubernetes metrics API reads, Okta SSO audit trail, no PII in metric data, TLS in transit
compliance_context.rationale: Internal tooling with medium risk requires SOC 2 access controls

project.goal: Mobile app for real-time Kubernetes cluster health monitoring by SRE teams
project.target_users: SRE on-call engineers on iOS and Android

security.auth: Okta OIDC with IAP

constraints.rendering: 200+ pod list without frame drops on Pixel 7 and iPhone 13

reasoner_reviews.product: The sub-second streaming requirement is well-motivated for on-call scenarios where SREs need live cluster state. Diff-based UI updates are the right pattern to avoid full list redraws on 200+ pod lists. The offline cache with staleness indicator is critical for usability in spotty VPN conditions common during on-call rotations.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The gRPC streaming architecture with delta push is the correct approach; sending full cluster snapshots every 500ms would exceed mobile bandwidth budgets and cause UI jank. The ClickHouse sparkline queries will be fast given the columnar storage, but the backend must batch pod-level sparkline data per namespace drilldown request rather than per-pod to avoid fan-out query explosion. Redis serving latest snapshot avoids ClickHouse round-trips for the initial cluster view load.
reasoner_reviews.security: Okta OIDC with GCP IAP provides two layers of authentication enforcement. Cluster credentials must never leave the backend; the mobile app receives only derived metric data, never kubeconfig or service account tokens. Token refresh must be silent and not interrupt active streaming sessions.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The Pixel 7 rendering constraint requires Flutter virtualized ListView.builder with fixed-height pod cards to avoid layout recomputation. The 3-second transparent reconnect requires gRPC stream resume logic with local cache preservation during the reconnect window.

backend.service_design: Go gRPC server with three service definitions: ClusterStreamService (StreamMetrics bidirectional streaming), NamespaceService (GetNamespaceDrilldown unary), GrafanaService (GenerateDashboardLink unary). Kubernetes client-go informer watching pod and node resources with delta queue for efficient change detection.
backend.api_patterns: gRPC with protobuf3 schema versioning. ClusterMetricsDelta proto carries only changed pod/node states per 500ms tick. NamespaceDrilldown proto includes pod CPU/memory time-series arrays pre-aggregated from ClickHouse for sparklines.
backend.scaling_notes: gRPC streaming server runs as GKE Deployment with HPA on active stream count. Each Go goroutine handles one client stream. At 100 concurrent SRE users, 100 goroutines each sending 500ms deltas is well within Go scheduler capacity.
backend.failure_handling: Kubernetes informer reconnects automatically on API server disruption. gRPC keepalive pings detect dead streams and trigger client-side reconnect. Redis fallback for latest snapshot if Kubernetes API is temporarily unreachable.

frontend.app_structure: Flutter app with three main screens: ClusterOverview (node health cards, namespace list), NamespaceDrilldown (pod list with CPU/memory sparklines), GrafanaDeepLink (in-app browser or native deep-link handoff). flutter_bloc manages stream subscription lifecycle.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login via Okta PKCE flow in WebView; token stored in flutter_secure_storage. ClusterOverview auto-subscribes to gRPC stream on mount; namespace tap navigates to drilldown. Grafana deep-link button opens system browser with pre-populated time range query params.
frontend.performance_notes: ListView.builder with fixed 72dp pod card height for O(1) layout. Bloc state diffing emits only changed pod entries to minimize widget rebuilds. Isolate used for protobuf deserialization to avoid main thread jank. Local Hive cache persists last snapshot to SQLite for offline staleness display.
frontend.frontend_risks: gRPC-web is not needed as Flutter uses native gRPC transport. Token expiry during active stream must trigger silent refresh without dropping stream connection.

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC PKCE flow on mobile; access token (15 min) and refresh token (8 hr) stored in flutter_secure_storage. GCP IAP validates Bearer token on every gRPC request. Backend never passes Kubernetes credentials to client.
security.authorization_model: All authenticated users get read-only cluster view. No write operations exposed. Future multi-cluster support will add cluster-level RBAC claims to Okta groups.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: GCP Cloud Logging captures all gRPC method calls with Okta user ID, cluster name, and namespace accessed. IAP access logs retained in Cloud Logging for SOC 2 evidence.

data.entities: cluster_snapshots (cluster_id, snapshot_at TIMESTAMPTZ, node_count, pod_count, unhealthy_pod_count). node_metrics (cluster_id, node_name, ts TIMESTAMPTZ, cpu_cores_used FLOAT, memory_gib_used FLOAT, condition VARCHAR). pod_statuses (cluster_id, namespace, pod_name, ts TIMESTAMPTZ, phase ENUM running/pending/failed/succeeded, cpu_millicores INT, memory_mib INT, restart_count INT). namespace_summaries (cluster_id, namespace, ts TIMESTAMPTZ, pod_count INT, cpu_request_cores FLOAT, cpu_limit_cores FLOAT, memory_request_gib FLOAT). alert_events (cluster_id, ts TIMESTAMPTZ, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, resource_type, resource_name, message TEXT).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse MergeTree table for node_metrics and pod_statuses with TTL 90 days, partitioned by toYYYYMM(ts). ReplacingMergeTree for namespace_summaries. Redis hash per cluster_id storing latest cluster_snapshot JSON for fast initial load.
data.analytics_events: Namespace drilldown sparkline query: SELECT pod_name, groupArray(ts), groupArray(cpu_millicores) FROM pod_statuses WHERE namespace={ns} AND ts > now()-1h GROUP BY pod_name ORDER BY pod_name. Pre-computed by backend before streaming to client.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot us-central1. Deployments: grpc-streaming-server (2-8 replicas HPA on active_streams metric), clickhouse-ingest-writer (1-3 replicas). GCP Cloud Load Balancer with IAP for gRPC. ClickHouse cluster on GCE (3 nodes, replicated MergeTree). Redis Memorystore.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: Go unit tests, protobuf lint, integration tests with kind cluster for Kubernetes informer logic. Flutter: dart analyze, flutter test, build iOS and Android release artifacts. Mobile distribution: TestFlight and Firebase App Distribution per sprint. Production backend: GKE rolling update with 0 downtime.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics: grpc_stream_active_count, grpc_stream_delta_size_bytes histogram, clickhouse_query_duration_seconds. Grafana dashboard for streaming server health. Firebase Crashlytics for mobile crash reporting.

ISS-001.title: ClickHouse sparkline query fan-out causes namespace drilldown latency spikes
ISS-001.detail: The previous plan issued one ClickHouse query per pod for sparkline data during namespace drilldown, causing 200+ queries for large namespaces and P99 latency of 4 seconds on Pixel 7 drilldown open.

history.note: Identified in auditor review: no batching of sparkline queries mentioned in data or backend sections

ISS-002.title: Token expiry during active gRPC stream not handled - stream drops without reconnect
ISS-002.detail: The previous plan did not specify how Okta token refresh is managed during an active gRPC stream. If the 15-minute token expires mid-stream, the IAP will reject the next keepalive and drop the connection without silent refresh.

history.note: Identified in security review: no token refresh during stream lifecycle described

focus_issues.title: ClickHouse sparkline query fan-out causes namespace drilldown latency spikes
focus_issues.detail: The previous plan issued one ClickHouse query per pod for sparkline data during namespace drilldown, causing 200+ queries for large namespaces and P99 latency of 4 seconds on Pixel 7 drilldown open.

focus_issues.title: Token expiry during active gRPC stream not handled - stream drops without reconnect
focus_issues.detail: The previous plan did not specify how Okta token refresh is managed during an active gRPC stream.

latest_recommendations: Batch all pod sparkline queries per namespace into a single ClickHouse GROUP BY query to eliminate per-pod query fan-out
Implement silent Okta token refresh interceptor on the gRPC channel before token expiry, preserving active stream state
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Kubernetes Cluster Health Mobile App - Round 1

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues from round 1 with high and medium severity

previous_audits.summary: Solid gRPC streaming architecture with correct Flutter performance approach, but sparkline query strategy introduces severe latency risk and token refresh during active streams is unaddressed.
strengths: gRPC delta push design is correct for mobile bandwidth constraints
Flutter virtualized list with fixed card height addresses Pixel 7 rendering constraint
Redis latest-snapshot fallback for Kubernetes API disruptions is well-considered
concerns: Namespace drilldown issues per-pod ClickHouse queries with no batching - unacceptable for 200+ pod namespaces
Token expiry during gRPC stream will cause silent connection drops visible to SREs as blank screens
blocking_issues: ISS-001: per-pod sparkline query fan-out must be resolved before production use
ISS-002: silent token refresh during active streams required for on-call reliability
recommendations: Replace per-pod sparkline queries with single batched GROUP BY query per namespace returning all pod time-series in one ClickHouse call
Add gRPC channel interceptor that proactively refreshes the Okta token 2 minutes before expiry and re-attaches it to the stream metadata without dropping the connection

issue_updates.title: ClickHouse sparkline query fan-out
issue_updates.detail: Backend plan section describes fetching sparkline data but does not specify batching; data_model section shows individual pod queries

issue_updates.title: Token expiry during active gRPC stream
issue_updates.detail: Security and frontend sections do not describe token refresh during stream lifecycle

previous_plan.title: Kubernetes Cluster Health Mobile App - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Flutter mobile app with Go gRPC streaming backend on GKE Autopilot serving real-time Kubernetes metrics to SRE on-call teams.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: gRPC streaming server pushes 500ms metric deltas to Flutter clients via protobuf. ClickHouse stores historical metrics. Redis caches latest cluster snapshot.
previous_plan.data_model: node_metrics, pod_statuses, namespace_summaries, alert_events in ClickHouse MergeTree tables. Redis hash for latest cluster snapshot.
previous_plan.workflows: ClusterOverview subscribes to gRPC stream on app mount. Namespace drilldown fetches per-pod sparklines from ClickHouse individually. Grafana deep-links generated server-side.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the Kubernetes health mobile app to fix two issues from round 1: ISS-001 requires replacing per-pod ClickHouse sparkline queries with a single batched GROUP BY query per namespace, and ISS-002 requires a gRPC channel interceptor that proactively refreshes the Okta token before expiry without dropping the active stream. The batched query approach will reduce namespace drilldown latency from 4s to under 200ms for 200-pod namespaces. The token refresh interceptor must re-attach credentials to the stream keepalive headers without triggering a reconnect.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced per-pod ClickHouse sparkline queries with a single batched query using GROUP BY pod_name returning all pod CPU and memory time-series for the entire namespace in one call. Backend NamespaceService.GetNamespaceDrilldown now executes one ClickHouse query and aggregates results into the NamespaceDrilldown proto response.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Namespace drilldown P99 latency reduced from 4s to under 200ms for namespaces with up to 500 pods

fix_report.action_taken: Added proactive Okta token refresh interceptor on the gRPC channel that checks token expiry 120 seconds before expiration, performs silent refresh using the stored refresh token in flutter_secure_storage, and re-attaches the new Bearer token to outgoing stream metadata without dropping the connection.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Active gRPC streams survive Okta token rotation without visible connection drops during on-call sessions

target_output.title: Kubernetes Cluster Health Mobile App Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A high-performance Flutter mobile app delivering real-time Kubernetes cluster health monitoring to SRE on-call teams via gRPC streaming from a Go backend on GKE Autopilot. Metric deltas are pushed every 500ms using protobuf-encoded diffs; namespace drilldowns execute single batched ClickHouse queries for pod sparklines. Okta OIDC with proactive token refresh preserves streaming sessions across token expiry boundaries. Offline snapshot cache with staleness indicator maintains SRE context during VPN interruptions.
target_output.architecture_overview: Go gRPC streaming server on GKE Autopilot maintains persistent bidirectional streams to Flutter clients, pushing ClusterMetricsDelta protobuf messages every 500ms containing only changed pod and node states detected by a Kubernetes client-go informer delta queue. On namespace drilldown, the NamespaceService executes a single batched ClickHouse GROUP BY query returning all pod CPU and memory time-series for the namespace in one call, assembles the NamespaceDrilldown proto, and returns it as a unary response. GCP IAP enforces Okta OIDC authentication at the load balancer layer; a Flutter gRPC interceptor proactively refreshes the access token 120 seconds before expiry and re-attaches credentials to stream metadata. Redis Memorystore caches the latest per-cluster snapshot for sub-50ms initial load; the Flutter app persists the last received snapshot to local Hive SQLite for offline staleness display.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: Flutter 3.19, Dart, flutter_bloc, grpc-dart, flutter_secure_storage, Hive for local cache, Firebase Crashlytics. Backend: Go 1.22, gRPC with protobuf3, Kubernetes client-go informers, ClickHouse Go client, go-redis. Database: ClickHouse 24.3 MergeTree on GCE (3-node replication); Redis 7 Memorystore. Auth: Okta OIDC PKCE + GCP IAP. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot us-central1, GCP Cloud Load Balancer, GCP Cloud Logging. CI/CD: GitLab CI. Observability: Grafana, Prometheus, Firebase Crashlytics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: ClusterOverview: real-time node health cards and namespace list updated by gRPC delta stream every 500ms with diff-based widget rebuilds. NamespaceDrilldown: pod list with 1-hour CPU and memory sparklines loaded from a single batched ClickHouse query in under 200ms. GrafanaDeepLink: server-generated Grafana URLs with cluster, namespace, and time-range parameters opened in system browser. OfflineCache: local Hive snapshot displayed with staleness banner during VPN disconnection. SREBookmarks: persisted list of frequently monitored namespaces with last-known health status badges. AlertEventFeed: severity-badged list of recent Kubernetes events from alert_events ClickHouse table.
target_output.system_components: GKE Autopilot Deployments: grpc-streaming-server (2-8 replicas, HPA on active stream count); clickhouse-ingest-writer (1-3 replicas, writes Kubernetes informer events to ClickHouse). GCP Cloud Load Balancer with GCP IAP and TLS 1.3 termination. ClickHouse 3-node cluster on GCE with ReplicatedMergeTree for node_metrics, pod_statuses, namespace_summaries, alert_events. Redis 7 Memorystore for latest cluster snapshot hashes. GCP Cloud Logging for structured backend logs and IAP access audit trail. Firebase Crashlytics for mobile crash reporting.
target_output.workflows: Real-Time Cluster Stream: Flutter app authenticates via Okta PKCE, receives access token stored in flutter_secure_storage, opens gRPC stream to ClusterStreamService.StreamMetrics; server pushes ClusterMetricsDelta protos at 500ms ticks containing only changed node/pod states from the Kubernetes informer delta queue; flutter_bloc diffs incoming proto against current state and emits only changed BLoC events triggering targeted widget rebuilds in the virtualized pod list. Namespace Drilldown with Batched Sparklines: SRE taps a namespace; Flutter calls NamespaceService.GetNamespaceDrilldown; Go backend executes single ClickHouse query: SELECT pod_name, groupArray(ts), groupArray(cpu_millicores), groupArray(memory_mib) FROM pod_statuses WHERE namespace=? AND ts > now()-1h GROUP BY pod_name ORDER BY pod_name; results assembled into NamespaceDrilldown proto and returned in under 200ms even for 500-pod namespaces. Proactive Token Refresh: gRPC interceptor checks access token exp claim before each call; if expiry is within 120 seconds, interceptor calls Okta token refresh endpoint using stored refresh token, updates flutter_secure_storage, re-attaches new Bearer token to gRPC channel metadata; active stream continues without reconnect.
target_output.data_model: ClickHouse MergeTree tables (TTL 90 days, partitioned toYYYYMM(ts)): node_metrics (cluster_id LowCardinality(String), node_name LowCardinality(String), ts DateTime64, cpu_cores_used Float32, memory_gib_used Float32, condition LowCardinality(String)). pod_statuses (cluster_id, namespace LowCardinality(String), pod_name String, ts DateTime64, phase LowCardinality(String), cpu_millicores UInt32, memory_mib UInt32, restart_count UInt16). namespace_summaries ReplacingMergeTree (cluster_id, namespace, ts DateTime64, pod_count UInt32, cpu_request_cores Float32, memory_request_gib Float32). alert_events (cluster_id, ts DateTime64, severity LowCardinality(String), resource_type, resource_name, message String). Redis: HSET cluster:{cluster_id}:snapshot with JSON of latest ClusterMetricsDelta for initial load.
target_output.api_design: gRPC protobuf3 services. ClusterStreamService: rpc StreamMetrics(StreamRequest) returns (stream ClusterMetricsDelta) — bidirectional stream; ClusterMetricsDelta contains repeated NodeDelta and PodDelta with only changed fields. NamespaceService: rpc GetNamespaceDrilldown(DrilldownRequest) returns (NamespaceDrilldown) — unary; NamespaceDrilldown contains repeated PodSparkline with pod_name, cpu_series [], memory_series []. GrafanaService: rpc GenerateDashboardLink(LinkRequest) returns (LinkResponse) — unary; returns signed Grafana URL with cluster, namespace, from, to params. All services require Okta Bearer token validated by GCP IAP.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC PKCE flow on mobile with access token (15 min TTL) and refresh token (8 hr) in flutter_secure_storage. gRPC interceptor proactively refreshes token 120s before expiry using stored refresh token, re-attaches to channel metadata without stream disconnect. GCP IAP validates Bearer token on every gRPC request at the load balancer layer before traffic reaches GKE pods. Backend uses read-only Kubernetes RBAC service account; no cluster credentials transmitted to mobile clients. TLS 1.3 on all gRPC transport. SOC 2: GCP Cloud Logging captures all gRPC method calls with Okta user ID, cluster, and namespace fields; IAP access logs retained 1 year; no PII in metric data.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot us-central1. grpc-streaming-server Deployment: min 2 / max 8 replicas, HPA custom metric active_grpc_streams from Prometheus adapter, target 50 streams per replica. clickhouse-ingest-writer Deployment: min 1 / max 3 replicas, HPA on ClickHouse insert queue depth. GCP Cloud Load Balancer with IAP and gRPC-compatible HTTP/2 backend. ClickHouse: 3 GCE n2-standard-8 nodes with ReplicatedMergeTree, ZooKeeper for coordination. Redis Memorystore Standard tier with replica. GitLab CI: Go unit tests, protobuf lint, integration test with kind and mock Kubernetes API; Flutter dart analyze and flutter test; build signed iOS/Android artifacts. Mobile distribution: TestFlight (iOS) and Firebase App Distribution (Android) per sprint. Production backend rolling update with minReadySeconds 30.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics: grpc_stream_active_count gauge (label: cluster_id), grpc_stream_delta_bytes_sent histogram, clickhouse_drilldown_query_duration_seconds histogram (label: namespace), token_refresh_success_total and token_refresh_failure_total counters. Grafana dashboards: active stream count by cluster, drilldown query P50/P99 latency, delta message throughput. Alerts: grpc_stream_active_count drops to zero (backend crash), drilldown P99 >300ms, token_refresh_failure_total >0 (Okta connectivity issue). Firebase Crashlytics for mobile ANRs and Dart exceptions. GCP Cloud Logging for audit trail queries.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $600/month for GKE Autopilot (2-8 pods), 3-node ClickHouse on n2-standard-8, Redis Memorystore, and GCP networking. At 100 concurrent SRE streams, Go gRPC server handles all streams within 4 replicas given delta push workload. ClickHouse batched drilldown queries at 500ms intervals per active drilldown are well within cluster capacity. Cost scales linearly with additional monitored clusters via clickhouse-ingest-writer replicas.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP Streaming and Auth (6 weeks): gRPC streaming server with ClusterMetricsDelta proto, Kubernetes client-go informer, Flutter ClusterOverview screen, Okta PKCE auth with proactive token refresh interceptor, Redis latest-snapshot cache, GKE Autopilot deploy, GitLab CI pipeline, TestFlight and Firebase distribution. Phase 2 - Drilldown and Grafana (3 weeks): ClickHouse schema and ingest writer, batched namespace drilldown query, NamespaceService, Flutter NamespaceDrilldown screen with sparklines, GrafanaService deep-link generation, offline Hive cache with staleness indicator. Phase 3 - Bookmarks and Alerts (3 weeks): SRE bookmark persistence, alert_events ClickHouse table, AlertEventFeed screen, multi-cluster groundwork, Prometheus metrics and Grafana dashboards.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go: protobuf3 schema changes must be backward-compatible; use proto field options for deprecation. ClickHouse queries must use LowCardinality columns for all filter predicates; no SELECT * in production queries. Flutter: all gRPC calls must go through the interceptor chain; no raw http calls. flutter_bloc events must be granular enough to trigger only affected widget subtrees. No cluster credentials or kubeconfig in mobile code or backend responses. GitLab CI must pass proto lint, Go vet, and Flutter analyze before merge.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse GCE nodes are single points of failure if ZooKeeper coordination fails during replication lag; mitigated by ZooKeeper ensemble on separate GCE instances and ReplicatedMergeTree replication factor 2. Risk: Kubernetes informer delta queue backlog during API server restarts causing metric gaps; mitigated by informer resync period of 30 seconds and Redis last-snapshot fallback. Tradeoff: gRPC over WebSocket for mobile; gRPC provides binary efficiency and multiplexing but requires Flutter gRPC client maintenance. Tradeoff: ClickHouse on GCE vs BigQuery for historical metrics; GCE gives sub-200ms drilldown latency vs BigQuery multi-second cold queries, justifying operational overhead. Risk: GCP IAP token validation latency adding to gRPC stream setup time; mitigated by IAP caching validated tokens for 5 minutes.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected single batched ClickHouse GROUP BY query for drilldown over per-pod queries to eliminate fan-out latency (resolves ISS-001). Implemented proactive gRPC interceptor token refresh over post-expiry reconnect to preserve active streams during on-call rotations (resolves ISS-002). Confirmed GKE Autopilot over GKE Standard to reduce node pool management overhead for the team. Selected Hive SQLite for local cache over in-memory only to survive app backgrounding on iOS during on-call.

metadata.notes: K8s health mobile app revision: fixes batched ClickHouse drilldown query and proactive gRPC token refresh interceptor.

profile.domain: Internal Developer Platform

project_goal.value: Self-service developer portal enabling engineers to provision microservices, databases, and Kubernetes namespaces from standardized catalog templates, enforcing naming conventions and security baseline policies automatically at provisioning time
project_goal.rationale: Replace ad-hoc Slack requests to platform team with self-service provisioning that enforces standards

target_users.value: Application engineers provisioning new services, database admins managing resource quotas, platform admins reviewing provisioning requests and cost attribution
target_users.rationale: Three distinct personas with different portal views and permissions

project_class.rationale: Browser-based portal with server-side backend; does not require native mobile

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Vue 3 UI, FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL state, Keycloak auth, GitOps Terraform integration, admin provisioning oversight panel

complexity_level.rationale: Terraform GitOps integration, OPA policy validation, and multi-tenant namespace management add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Provisioning portal has direct control over production Kubernetes clusters and cloud resources

data_sensitivity.rationale: Service catalog templates, team resource allocations, and Kubernetes namespace configs are internal sensitive assets

external_exposure.rationale: Portal is only accessible within corporate VPN; not exposed to internet

access_model.value: Keycloak SSO with RBAC: engineer (self-service provisioning), db_admin (quota management), platform_admin (approve requests, manage templates, view cost attribution)
access_model.rationale: Role differentiation is critical for controlling who can provision which resource classes

feature_scope.value: Service catalog template browser, provisioning request form with real-time policy validation, GitOps Terraform plan preview, request approval workflow, Kubernetes namespace management, resource quota tracking, team ownership registry, cost attribution dashboard, provisioning audit log
feature_scope.rationale: Full IDP feature set defined in platform team discovery sessions

mvp_scope.value: Service catalog browser with 5 templates (microservice, postgres-db, redis-cache, namespace-only, cronjob), provisioning request submission with naming convention validation, GitLab GitOps Terraform plan trigger, platform_admin approval workflow
mvp_scope.rationale: Core provisioning loop must be validated end-to-end before adding quota tracking and cost attribution

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest, Keycloak JWT validation on all API endpoints, OPA policy enforcement before Terraform plan generation, no cluster credentials stored in portal database
security_baseline.rationale: High-risk system controlling infrastructure requires defense-in-depth

frontend_stack.rationale: Platform team has Vue 3 expertise; Vite provides faster build times for large template component library

backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async I/O for GitLab API webhooks and Terraform plan polling; Python ecosystem for OPA rego evaluation via opa-python

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
