data_platform.rationale: Managed PostgreSQL with HA on Azure matching existing infrastructure footprint

hosting_target.value: AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service) within corporate VPN namespace
hosting_target.rationale: AKS is the existing platform for internal tooling; VPN-restricted ingress enforced

privacy_retention_policy.value: Provisioning audit logs retained 3 years for compliance; deprovisioned resource records retained 1 year then archived; no PII stored
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Internal change audit trail requirement for SOC 2

future_scope.value: Cost attribution with Azure billing API integration, automated decommissioning workflows, drift detection for provisioned resources vs catalog spec
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap post-MVP adoption

constraints.value: Portal must not store AKS cluster credentials; all Terraform applies triggered via GitLab GitOps pipeline; provisioning actions must complete within 10 minutes end-to-end
constraints.rationale: Security requirement to prevent portal compromise from yielding cluster access

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for AKS workload metrics; structured JSON application logs via Fluent Bit to Azure Monitor; provisioning duration histograms
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing AKS observability stack extended to cover portal

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; platform team reviews provisioning correctness of each new catalog template before merging
execution_preference.rationale: Template correctness review is critical to avoid propagating misconfigured Terraform

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II in scope; internal change management policy requires approval workflow for all production namespace provisioning; audit log of all provisioning actions required
compliance_context.rationale: Infrastructure provisioning changes must meet change management controls

project.goal: Self-service IDP for microservice and infrastructure provisioning
project.target_users: Application engineers, DB admins, platform admins

frontend.state: Pinia for request form state and template catalog cache

security.auth: Keycloak SSO with JWT Bearer
security.transport: TLS 1.3 in transit; no cluster credentials in DB

data.cache: Redis 7 for template catalog and GitLab plan status polling cache

constraints.cluster_access: No AKS credentials stored in portal; Terraform via GitLab GitOps

open_questions.opa_hosting: Whether OPA runs as sidecar to FastAPI or as standalone cluster service

reasoner_reviews.product: The provisioning request form with real-time OPA policy validation is the highest-friction point for engineers. The UI must surface policy violations with actionable error messages referencing the specific naming convention rule violated, not generic error codes.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The constraint that the portal must not store AKS credentials is the key architectural driver. All Terraform applies must flow through the GitLab GitOps pipeline with the portal acting as an orchestrator that triggers pipelines and polls for completion. OPA should run as a sidecar to FastAPI to keep policy evaluation latency under 50ms without network round-trip.
reasoner_reviews.security: Keycloak JWT validation must be enforced at the FastAPI middleware layer, not just the route level. OPA policy must validate both naming conventions and resource class authorization (engineers cannot provision GPU node pools). Provisioning audit_logs must be append-only with no DELETE permission on the service account.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 10-minute provisioning SLA requires the GitLab pipeline to complete Terraform plan + apply within that window. Pre-tested templates with minimal plan evaluation time are essential. Redis polling cache for GitLab pipeline status prevents hammering the GitLab API during concurrent provisioning requests.

backend.service_design: Three FastAPI service modules: catalog (template CRUD, OPA validation), provisioning (request lifecycle, GitLab pipeline orchestration, status polling), admin (quota management, cost attribution, audit log). OPA sidecar on port 8181 for policy evaluation.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful /api/v1: GET /catalog/templates (list with filter), POST /provisioning/requests (submit, triggers OPA check), GET /provisioning/requests/:id/status (polls GitLab pipeline), POST /admin/quota-overrides (platform_admin only). OpenAPI 3.0 spec generated via fastapi built-in.
backend.failure_handling: GitLab pipeline trigger failures: exponential backoff retry (3 attempts), alert on third failure. OPA evaluation timeout: 100ms hard limit, reject request with policy_eval_timeout error. PostgreSQL connection pool via asyncpg (max 20 connections).
backend.scaling_notes: FastAPI on AKS Deployment with 2 replicas; HPA on CPU 70%. Redis for template catalog caching (TTL 5 minutes) reduces PostgreSQL read load during peak provisioning windows.

frontend.app_structure: Vue 3 SPA with Vite, route-based code splitting: /catalog (template browser), /provision (request form), /requests (my requests list), /admin (platform_admin panel with quota and cost views)
frontend.pages_and_flows: Template Catalog: card grid with resource class filter (microservice/database/namespace), search, and template detail modal showing required parameters and example Terraform output. Provisioning Form: dynamic fields per template type, real-time OPA validation feedback (green/red per field), Terraform plan preview panel, submit button disabled until all validations pass.
frontend.state_management: Pinia store: catalogStore (template list, cached 5 min), provisioningFormStore (active form fields, validation state, OPA error messages), requestsStore (my requests with polling for in-progress items).

security.auth_design: Keycloak SSO with Authorization Code PKCE flow; Vue frontend redirects to Keycloak login; FastAPI validates JWT via python-keycloak library on every request; role claims extracted from JWT for RBAC.
security.authorization_model: RBAC roles: engineer (read catalog, submit requests for own team), db_admin (submit database provisioning requests, manage quotas), platform_admin (approve all requests, manage templates, view all team resources). OPA policies enforce that engineer role cannot request GPU node pools or quota increases.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: provisioning_audit_logs table: append-only, PostgreSQL service account has INSERT but no DELETE or UPDATE. Logs include user_id, team_id, action (submitted/approved/rejected/applied), resource_type, resource_name, timestamp. Exported to Azure Monitor via Fluent Bit for SOC 2 evidence retention.
security.security_risks: Portal storing a GitLab API token with pipeline trigger scope; mitigated by scoping token to specific project, rotating every 90 days via Azure Key Vault, and auditing all pipeline trigger events.

data.entities: service_catalog_templates (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, resource_class ENUM microservice/postgres_db/redis_cache/namespace/cronjob, terraform_template_ref VARCHAR, required_params JSONB, naming_convention_rules JSONB, version INT, active BOOL, created_by UUID, created_at). provisioning_requests (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK, requested_by UUID, template_id UUID FK, resource_name VARCHAR, resource_params JSONB, status ENUM pending/validating/approved/running/completed/failed, gitlab_pipeline_id BIGINT, submitted_at, approved_by UUID NULLABLE, approved_at NULLABLE, completed_at NULLABLE). kubernetes_namespaces (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, namespace_name VARCHAR, cluster VARCHAR, resource_quota JSONB, team_ownership_id UUID FK, provisioned_at). baseline_policies (id UUID PK, policy_name VARCHAR, opa_rego_ref VARCHAR, resource_class ENUM, active BOOL, version INT). resource_quotas (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK, resource_class ENUM, limit_value INT, unit VARCHAR, current_usage INT, updated_at). provisioning_audit_logs (id UUID PK, user_id UUID, team_id UUID, action VARCHAR, resource_type VARCHAR, resource_name VARCHAR, request_id UUID FK, metadata JSONB, created_at) -- append-only.
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server (General Purpose, 4 vCores, 16GB RAM) with 1 read replica for admin reporting queries. Redis 7 on AKS for template catalog cache (key: catalog:templates:v{version}, TTL 300s) and GitLab pipeline status cache (key: pipeline:status:{request_id}, TTL 10s during active provisioning).
data.retention_and_deletion: provisioning_audit_logs: retained 3 years, archived to Azure Blob Storage after 1 year via pg_partman monthly partitions. provisioning_requests for deprovisioned resources: soft-deleted, hard-deleted after 1 year. service_catalog_templates: version-controlled, old versions soft-deleted but retained indefinitely for audit trail.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster within corporate VPN, dedicated 'idp-portal' namespace. Deployments: portal-api (FastAPI, 2 replicas), opa-sidecar (per portal-api pod), portal-frontend (Nginx static, 2 replicas), redis (1 replica with PVC). Ingress: Azure Application Gateway Ingress Controller with VPN-only IP allowlist. PostgreSQL: Azure Flexible Server outside AKS.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipeline: ruff + mypy on Python, pytest (80% coverage gate), docker build + Azure Container Registry push, helm upgrade --install on AKS via gitops pipeline on merge to main. Vue frontend: vite build + nginx container. OPA policies tested with opa test ./policies in CI.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes FastAPI :8080/metrics. Key metrics: provisioning_request_duration_seconds histogram (alert: p99 > 600s), opa_policy_eval_duration_ms histogram, gitlab_pipeline_trigger_errors_total. Grafana dashboards: provisioning throughput by template type, OPA validation failure rate by rule, pipeline trigger lag.
devops.devops_risks: GitLab API token with pipeline trigger scope is a sensitive credential; stored in Azure Key Vault, mounted via Kubernetes ExternalSecret, rotated on 90-day schedule.

ISS-001.title: OPA policy hosting architecture not decided in round 1 plan
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described OPA policy enforcement without specifying whether OPA runs as a sidecar, standalone cluster service, or embedded library. This ambiguity blocks infrastructure provisioning and latency planning.

history.note: OPA mentioned but deployment model undefined

ISS-002.title: GitLab API token credential handling not specified
ISS-002.detail: The portal requires a GitLab API token to trigger Terraform pipelines. Round 1 plan did not address how this token is stored, rotated, or scoped. Storing it in environment variables or PostgreSQL violates the security constraint.

history.note: GitLab token not mentioned in security section

focus_issues.title: OPA policy hosting architecture not decided
focus_issues.detail: OPA deployment model (sidecar vs standalone) must be decided to finalize provisioning latency budget and infrastructure manifests.

focus_issues.title: GitLab API token credential handling not specified
focus_issues.detail: Token must be stored in Azure Key Vault with scoped permissions and rotation schedule, not in DB or env vars.

latest_recommendations: Specify OPA as sidecar vs standalone service and justify latency tradeoff
Define GitLab API token storage in Azure Key Vault with Kubernetes ExternalSecret mount and 90-day rotation
Add OPA policy test suite to CI pipeline

convergence_state.reason: High-severity GitLab token security issue and OPA deployment decision both unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Round 1 plan established the correct high-level architecture but left two critical implementation gaps: OPA deployment model undefined and GitLab token security unaddressed, both blocking implementation.
strengths: Service catalog template model with OPA-backed naming convention validation is the right architecture
GitLab GitOps pipeline pattern correctly avoids storing AKS cluster credentials in portal
Keycloak RBAC roles (engineer/db_admin/platform_admin) are well-defined
concerns: OPA hosting model not specified; sidecar vs standalone has significant latency and operational implications
No mention of GitLab API token storage, scoping, or rotation in security section
provisioning_audit_logs append-only enforcement not specified at database layer
blocking_issues: ISS-002: GitLab API token is a high-risk credential that must be addressed before any staging deployment
recommendations: Choose OPA sidecar per api pod for sub-50ms policy eval without network latency; document in architecture_overview
Define GitLab token stored in Azure Key Vault, mounted via Kubernetes ExternalSecret, scoped to idp-portal GitLab project only, rotated every 90 days
Add INSERT-only PostgreSQL role for audit log writes

issue_updates.title: OPA policy hosting architecture not decided
issue_updates.detail: Round 1 plan omitted OPA deployment model entirely

issue_updates.title: GitLab API token credential handling not specified
issue_updates.detail: Security section in round 1 did not address GitLab token storage or rotation

previous_plan.title: Internal Developer Portal Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Vue 3 / FastAPI self-service developer portal on AKS enabling engineers to provision microservices and Kubernetes namespaces from Terraform-backed catalog templates with OPA policy enforcement and platform_admin approval workflows.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Vue 3 SPA querying FastAPI backend on AKS. OPA evaluates naming convention policies. GitLab GitOps pipeline applies Terraform to target clusters. Portal stores provisioning state in PostgreSQL.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Keycloak JWT auth, RBAC roles, TLS 1.3. OPA enforces naming conventions. GitLab pipeline applies Terraform without portal storing cluster credentials.
previous_plan.data_model: service_catalog_templates, provisioning_requests, kubernetes_namespaces, baseline_policies, resource_quotas, provisioning_audit_logs entities in PostgreSQL.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must resolve OPA sidecar deployment decision and GitLab API token security. OPA as sidecar per portal-api pod is the right choice -- eliminates network latency for policy evaluation (sub-50ms), avoids single-point-of-failure standalone service, and keeps policy evaluation co-located with request handling. GitLab token must go into Azure Key Vault with Kubernetes ExternalSecret, scoped to pipeline trigger only on the idp-portal project, with 90-day rotation. Also need to add INSERT-only PostgreSQL role for audit log enforcement.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified OPA as sidecar container in portal-api pods on port 8181, justified by sub-50ms policy eval latency requirement and avoiding standalone service SPOF. Added OPA sidecar to system_components and deployment_and_operations manifests.
fix_report.expected_outcome: OPA policy evaluation completes within 50ms per request without network round-trip; OPA scales with portal-api pod replicas automatically

fix_report.action_taken: Specified GitLab API token stored in Azure Key Vault, mounted via Kubernetes ExternalSecret to portal-api pods as environment variable, scoped to pipeline trigger scope on idp-portal GitLab project only, rotated every 90 days via Azure Key Vault rotation policy with Slack alert on rotation completion. Added to security_and_compliance and deployment_and_operations.
fix_report.expected_outcome: GitLab token is never stored in PostgreSQL or application config; compromise of portal database does not yield cluster access; 90-day rotation bounds exposure window

target_output.title: Internal Developer Portal Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Vue 3 and FastAPI self-service developer portal on AKS enabling engineers to provision microservices, databases, and Kubernetes namespaces from standardized Terraform-backed service catalog templates. OPA sidecars enforce naming convention and security baseline policies in under 50ms before triggering GitLab GitOps pipelines that apply Terraform to target clusters. Platform admins manage templates, approve production provisioning requests, and monitor team resource quotas and cost attribution through a dedicated admin panel.
target_output.architecture_overview: Vue 3 SPA (Vite build, Nginx container) served from AKS ingress behind Azure Application Gateway with VPN-only IP allowlist. Frontend authenticates via Keycloak Authorization Code PKCE, then queries FastAPI backend at /api/v1. Each portal-api pod runs an OPA sidecar on port 8181; FastAPI calls OPA synchronously for naming convention and resource class policy evaluation (sub-50ms, no network hop). On provisioning request approval, FastAPI triggers a GitLab CI pipeline in the idp-portal-gitops project via GitLab API, passing Terraform variable files as pipeline variables. The GitLab pipeline performs terraform plan then terraform apply against AKS using a cluster-scoped service account. Portal never receives or stores AKS credentials. Pipeline completion status polled via GitLab API with Redis cache (TTL 10s) to prevent API rate exhaustion. Provisioning audit events written to PostgreSQL via an INSERT-only service account.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Vue 3, TypeScript, Vite, PrimeVue, Tailwind CSS, Pinia, Vue Router. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, asyncpg, python-keycloak, opa-python for OPA sidecar client, redis-py, GitLab API client (python-gitlab). OPA: OPA v0.63 sidecar per portal-api pod. Database: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server with asyncpg connection pool. Cache: Redis 7 on AKS. Auth: Keycloak 24 (existing org SSO). GitOps: GitLab CI with Terraform 1.8 in pipeline containers. Infra: AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service), Azure Application Gateway Ingress, Azure Key Vault, Kubernetes ExternalSecret operator, Fluent Bit, Grafana, Prometheus.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Service Catalog Browser: filterable card grid by resource class (microservice/postgres_db/redis_cache/namespace/cronjob), template detail modal with required parameters and naming convention rules, version history. Provisioning Request Form: dynamic fields per template type, real-time OPA policy validation with field-level error messages ('Name must match pattern ^[a-z][a-z0-9-]{2,40}$'), Terraform plan preview via GitLab plan pipeline, submit button enabled only on full validation pass. Request Tracker: my-requests list with status badges (pending/validating/approved/running/completed/failed), live polling for in-progress requests, GitLab pipeline link. Admin Panel: all pending requests queue with approve/reject, template management (create/version/deactivate), team quota management, cost attribution table, provisioning audit log export.
target_output.system_components: portal-frontend Deployment (Nginx static, 2 replicas, serves Vue SPA), portal-api Deployment (FastAPI 2 replicas, each with OPA v0.63 sidecar on :8181), redis Deployment (1 replica, 4GB PVC), Azure Application Gateway Ingress Controller (VPN IP allowlist, TLS termination), Keycloak (existing org SSO, external), PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server with read replica (external to AKS), Azure Key Vault (GitLab token, rotation policy), Kubernetes ExternalSecret operator (syncs Key Vault secrets to K8s secrets), Fluent Bit DaemonSet (log forwarding to Azure Monitor), GitLab CI (idp-portal-gitops project with Terraform pipeline).
target_output.workflows: Provisioning Request: Engineer selects 'microservice' template, fills in service_name (validated in real-time by OPA sidecar: name pattern, team prefix, length), selects resource_tier (standard/large). FastAPI POSTs provisioning request, OPA policy eval returns allow/deny with specific rule IDs on deny. On allow, request saved to provisioning_requests with status=pending. platform_admin receives notification, reviews request details, approves. FastAPI triggers GitLab pipeline (POST /projects/idp-portal-gitops/pipeline) with Terraform tfvars as variables. Portal polls GitLab pipeline status every 10s via Redis-cached GET. On pipeline success, kubernetes_namespaces record created, provisioning_audit_log entry written, engineer notified. Template Update: platform_admin edits template parameters or naming convention OPA rules in admin panel. New template version created (old version soft-deleted). OPA policy bundle in GitLab repo updated via merge request. OPA sidecars reload policy bundle on 60-second poll interval.
target_output.data_model: service_catalog_templates (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR(80), resource_class ENUM microservice/postgres_db/redis_cache/namespace/cronjob, terraform_template_ref VARCHAR (GitLab project path + template filename), required_params JSONB (param name, type, description, validation_regex), naming_convention_rules JSONB (opa_rule_id, description), version INT DEFAULT 1, active BOOL DEFAULT true, created_by UUID FK users, created_at TIMESTAMP). provisioning_requests (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK teams, requested_by UUID FK users, template_id UUID FK service_catalog_templates, resource_name VARCHAR(60) NOT NULL, resource_params JSONB, status VARCHAR(20) CHECK(status IN ('pending','validating','approved','running','completed','failed')), gitlab_pipeline_id BIGINT, submitted_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now(), approved_by UUID NULLABLE, approved_at NULLABLE, completed_at NULLABLE, failure_reason TEXT NULLABLE). kubernetes_namespaces (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, namespace_name VARCHAR(60) UNIQUE, cluster VARCHAR(40), resource_quota JSONB (cpu_limit, memory_limit, storage_limit), team_ownership_id UUID FK teams, provisioned_at TIMESTAMP, deprovisioned_at NULLABLE). baseline_policies (id UUID PK, policy_name VARCHAR, opa_rego_ref VARCHAR (GitLab path to .rego file), resource_class ENUM, active BOOL, version INT). resource_quotas (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK, resource_class ENUM, quota_limit INT, unit VARCHAR, current_usage INT, updated_at TIMESTAMP). provisioning_audit_logs (id UUID PK, user_id UUID NOT NULL, team_id UUID NOT NULL, action VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL, resource_type VARCHAR(40), resource_name VARCHAR(80), request_id UUID NULLABLE, metadata JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now()) -- INSERT-only service account idp_audit_writer; no DELETE/UPDATE grants.
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/provisioning/requests -- submit request body {template_id, resource_name, resource_params}; triggers OPA eval synchronously; returns {request_id, status, opa_violations[]}. GET /api/v1/provisioning/requests/:id/status -- returns {status, gitlab_pipeline_id, pipeline_status, completed_at}; uses Redis cache for pipeline_status. GET /api/v1/catalog/templates?resource_class=microservice&active=true -- returns template list with required_params. POST /api/v1/admin/templates -- create new template version (platform_admin only). PATCH /api/v1/admin/requests/:id/approve -- approve pending request (platform_admin only). GET /api/v1/admin/audit-log?team_id=X&from=2026-01-01 -- paginated audit log export. All endpoints require Keycloak JWT Bearer; RBAC enforced via FastAPI dependency.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Keycloak JWT validation via python-keycloak JWKS verification on every FastAPI request; role claim extracted for RBAC enforcement. OPA sidecar (port 8181, localhost only) evaluates naming_convention and resource_class_authorization policies before request persisted to DB; deny responses include specific rule_id and human-readable violation message. GitLab API token stored in Azure Key Vault (secret name: idp-gitlab-pipeline-token), synced to Kubernetes secret via ExternalSecret operator, mounted as env var GITLAB_PIPELINE_TOKEN to portal-api pods; token scoped to pipeline trigger on idp-portal-gitops project only; 90-day rotation policy configured in Key Vault with Cloud Scheduler alert on rotation event. provisioning_audit_logs table: PostgreSQL role idp_audit_writer has INSERT only (no DELETE/UPDATE/SELECT on this table); portal-api connects as idp_audit_writer for audit writes, separate idp_api role for all other operations. TLS 1.3 on all Azure Application Gateway ingress; inter-pod communication on cluster network. SOC 2 controls: audit log export to Azure Monitor via Fluent Bit for 3-year evidence retention.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS cluster, VPN-restricted namespace 'idp-portal'. portal-api Deployment: 2 replicas, each pod has containers [fastapi-app (:8080), opa-sidecar (:8181)], HPA CPU 70% (max 4 replicas), liveness GET /healthz, readiness GET /readyz. portal-frontend Deployment: 2 replicas Nginx static, HPA CPU 80%. Redis: 1 replica with 4GB PVC. ExternalSecret: syncs Azure Key Vault secret idp-gitlab-pipeline-token to K8s secret gitlab-token on 1-hour refresh interval. GitLab CI (idp-portal-gitops project): stages -- validate (terraform validate + fmt check), plan (terraform plan with output saved as artifact), apply (manual gate for production, auto for dev/staging), notify (POST provisioning status back to portal-api webhook). GitLab CI .gitlab-ci.yml committed to idp-portal-gitops repo; OPA policy bundle (.rego files) in policies/ directory, loaded by OPA sidecars via bundle API on 60s poll. PostgreSQL Flexible Server: 4 vCores 16GB, Azure availability zone HA, read replica in secondary zone. Fluent Bit DaemonSet forwards structured JSON logs to Azure Monitor Log Analytics workspace. GitLab CI pipeline for portal code: ruff + mypy + pytest (80% coverage gate) on Python, vite build on Vue, docker build + ACR push + helm upgrade --atomic on AKS.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes portal-api :8080/metrics every 15s. Key metrics: provisioning_request_duration_seconds{template_type,status} histogram (alert: p99 > 600s), opa_policy_eval_duration_ms histogram (alert: p99 > 50ms), gitlab_pipeline_trigger_errors_total counter (alert: rate > 0.1/min), template_validation_failure_total{rule_id} counter (tracks which naming rules fail most often). Grafana dashboards: provisioning throughput by template type, OPA policy evaluation latency, GitLab pipeline trigger success rate, resource quota utilization by team. Azure Monitor: provisioning_audit_logs exported via Fluent Bit; alert on any DELETE/UPDATE on audit table.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS workloads: ~$300/month (2 replicas portal-api + frontend + redis). PostgreSQL Flexible Server 4 vCores: ~$250/month. Azure Application Gateway: ~$100/month. Azure Key Vault + ExternalSecret: ~$20/month. Total ~$670/month well within internal tooling budget. Scaling: portal-api HPA up to 4 replicas on request volume spikes; PostgreSQL read replica absorbs admin panel reporting queries without impacting provisioning write path.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): 5 catalog templates (microservice, postgres-db, redis-cache, namespace-only, cronjob), provisioning request form with OPA naming convention validation, GitLab GitOps Terraform pipeline integration, platform_admin approval workflow, provisioning_audit_logs, Keycloak RBAC, GitLab token in Azure Key Vault. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Resource quota tracking and enforcement, team ownership registry with self-service transfer, cost attribution dashboard with Azure billing API integration, template versioning UI, OPA policy management UI for platform_admins. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Automated deprovisioning workflows, drift detection (compare kubernetes_namespaces records vs live AKS namespace state), GitLab MR-based template review process, SOC 2 audit log export automation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python ruff + mypy strict in CI. FastAPI dependency injection for Keycloak JWT parsing (reusable Depends(get_current_user) with role check). OPA policies tested with opa test ./policies in CI before any policy merge. Vue 3 Composition API with typed Pinia stores. All Terraform templates peer-reviewed by platform team member before catalog entry creation. No AKS credentials or cluster kubeconfigs committed to any repository. ADRs for template schema changes stored in /docs/adr.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GitLab pipeline timeout causing provisioning requests stuck in 'running' status beyond 10-minute SLA. Mitigation: Portal polls GitLab pipeline every 10s with 15-minute hard timeout, auto-fails request and triggers platform_admin alert on timeout. Risk: OPA policy bundle refresh lag (60s poll) means new naming convention rules take up to 60s to propagate to all sidecars. Mitigation: Acceptable for non-real-time policy updates; document in runbook. Tradeoff: OPA sidecar per pod vs standalone OPA service -- sidecar chosen for latency and simplicity; adds ~128MB memory per portal-api pod but eliminates network SPOF. Tradeoff: GitLab GitOps for Terraform vs Atlantis -- GitLab chosen to reuse existing org platform; Atlantis would add operational overhead.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: OPA sidecar per portal-api pod chosen (not standalone service) for sub-50ms policy eval without network latency. GitLab API token scoped to pipeline trigger scope on idp-portal-gitops project only, stored in Azure Key Vault with ExternalSecret mount. INSERT-only PostgreSQL role idp_audit_writer enforces append-only audit log at database permission layer, not application layer.

metadata.notes: Round 2 resolves OPA sidecar architecture decision and GitLab token Azure Key Vault security; internal IDP on AKS with Terraform GitOps.

profile.domain: Developer Tooling

project_goal.value: Mobile app allowing developers to tail application logs from AWS CloudWatch and GCP Cloud Logging via a backend streaming relay, with real-time regex filtering, severity highlighting, and shareable log session links
project_goal.rationale: Developers debugging cloud services need mobile log access during incidents without VPN-connected laptops; existing web dashboards are unusable on phone screens

target_users.value: Software developers debugging live cloud services during incidents; SRE engineers monitoring log streams during on-call responses
target_users.rationale: Primary use case is incident debugging without workstation access

project_class.rationale: Native cross-platform mobile app with React Native for developer audience familiarity

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, integrations
capabilities.rationale: React Native frontend, Go streaming backend, PostgreSQL for session persistence, Cognito auth, WebSocket realtime, CloudWatch and Cloud Logging integrations

complexity_level.rationale: WebSocket session management with server-side regex filtering and multi-cloud log source abstraction adds meaningful complexity

risk_level.rationale: Application log streams may contain sensitive debug data; internal tool with Cognito auth; no external exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Log content is internal operational data; no personal or financial data in scope

external_exposure.rationale: Backend accessible only to Cognito-authenticated users via ALB; not reachable from public internet without VPN

access_model.value: Cognito user pools with service-based access groups; developers can only stream logs from services in their team's allowed service list; admins can stream any service
access_model.rationale: Log access scoped to team ownership prevents cross-team log leakage

feature_scope.value: Real-time log streaming from CloudWatch and GCP Cloud Logging; server-side regex filter evaluation; severity-based color highlighting; shareable log session links encoding service+environment+filter; bookmarked queries per user; session replay from stored log buffers
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set required for debugging and sharing during incidents

mvp_scope.value: CloudWatch log streaming with WebSocket, regex filter, severity highlighting, shareable session link
mvp_scope.rationale: CloudWatch is primary log source; GCP integration and session replay in phase 2

security_baseline.value: Cognito JWT with 1-hour access tokens, TLS 1.3 on WebSocket connections, service access list enforced server-side per user group, no raw log content stored server-side beyond 30-minute rolling buffer for replay
security_baseline.rationale: Log content is sensitive; retention window limited to incident debugging window

frontend_stack.value: React Native 0.74 with TypeScript, Zustand for local state, react-native-fast-websocket for low-latency stream rendering
frontend_stack.rationale: React Native chosen for developer familiarity and ecosystem; fast-websocket for high-throughput line rendering

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin, gorilla/websocket for stream multiplexing
backend_stack.rationale: Go goroutine per WebSocket session model handles hundreds of concurrent streams efficiently

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for session metadata and bookmarked queries; Redis 7 for 30-minute rolling log buffers per session
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for durable session and user data; Redis for ephemeral log replay buffers

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 behind ALB
hosting_target.rationale: Existing AWS infrastructure; Fargate removes node management overhead

privacy_retention_policy.value: Log content not persisted beyond 30-minute Redis rolling buffer; session metadata retained 90 days; shareable link tokens expire after 24 hours
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Log content retention minimized; session metadata retained for debugging analytics

future_scope.value: GCP Cloud Logging integration; session replay from Redis buffer; push notifications for error-level log spikes; team-level log search history
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 enhancements post-MVP

constraints.value: Backend must handle 200 concurrent WebSocket sessions per Fargate task; mobile UI must render 50 log lines per second without frame drops on iPhone 12
constraints.rationale: Concurrency floor set by expected incident peak; rendering performance floor set by typical log velocity

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for WebSocket session metrics; structured JSON logs from Go backend; Datadog alert on WebSocket session error rate
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing Datadog setup on ECS Fargate

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints with Expo EAS TestFlight releases every sprint
execution_preference.rationale: Fast feedback loop from developer users

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II: access logging for CloudWatch log group access, Cognito audit trail, log content not persisted beyond retention window, service access list enforced per team
compliance_context.rationale: Internal tooling medium risk requires SOC 2-aligned access controls

project.goal: Mobile log streaming app with regex filtering and shareable sessions
project.target_users: Developers and SREs debugging cloud services from mobile

backend.log_sources: AWS CloudWatch Logs API, GCP Cloud Logging API

security.auth: Cognito user pools with service access groups

reasoner_reviews.product: Real-time server-side regex filtering is the right design: evaluating regex client-side on all log lines would overwhelm mobile CPU at 50 lines/second. The shareable link encoding service+environment+filter is the highest-value collaboration feature for incident response. The 30-minute Redis buffer for replay covers the typical incident triage window without requiring persistent log storage.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go gorilla/websocket with one goroutine per session is the appropriate model for 200 concurrent streams. The CloudWatch Logs StartLiveTail API is the correct integration point for low-latency streaming versus FilterLogEvents polling. Server-side regex evaluation must use the RE2 engine (Go regexp package) to prevent ReDoS attacks from developer-supplied filter expressions. Redis sorted set keyed by session_id with timestamp scores is the right data structure for the rolling 30-minute buffer.
reasoner_reviews.security: Server-side service access list validation must be checked both at WebSocket handshake and on filter change requests; if the check is only at handshake, a filter change could be used to pivot to a different log group. Cognito JWT must be validated on WebSocket upgrade, not just on the initial HTTP request.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 200 concurrent WebSocket sessions per Fargate task target requires careful goroutine stack tuning; Go default goroutine stacks start at 8KB and grow to 1MB under recursion, so session goroutines must avoid deep call stacks.

backend.service_design: Go Gin HTTP server with two route groups: /api for REST (session management, bookmark CRUD, shareable link generation) and /ws for WebSocket stream upgrade. LogSourceManager interface with CloudWatchAdapter and CloudLoggingAdapter implementations. StreamSession struct per connection: session_id, user_id, service_name, log_group, filter_expression compiled RE2 regexp, rolling buffer Redis key.
backend.api_patterns: WebSocket upgrade at GET /ws/stream?service={svc}&env={env}&filter={regex}. REST: POST /api/sessions, GET /api/sessions/{id}, DELETE /api/sessions/{id}, POST /api/bookmarks, GET /api/bookmarks, POST /api/links (generate shareable link token), GET /api/links/{token} (resolve link to session params).
backend.failure_handling: CloudWatch StartLiveTail reconnects automatically on API disconnection with exponential backoff. WebSocket ping/pong keepalive every 30 seconds; dead connections detected and session cleaned up. RE2 regex compilation errors returned as WebSocket error frame before streaming begins.
backend.background_jobs: Redis buffer cleanup job removes sorted set entries older than 30 minutes every 5 minutes. Shareable link expiry job deletes token records older than 24 hours.

frontend.app_structure: React Native app with four screens: ServiceSelector (pick service, environment), StreamView (scrolling log list with filter bar), BookmarksView (saved queries), ShareLinkView (link generation and copy). Zustand store manages active stream state, filter expression, and received log lines.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Cognito login via Amplify. ServiceSelector shows user's allowed services from Cognito group claims. StreamView subscribes to WebSocket; incoming log lines appended to Zustand log buffer; virtualized FlatList renders visible window. Filter bar sends regex update message to backend over active WebSocket connection. Share button calls POST /api/links and copies the returned deep link URL.
frontend.performance_notes: FlatList with getItemLayout for fixed-height 48dp log rows to avoid measure overhead at 50 lines/second. Log buffer in Zustand capped at 2000 lines to prevent memory growth during long sessions. Incoming WebSocket messages batched in 100ms intervals before state update to avoid 50 individual re-renders per second.

security.auth_design: Cognito JWT validated on WebSocket HTTP upgrade request. Service access groups extracted from Cognito ID token custom claims. Service name in WebSocket query params validated against user's allowed service list before stream start.
security.authorization_model: Developer group: can stream services in their team's allow list. Admin group: can stream any service. Filter expressions validated against allowed service before execution, not just at connection time.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudWatch GetLogEvents calls logged to Datadog with user_id and log_group fields. WebSocket session open/close events logged. No log line content written to structured logs.

data.entities: stream_sessions (id UUID PK, user_id, service_name, environment, log_group_arn, filter_expression, started_at, ended_at nullable, redis_buffer_key). bookmarked_queries (id UUID PK, user_id, name, service_name, environment, filter_expression, created_at). shareable_links (id UUID PK, token VARCHAR unique, service_name, environment, filter_expression, created_by, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Redis: ZADD session:{session_id}:buffer {timestamp} {log_line_json} - sorted set with 30-minute TTL.
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 for session metadata and bookmarks. Redis 7 sorted sets for rolling log buffers (ZADD with score=epoch_ms, ZREMRANGEBYSCORE to trim entries older than 30 minutes). Shareable link tokens stored in PostgreSQL with expiry enforced at lookup time.
data.data_risks: Log line content in Redis sorted set values is in-memory only; Redis eviction policy must be allkeys-lru to prevent OOM on high-volume streams.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate us-east-1. Service: log-streaming-api (min 2 / max 8 tasks, HPA on active WebSocket connection count via Datadog metric). PostgreSQL RDS instance. Redis ElastiCache cluster. ALB with HTTPS listener and WebSocket protocol support.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Go unit tests with RE2 regex edge cases, WebSocket session lifecycle integration tests, 80% coverage gate. React Native: eslint, tsc, Jest unit tests. EAS Build for iOS/Android. ECS rolling deploy with health check grace period 60 seconds for active WebSocket drain.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM sidecar on ECS tasks. Custom metrics: websocket_active_sessions gauge, log_lines_forwarded_per_second histogram, cloudwatch_api_errors_total, regex_evaluation_latency_microseconds histogram. Datadog dashboard: session concurrency, log throughput, CloudWatch API error rate.

ISS-001.title: WebSocket filter updates not re-validated against service access list allowing log group pivot
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 only validated the service access list at WebSocket handshake. A filter change message sent over the open socket could modify the log_group parameter and redirect the stream to a log group the user is not authorized to access.

history.note: Identified in security review: access check only at handshake, not on filter update messages

ISS-002.title: Log buffer Redis sorted set has no TTL causing OOM risk on long-running sessions
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 used ZADD to populate the Redis sorted set but only called ZREMRANGEBYSCORE in the background cleanup job every 5 minutes. During a long session with high log volume, the sorted set can grow unboundedly within the 5-minute window, risking Redis OOM.

history.note: Identified in data review: ZADD without per-insert trim on high-volume streams

focus_issues.title: WebSocket filter updates not re-validated against service access list allowing log group pivot
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 only validated the service access list at WebSocket handshake. Filter update messages can modify the active log group without re-authorization.

focus_issues.title: Log buffer Redis sorted set has no TTL causing OOM risk on long-running sessions
focus_issues.detail: ZADD without per-insert trim allows Redis sorted set to grow unboundedly during high-volume sessions between 5-minute cleanup intervals.

latest_recommendations: Re-validate service access list on every WebSocket filter update message, not only at handshake, to prevent log group pivoting
Call ZREMRANGEBYSCORE inline after every ZADD to the Redis log buffer sorted set, trimming entries older than 30 minutes in the same Redis pipeline as the insert
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Real-Time Log Streaming Mobile App - Round 1

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues: high severity access control gap and medium severity Redis OOM risk

previous_audits.summary: Correct WebSocket streaming architecture with server-side regex evaluation and appropriate Redis buffer design, but contains a high severity authorization bypass via filter update messages and a Redis OOM risk from deferred buffer trimming.
strengths: Server-side RE2 regex evaluation prevents ReDoS from developer-supplied patterns
CloudWatch StartLiveTail integration is correct for low-latency streaming
Redis sorted set buffer design is appropriate for 30-minute replay window
concerns: Service access validation only at handshake allows log group pivot via filter update
ZADD without inline ZREMRANGEBYSCORE allows Redis memory unbounded growth during high-volume streams
blocking_issues: ISS-001: filter update authorization bypass is a high severity access control gap
ISS-002: Redis OOM risk must be mitigated before production deployment
recommendations: Re-validate service access list in the filter update message handler before updating the active log group
Inline ZREMRANGEBYSCORE after every ZADD using a Redis pipeline to keep sorted set bounded within the same write operation

issue_updates.title: Filter update authorization bypass
issue_updates.detail: Security section describes access check only at WebSocket upgrade, not in filter update message handler

issue_updates.title: Redis buffer OOM risk
issue_updates.detail: Data section shows cleanup job runs every 5 minutes with no per-insert trim

previous_plan.title: Real-Time Log Streaming Mobile App - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: React Native mobile app with Go WebSocket streaming backend on ECS Fargate for real-time cloud log tailing with regex filtering.
previous_plan.technology_stack: React Native 0.74, Go 1.22 Gin + gorilla/websocket, PostgreSQL 16, Redis 7, ECS Fargate, Cognito, Datadog.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Cognito JWT validated at WebSocket upgrade. Service access group checked at handshake. RE2 regex validation before stream start.
previous_plan.data_model: stream_sessions, bookmarked_queries, shareable_links in PostgreSQL. Redis ZADD sorted set per session for 30-minute log buffer.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the log streaming mobile app to fix two issues from round 1. ISS-001 requires adding service access list re-validation inside the WebSocket filter update message handler, not just at handshake, so log group pivoting via filter change is blocked. ISS-002 requires inlining ZREMRANGEBYSCORE after every ZADD in a Redis pipeline so the sorted set is trimmed to the 30-minute window on every insert, preventing OOM risk during high-volume sessions. Both fixes are surgical changes to security and data sections with minor workflow updates.

fix_report.action_taken: Added service access list re-validation in the WebSocket filter update message handler. When the client sends a filter update message containing a new service or log_group value, the backend re-validates the new service name against the user's Cognito group allow list before applying the filter change. If unauthorized, the backend sends an error frame and closes the session.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Filter update messages cannot redirect streams to unauthorized log groups; access check is enforced on every service change regardless of how the session was opened

fix_report.action_taken: Changed Redis log buffer writes to use a pipelined ZADD followed by ZREMRANGEBYSCORE(0, now_ms - 1800000) in the same Redis pipeline, executed atomically per log line insert. This trims the sorted set to the 30-minute window on every write, bounding memory growth to the maximum number of log lines receivable in 30 minutes regardless of session duration.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Redis sorted set per session bounded to 30 minutes of log content at all times; no OOM risk from long-running high-volume sessions

target_output.title: Real-Time Log Streaming Mobile App Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A React Native mobile app enabling developers to tail AWS CloudWatch logs in real time during incident debugging via a Go WebSocket backend on ECS Fargate. Server-side RE2 regex filtering reduces mobile bandwidth; service access lists are enforced on every filter update message to prevent log group pivoting. Redis sorted set buffers bound to 30-minute inline trim prevent OOM on high-volume streams. Shareable session links encode service+environment+filter for incident collaboration. Cognito scopes access to team-owned log groups.
target_output.architecture_overview: Go Gin backend upgrades incoming HTTP connections to WebSocket sessions at /ws/stream, validating the Cognito JWT and the requested service name against the user's group allow list at both handshake and on each filter update message. A per-session goroutine opens a CloudWatch Logs StartLiveTail stream, evaluates each incoming log event against the compiled RE2 filter expression, and forwards matching lines to the WebSocket client. Matching log lines are also written to a Redis sorted set (scored by epoch milliseconds) via a pipelined ZADD + ZREMRANGEBYSCORE trim to keep the buffer bounded to 30 minutes. React Native renders incoming log lines as a virtualized FlatList with fixed-height rows, batching WebSocket messages in 100ms intervals before Zustand state updates to sustain 50 lines/second without frame drops on iPhone 12.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native 0.74, TypeScript, Zustand, react-native-fast-websocket, AWS Amplify for Cognito PKCE. Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, gorilla/websocket, AWS SDK v2 for CloudWatch Logs StartLiveTail. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS. Cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache for log buffer sorted sets. Auth: Cognito user pools with service access group claims. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate us-east-1, ALB with WebSocket support. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Observability: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: LogStreamView: developer selects service and environment from allowed services list, opens WebSocket stream, receives filtered log lines rendered with severity color coding (ERROR=red, WARN=yellow, DEBUG=gray). RegexFilter: developer types filter expression; backend validates RE2 syntax, re-validates service access, recompiles and applies server-side; only matching lines forwarded. ShareableLink: POST /api/links generates token encoding service+env+filter; token resolves to same stream configuration for recipient. BookmarkedQuery: user saves service+env+filter combo with name for quick reuse. SessionReplay: Redis buffer allows replay of last 30 minutes of a session after reconnection.
target_output.system_components: ECS Fargate service (log-streaming-api, min 2 / max 8 tasks, HPA on websocket_active_sessions Datadog metric). ALB with HTTPS/443 and WebSocket idle timeout 600 seconds. PostgreSQL 16 RDS (stream_sessions, bookmarked_queries, shareable_links). Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster (sorted set per session: session:{id}:buffer, inline ZADD+ZREMRANGEBYSCORE, allkeys-lru eviction policy). Cognito user pool with custom group claims for service access lists. CloudWatch Logs StartLiveTail integration (one API connection per active session). Datadog APM sidecar per ECS task.
target_output.workflows: Real-Time Filtered Stream: developer opens StreamView, selects 'checkout-api' in staging from allowed services list; app sends WebSocket upgrade GET /ws/stream?service=checkout-api&env=staging&filter=ERROR with Cognito Bearer token; backend validates JWT, checks checkout-api in user's service allow list, compiles RE2 regexp for filter 'ERROR', opens CloudWatch StartLiveTail on /aws/ecs/checkout-api/staging log group; session goroutine evaluates each CloudWatch event against RE2 filter and forwards matching lines to WebSocket; each forwarded line is also written via Redis PIPELINED ZADD+ZREMRANGEBYSCORE to session:{id}:buffer. Filter Update with Re-Authorization: developer changes filter to 'payment-service ERROR'; backend filter update handler extracts service name from new filter params, re-validates 'payment-service' against user's group allow list; if authorized, closes CloudWatch stream for old log group, opens new StartLiveTail on payment-service log group, updates session record; if unauthorized, sends WebSocket error frame {type:'auth_error', detail:'service not in allow list'} and closes session. Shareable Link: developer taps Share, app calls POST /api/links with current service+env+filter; backend generates UUID token, stores in shareable_links table with 24-hour expiry; app copies deep link 'logstream://open?token={uuid}'; recipient opens link, app resolves token via GET /api/links/{token}, opens new WebSocket stream with same service+env+filter if authorized.
target_output.data_model: PostgreSQL tables: stream_sessions (id UUID PK, user_id VARCHAR, service_name VARCHAR, environment VARCHAR, log_group_arn TEXT, filter_expression TEXT, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ended_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable, redis_buffer_key VARCHAR). bookmarked_queries (id UUID PK, user_id, name VARCHAR, service_name, environment, filter_expression, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). shareable_links (id UUID PK, token VARCHAR unique, service_name, environment, filter_expression, created_by, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Redis: ZADD session:{session_id}:buffer {epoch_ms} {json_log_line} followed in same pipeline by ZREMRANGEBYSCORE session:{session_id}:buffer 0 {epoch_ms - 1800000} to trim entries older than 30 minutes on every insert. Redis eviction policy allkeys-lru. Sorted set max cardinality bounded by log line rate * 1800 seconds.
target_output.api_design: WebSocket endpoint: GET /ws/stream?service={svc}&env={env}&filter={regex} - Cognito JWT in Authorization header; upgrades to WebSocket; filter update via JSON message {type:'update_filter', service:{svc}, env:{env}, filter:{regex}} which triggers re-authorization. REST: POST /api/sessions (create session record), GET /api/sessions/{id} (status and metadata), DELETE /api/sessions/{id} (terminate stream), GET /api/bookmarks (list user bookmarks), POST /api/bookmarks (save query), DELETE /api/bookmarks/{id}, POST /api/links (generate shareable token, returns {token, link_url, expires_at}), GET /api/links/{token} (resolve link params). All REST endpoints require Cognito Bearer token.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito JWT validated at WebSocket HTTP upgrade using Cognito JWKS endpoint. Service access list extracted from Cognito ID token custom:allowed_services claim. Re-validated on every filter update message: if new service is not in custom:allowed_services, WebSocket error frame sent and connection closed. RE2 regex compilation (Go regexp package) prevents ReDoS from developer-supplied filter expressions; compilation error returned before stream start. TLS 1.3 on all WebSocket and REST connections via ALB. No log line content written to Datadog structured logs or ECS task stdout. Redis allkeys-lru eviction policy prevents OOM on unexpected buffer growth. Shareable link tokens are opaque UUIDs with 24-hour TTL enforced at lookup; no service credentials embedded. SOC 2: Datadog logs capture session open/close events with user_id and service_name; CloudWatch API calls logged with user context; no log content persisted beyond 30-minute Redis window.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate us-east-1. log-streaming-api service: min 2 / max 8 tasks, HPA on custom Datadog metric websocket_active_sessions (scale up at 150 sessions per task, scale down at 50). ALB with WebSocket idle timeout 600 seconds and HTTPS/443. PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for session metadata. Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster with replica for buffer sets. GitHub Actions CI: Go unit tests including RE2 edge cases and filter update authorization tests, WebSocket lifecycle integration tests, 80% branch coverage gate. ECS rolling deploy with ELB deregistration delay 60 seconds to allow active WebSocket drain before task termination. Mobile: Expo EAS Build for iOS and Android; TestFlight per sprint.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM custom metrics: websocket_active_sessions gauge (label: environment), log_lines_forwarded_per_second histogram (label: service), cloudwatch_startlivetail_reconnects_total counter, regex_compile_errors_total counter, filter_update_auth_rejections_total counter. Alerts: websocket_active_sessions approaches 200*task_count threshold (scale warning), cloudwatch_startlivetail_reconnects_total spikes (CloudWatch API degradation), filter_update_auth_rejections_total > 0 (possible access control probe). Structured Go logs include session_id, user_id, service_name, event type - never log line content.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $500/month for ECS Fargate (2-8 tasks), RDS Multi-AZ, ElastiCache cluster, and ALB. CloudWatch StartLiveTail API costs approximately $0.01 per session-hour; at 100 concurrent sessions, ~$72/month. Redis memory per session: at 1000 lines/minute for 30 minutes, each line averaging 200 bytes JSON, one session uses approximately 6MB; 200 sessions = 1.2GB Redis memory. Standard ElastiCache r7g.large covers this comfortably.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP CloudWatch Streaming (6 weeks): WebSocket backend with CloudWatch StartLiveTail, Cognito auth with service access list enforcement on handshake and filter updates, RE2 server-side filtering, Redis inline buffer trim, shareable link generation, React Native StreamView and ServiceSelector screens, ECS Fargate deploy, GitHub Actions CI, Expo EAS TestFlight releases. Phase 2 - GCP Integration and Replay (3 weeks): GCP Cloud Logging adapter behind LogSourceManager interface, session replay from Redis sorted set buffer, BookmarksView screen, push notification prototype for error log spikes. Phase 3 - Analytics and Polish (3 weeks): team-level log search history, Datadog dashboards for log throughput analytics, filter expression autocomplete in React Native UI.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go: compile all developer-supplied filter expressions with regexp.Compile before starting CloudWatch stream; reject with error frame if compilation fails. Re-validate service access on every filter update message before applying; never update active log group without authorization check. All Redis writes use pipelined ZADD + ZREMRANGEBYSCORE in the same pipeline; no standalone ZADD to log buffer keys. Never log log line content in structured logs. React Native: batch WebSocket message processing in 100ms intervals with requestAnimationFrame alignment. FlatList must use getItemLayout with fixed 48dp row height. Zustand log buffer capped at 2000 lines with FIFO eviction.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CloudWatch StartLiveTail API has a limit of 10 concurrent streams per account in some regions; mitigated by grouping multiple user sessions targeting the same log group into one shared StartLiveTail stream with fan-out at the backend. Risk: high log velocity (10K+ lines/minute) from a noisy service saturating mobile WebSocket bandwidth; mitigated by server-side regex filtering reducing forwarded volume. Tradeoff: Redis sorted set buffer vs PostgreSQL log storage; Redis gives in-memory speed for replay but loses content on Redis restart; accepted given 30-minute retention policy makes durability unnecessary. Risk: goroutine stack growth on 200 concurrent sessions if CloudWatch read goroutines recurse deeply; mitigated by using iterative CloudWatch event polling rather than recursive callbacks.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Added filter update re-authorization to prevent log group pivoting via filter change messages (resolves ISS-001). Replaced deferred Redis cleanup with inline pipelined ZADD+ZREMRANGEBYSCORE to bound sorted set growth on every insert (resolves ISS-002). Confirmed CloudWatch StartLiveTail over FilterLogEvents polling for lower-latency streaming. Selected Redis sorted set with epoch_ms score over circular buffer list to support time-range replay queries.

metadata.notes: Log streaming mobile app revision: fixes filter update authorization bypass and Redis buffer OOM risk.

profile.domain: On-Call Incident Management

project_goal.value: Mobile companion app for on-call engineers replacing pager-based alerting with push-notification-driven incident management, enabling alert acknowledgment, runbook access, teammate escalation, and incident status updates from iOS and Android devices
project_goal.rationale: Replace aging pager infrastructure with modern mobile-native on-call tooling

target_users.value: On-call engineers across all engineering teams who respond to production incidents from iOS and Android devices
target_users.rationale: Primary users are responders who need low-friction alert management during incidents

project_class.rationale: React Native for iOS and Android with Go backend API; mobile-first incident response workflows

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, realtime
capabilities.rationale: Mobile UI, Go REST API, PostgreSQL incident data, Cognito auth, PagerDuty/monitoring integrations, WebSocket real-time status updates

complexity_level.rationale: Core incident lifecycle is well-understood; complexity comes from push notification reliability and escalation timer precision

risk_level.rationale: Failed alert delivery during production incidents has direct operational impact; notification reliability is safety-critical

data_sensitivity.rationale: Incident data and runbook links are internal engineering information, not PII or financial

external_exposure.rationale: Cognito JWT required for all API access; no public endpoints

access_model.value: AWS Cognito user pools with MFA; RBAC roles: responder (acknowledge, update status, escalate), schedule_admin (manage on-call schedules and escalation policies), admin (manage alert sources and integrations)
access_model.rationale: Role separation prevents unauthorized escalation policy modification during incidents

feature_scope.value: Push notification delivery for alerts with severity and runbook link, incident acknowledgment, on-call schedule management, escalation policy engine with configurable timeout, multi-channel escalation (push + SMS + email), incident status updates (acknowledged/investigating/resolved), incident history timeline, alert source integrations (PagerDuty webhook, Datadog webhook, custom webhook)
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for replacing pager-based alerting

mvp_scope.value: Push notification delivery for critical and high severity alerts, incident acknowledgment flow, basic on-call schedule (primary + secondary), single-level escalation on 5-minute ack timeout, incident status updates
mvp_scope.rationale: Core alert-to-ack flow must be proven reliable before adding complex escalation trees and alert source integrations

security_baseline.value: Cognito MFA for all accounts, TLS 1.3 in transit, APNs/FCM tokens stored encrypted, no incident payload stored in push notification body (only incident_id), JWT expiry 1 hour with silent refresh
security_baseline.rationale: Mobile push payloads transit Apple/Google infrastructure; sensitive incident details must not be in notification payload

frontend_stack.value: React Native with TypeScript using Expo SDK 51
frontend_stack.rationale: Expo enables single codebase iOS/Android with managed push notification via Expo Notifications; team has React Native expertise

backend_stack.rationale: Go provides low-latency alert ingestion and precise timer-based escalation scheduling; Gin for HTTP API; goroutines for escalation timer workers

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on AWS RDS Multi-AZ with Redis 7 on ElastiCache for escalation timer state
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for incident records; Redis for escalation timer keys with TTL-based expiry events driving escalation logic

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 with Multi-AZ ALB
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with HA; us-east-1 for low latency to engineering teams

privacy_retention_policy.value: Incident records retained 2 years; escalation audit logs retained 3 years; push notification device tokens purged 30 days after device deregistration
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Incident history needed for post-incident review; token hygiene prevents orphaned device records

future_scope.value: Multi-level escalation trees, incident war room chat, Slack bot integration, mobile incident timeline collaboration, AI-powered runbook suggestions
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap after pager replacement proven reliable

constraints.value: Push notification delivery must achieve P99 under 10 seconds from alert source receipt; escalation timer precision within 30 seconds of configured timeout
constraints.rationale: Reliability SLAs that define success of pager replacement

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on ECS Fargate tasks; structured JSON logs; PagerDuty dead-man's-switch integration to alert if the alerting system itself goes silent
observability_baseline.rationale: Monitoring the monitoring system is essential for a high-risk on-call tool

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints with reliability-gate: each sprint must pass push delivery SLA test before feature work proceeds
execution_preference.rationale: Reliability is the primary deliverable; features must not be shipped at expense of notification delivery

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II in scope; audit log of all escalation events and acknowledgment actions required; on-call schedules treated as sensitive personnel data
compliance_context.rationale: Incident response tooling is in SOC 2 operational evidence scope

project.goal: Mobile-first on-call incident management replacing pager infrastructure
project.target_users: On-call engineers on iOS and Android

frontend.framework: React Native with TypeScript, Expo SDK 51
frontend.styling: NativeWind (Tailwind for RN), React Navigation v6
frontend.state: Zustand for incident list and active incident state; React Query for API calls

backend.framework: Gin HTTP server; escalation engine as separate Goroutine worker pool

security.auth: AWS Cognito MFA; device token encryption at rest
security.transport: TLS 1.3; no incident details in APNs/FCM payload

data.cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache for escalation timer TTL keys

constraints.escalation_precision: Escalation timer within 30s of configured timeout

open_questions.push_channel: Whether to use Expo Push Service or direct APNs/FCM integration for production reliability

reasoner_reviews.product: Push notification delivery reliability is the defining success metric. The dead-man's-switch monitoring setup is critical -- the on-call system alerting on its own silence is the correct posture for a safety-critical tool. MVP scope is appropriately conservative with single-level escalation before adding complex escalation trees.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Redis TTL-based escalation timer is the right approach over cron jobs or database polling -- Redis keyspace notifications fire within milliseconds of TTL expiry, enabling precise escalation timing. The separation of the escalation engine as a goroutine worker pool independent from the API server prevents API load from affecting escalation timer precision. Direct APNs/FCM integration is preferable to Expo Push Service for production reliability and delivery receipt tracking.
reasoner_reviews.security: No incident details in APNs/FCM notification payload is the correct security decision. Push payloads should contain only incident_id and severity_level; the mobile app fetches full incident details via authenticated API call on notification tap. Cognito MFA must be enforced at the Cognito user pool level, not just the app.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 10-second P99 push delivery requires the alert ingestion to APNs/FCM path to have minimal queuing. The Go alert ingestion handler should call APNs/FCM directly without queuing through Kafka or SQS for the primary notification path; Redis escalation timer set atomically with notification send to prevent race conditions.

backend.service_design: Three logical services in one Go binary (for MVP): alert-ingestion (webhook receivers for PagerDuty/Datadog/custom), incident-api (CRUD for incidents, schedules, escalation policies), escalation-engine (Redis keyspace notification subscriber driving timer-based escalation). Separate go binary for escalation-engine in production.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful: POST /webhooks/pagerduty (ingest PagerDuty alert), POST /webhooks/datadog (ingest Datadog alert), GET /incidents (active incidents list), PATCH /incidents/:id/acknowledge, PATCH /incidents/:id/status, GET /schedules (on-call schedule), GET /schedules/current-oncall (who is on-call now). JWT auth on all except /webhooks/* (HMAC webhook signature validation).
backend.failure_handling: APNs/FCM delivery failures: retry with exponential backoff (3 attempts, max 30s), mark push_notifications record as failed, trigger escalation immediately rather than waiting for ack timeout. Redis failover: ElastiCache Multi-AZ with automatic failover; escalation engine reconnects within 5s on primary failure.
backend.scaling_notes: ECS Fargate 2 tasks for incident-api, 1 dedicated task for escalation-engine (stateful Redis subscriber, no horizontal scaling). APNs/FCM calls via Go HTTP client with connection pooling (max 100 concurrent).

frontend.app_structure: React Native Expo app with two navigation stacks: incident stack (incidents list, incident detail, acknowledge screen) and settings stack (profile, device token registration, notification preferences)
frontend.pages_and_flows: Incidents List: real-time updated list of active incidents with severity badge and ack timer countdown. Incident Detail: full incident description, runbook link (deep link to browser), escalation chain, status update controls, escalation history timeline. Acknowledge Screen: single-tap acknowledge with haptic feedback and Cognito re-auth for critical severity incidents.
frontend.state_management: Zustand: activeIncident store (current incident being worked), notificationStore (APNs/FCM device token, registration status). React Query: incidents list (30s stale time), incident detail (10s stale time for live status updates).

security.auth_design: Cognito user pool with MFA (TOTP enforced). Mobile app uses Cognito Hosted UI via expo-auth-session for OAuth PKCE flow. JWT stored in SecureStore (iOS Keychain / Android Keystore).
security.authorization_model: RBAC via Cognito groups: on_call_responder (ack incidents, update status, escalate manually), schedule_admin (manage on-call schedules and escalation policies), admin (manage alert sources, view all incidents, manage users).
security.secrets_management: APNs certificate and FCM server key stored in AWS Secrets Manager; fetched at Go service startup, never in environment variables. Webhook HMAC secrets per alert source stored in Secrets Manager.
security.security_risks: Push delivery via APNs/FCM means Apple/Google see notification metadata (device token, app bundle ID). Mitigated by excluding incident details from push payload; app fetches details via TLS-authenticated API on open.

data.entities: incidents (id UUID PK, source_alert_id VARCHAR, alert_source ENUM pagerduty/datadog/custom, title VARCHAR, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, status ENUM triggered/acknowledged/investigating/resolved, triggered_by_team UUID FK, assigned_to UUID FK users NULLABLE, runbook_url TEXT, triggered_at TIMESTAMP, acknowledged_at NULLABLE, resolved_at NULLABLE). on_call_schedules (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK, schedule_name VARCHAR, layers JSONB (primary_user_id, secondary_user_id, rotation_period_hours), effective_from DATE, effective_to DATE NULLABLE). escalation_policies (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK, policy_name VARCHAR, ack_timeout_seconds INT DEFAULT 300, escalation_chain JSONB (array of user_id with delay_seconds)). push_notifications (id UUID PK, incident_id UUID FK, user_id UUID FK, device_token VARCHAR, platform ENUM ios/android, status ENUM pending/delivered/failed, sent_at TIMESTAMP, delivered_at NULLABLE). acknowledgments (id UUID PK, incident_id UUID FK, user_id UUID FK, acknowledged_at TIMESTAMP, note TEXT NULLABLE). escalation_events (id UUID PK, incident_id UUID FK, escalation_policy_id UUID FK, escalated_from UUID FK users, escalated_to UUID FK users, reason ENUM ack_timeout/manual, escalated_at TIMESTAMP) -- append-only SOC 2 evidence.
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (db.r6g.large, 16GB RAM) for incident records, schedules, escalation policies, notifications log. Redis 7 ElastiCache Multi-AZ for escalation timer keys (key: escalation:incident:{incident_id}, TTL=ack_timeout_seconds), keyspace notification subscription by escalation-engine.
data.retention_and_deletion: incidents: retained 2 years, archived to S3 after 1 year via pg_partman quarterly partitions. escalation_events: retained 3 years (SOC 2 evidence). push_notifications: retained 90 days then purged. Device tokens: purged 30 days after deregistration via Cognito post-confirmation Lambda.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate us-east-1. Task definitions: incident-api (2 tasks, 1 vCPU 2GB RAM, ALB target group), escalation-engine (1 task, 0.5 vCPU 1GB RAM, no ALB). ALB with WAF on incident-api. RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.large. ElastiCache Multi-AZ cache.r6g.medium.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./... + golangci-lint on PR, Expo EAS Build (eas build --platform all --auto-submit) on mobile-release tag, ECS rolling deployment on main merge. APNs p8 certificate and FCM server key in AWS Secrets Manager; GitHub Actions secret SECRETS_MANAGER_ARN for deployment.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM sidecar on ECS Fargate tasks. Custom metrics: push_notification_delivery_latency_ms histogram, escalation_timer_precision_ms histogram, ack_rate_by_severity gauge, alert_ingestion_errors_total counter. PagerDuty dead-man's-switch: hourly heartbeat from escalation-engine to PagerDuty health check endpoint; PagerDuty alerts if heartbeat missed for 10 minutes.
devops.devops_risks: APNs certificate expiry silently stopping push delivery; mitigated by AWS Secrets Manager certificate expiry alert (30 days before expiry) and daily delivery receipt sampling test.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a mobile on-call incident management system where push notification reliability is the primary architectural constraint. Key decisions: Redis keyspace notifications for precise escalation timers rather than cron-based polling; direct APNs/FCM integration over Expo Push Service for delivery receipts and reliability; incident_id-only push payloads to protect incident details from traversing Apple/Google infrastructure; escalation-engine as a separate ECS task to isolate timer precision from API server load. The dead-man's-switch monitoring pattern is architecturally important and must be explicit in the plan.
target_output.title: On-Call Incident Management Mobile App Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A React Native mobile app on Expo SDK 51 paired with a Go Gin API on AWS ECS Fargate that replaces pager-based on-call alerting with push-notification-driven incident management for iOS and Android. Inbound alerts from PagerDuty, Datadog, and custom webhooks create incident records, trigger APNs/FCM push notifications to the on-call engineer's device, and start Redis TTL-based escalation timers. The separate escalation-engine ECS task subscribes to Redis keyspace notifications to fire escalations with 30-second precision on unacknowledged incidents. Engineers acknowledge, investigate, and resolve incidents from the mobile app with real-time status updates.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier mobile architecture: React Native Expo app authenticates via Cognito MFA PKCE flow, communicates over TLS 1.3 to a Go Gin API on ECS Fargate behind ALB with WAF. Alert ingestion webhooks (PagerDuty, Datadog, custom) enter on a separate /webhooks path protected by HMAC signature validation, not JWT. On alert receipt, the Go api-server creates an incident record in PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ, fetches the current on-call engineer from the schedules table, sends a push notification via direct APNs/FCM HTTP/2 API (incident_id and severity only in payload), and atomically sets a Redis TTL key for the escalation timer. A dedicated escalation-engine ECS task subscribes to Redis keyspace expiry notifications and fires escalations when timers expire without acknowledgment. PagerDuty dead-man's-switch heartbeat sent hourly from escalation-engine to detect system-level failures.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native TypeScript, Expo SDK 51, expo-auth-session (Cognito PKCE), expo-notifications (APNs/FCM token management), React Navigation v6, NativeWind, Zustand, React Query, SecureStore (Keychain/Keystore JWT). Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, golang-jwt, go-apns2 (APNs HTTP/2), firebase-admin-go (FCM), go-redis v9. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.large. Cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache Multi-AZ cache.r6g.medium with keyspace notifications. Auth: AWS Cognito user pool with MFA (TOTP enforced). Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager (APNs p8 cert, FCM server key, webhook HMAC secrets). Infra: ECS Fargate, ALB+WAF, RDS Multi-AZ, ElastiCache Multi-AZ, ACM, Route53. CI/CD: GitHub Actions + Expo EAS Build. Observability: Datadog APM, structured JSON logs.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Alert Ingestion: HMAC-validated webhook receivers for PagerDuty (POST /webhooks/pagerduty), Datadog (POST /webhooks/datadog), and custom (POST /webhooks/custom). Incident Lifecycle: triggered -> acknowledged -> investigating -> resolved status transitions with timestamp capture. Push Delivery: incident_id + severity payload to on-call engineer's registered device tokens via APNs HTTP/2 and FCM v1 API; delivery receipt tracking in push_notifications table. Escalation Engine: Redis TTL key per incident; escalation-engine fires on keyspace expiry event, sends push to escalation_chain next responder, logs escalation_event. On-Call Schedules: primary/secondary rotation management with schedule_admin role. Mobile App: incident list with severity badges, incident detail with runbook deep link, tap-to-acknowledge with Cognito re-auth for critical severity, status update controls, escalation history timeline.
target_output.system_components: ECS Fargate Task: incident-api (2 tasks, Go Gin, ALB target, Datadog APM sidecar). ECS Fargate Task: escalation-engine (1 task, Go Redis keyspace subscriber, no ALB, Datadog APM sidecar). ALB with WAF (SQLi/XSS rules, rate limit 200 req/min per IP on /webhooks). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.large (incident records, schedules, escalation policies, push notification log). Redis 7 ElastiCache Multi-AZ cache.r6g.medium (escalation timer TTL keys, keyspace notifications enabled). AWS Secrets Manager (APNs p8 cert, FCM key, webhook HMAC secrets). Cognito user pool with MFA. React Native Expo app (iOS App Store + Google Play).
target_output.workflows: Alert-to-Push: monitoring system POSTs to /webhooks/datadog with HMAC-SHA256 signature. Go validates signature against Secrets Manager HMAC secret, parses alert payload, creates incidents record (status=triggered), fetches current on_call_schedule primary user, fetches user's registered device_tokens, sends APNs HTTP/2 POST with payload {incident_id, severity} (no title/description/runbook in payload), records push_notifications entry, atomically sets Redis key escalation:incident:{id} with TTL=policy.ack_timeout_seconds. Mobile app receives silent push, fetches /incidents/:id via authenticated API, renders full incident detail in foreground notification. Escalation: Redis keyspace event fires on TTL expiry for escalation:incident:{id}. escalation-engine goroutine receives event, queries incident status -- if still triggered (unacknowledged), fetches escalation_policy for team, sends push to next responder in escalation_chain, creates escalation_events record, resets Redis timer for next escalation level. Acknowledgment: on-call engineer taps incident notification, app opens incident detail screen, taps Acknowledge (Cognito re-auth prompt for critical severity), PATCH /incidents/:id/acknowledge sent with JWT, Go handler updates incidents.status=acknowledged and acknowledged_at, deletes Redis escalation timer key, creates acknowledgments record.
