target_output.data_model: incidents (id UUID PK, source_alert_id VARCHAR(200), alert_source ENUM('pagerduty','datadog','custom'), title VARCHAR(500), description TEXT, severity ENUM('critical','high','medium','low'), status ENUM('triggered','acknowledged','investigating','resolved'), team_id UUID NOT NULL FK teams, assigned_to UUID NULLABLE FK users, runbook_url TEXT, triggered_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, acknowledged_at NULLABLE, resolved_at NULLABLE, created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now()). on_call_schedules (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK, schedule_name VARCHAR(100), layers JSONB -- [{user_id, role:'primary'|'secondary', rotation_period_hours}], effective_from DATE, effective_to DATE NULLABLE, created_by UUID FK users). escalation_policies (id UUID PK, team_id UUID FK, policy_name VARCHAR(100), ack_timeout_seconds INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 300, escalation_chain JSONB NOT NULL -- [{user_id, delay_seconds, channels:['push','sms','email']}], created_at TIMESTAMP). device_registrations (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK, platform ENUM('ios','android'), device_token VARCHAR(512) ENCRYPTED, expo_push_token VARCHAR(256), registered_at TIMESTAMP, last_seen TIMESTAMP, deregistered_at NULLABLE). push_notifications (id UUID PK, incident_id UUID FK, user_id UUID FK, platform ENUM, push_token_last4 VARCHAR(4), status ENUM('pending','delivered','failed'), error_code VARCHAR(50) NULLABLE, sent_at TIMESTAMP, delivered_at NULLABLE). acknowledgments (id UUID PK, incident_id UUID FK, user_id UUID FK, acknowledged_at TIMESTAMP, note TEXT NULLABLE). escalation_events (id UUID PK, incident_id UUID FK, policy_id UUID FK, escalated_from UUID FK users, escalated_to UUID FK users, reason ENUM('ack_timeout','manual'), escalated_at TIMESTAMP) -- append-only SOC 2 evidence.
target_output.api_design: POST /webhooks/pagerduty (HMAC-SHA256 signature validation, no JWT). POST /webhooks/datadog (HMAC validation). POST /webhooks/custom (HMAC validation). GET /api/v1/incidents?status=triggered&team_id=X (active incidents list). GET /api/v1/incidents/:id (full incident detail with escalation history). PATCH /api/v1/incidents/:id/acknowledge {note?} (responder role). PATCH /api/v1/incidents/:id/status {status, note} (responder role). POST /api/v1/incidents/:id/escalate {user_id, reason} (manual escalation). GET /api/v1/schedules/current-oncall?team_id=X (who is on-call now). GET /api/v1/schedules/:id (schedule detail). PUT /api/v1/schedules/:id (schedule_admin only). POST /api/v1/devices/register {platform, device_token, expo_push_token} (register mobile device). DELETE /api/v1/devices/:token (deregister). All /api/v1/* require Cognito JWT Bearer. Rate limit: 500 req/min per user on API, 200 req/min per IP on webhooks.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito MFA (TOTP) enforced at user pool level; cannot be disabled per user. Mobile JWT stored exclusively in SecureStore (iOS Keychain / Android Keystore), never AsyncStorage. JWT TTL 1 hour, silent Cognito refresh token flow. Webhook HMAC-SHA256 validation against Secrets Manager secret per alert source; invalid signatures logged and rejected with 401 (not 403, to avoid information leakage on valid sources). APNs p8 certificate and FCM v1 server key in AWS Secrets Manager; fetched at Go process startup; never in environment variables or ECS task definition. Push notification payloads: {incident_id, severity} only -- no title, description, runbook URL, or team name. Device tokens encrypted at rest in device_registrations table (AES-256 column encryption). escalation_events and acknowledgments tables: PostgreSQL role on_call_api has INSERT but no DELETE/UPDATE (append-only SOC 2 evidence). SOC 2 Type II: escalation audit trail covers ack_timeout and manual escalation events with full chain logged. Dead-man's-switch: hourly POST to PagerDuty check-in URL from escalation-engine; PagerDuty alerts on-call on silence for 10 minutes.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate us-east-1. incident-api: Task definition 1 vCPU 2GB, 2 tasks minimum, ALB target group, HPA on CPU 60% (max 4 tasks), liveness GET /healthz (200 with DB ping), readiness GET /readyz (200 after Redis connection established). escalation-engine: Task definition 0.5 vCPU 1GB, 1 task (Redis subscriber, not horizontally scalable without leader election), liveness check: POST to PagerDuty dead-man's-switch every 60s. RDS Multi-AZ: db.r6g.large with automated failover (failover time 60-120s; escalation-engine reconnects automatically). ElastiCache Multi-AZ: cache.r6g.medium, keyspace notifications enabled (notify-keyspace-events Ex). GitHub Actions CI: go test ./... + golangci-lint on PR; ECS rolling deploy on main merge via aws ecs update-service --force-new-deployment. Expo EAS Build triggered on mobile-release/* tags: eas build --platform all --auto-submit to App Store and Play Store. Secrets: APNs cert and FCM key in Secrets Manager with CloudWatch alarm on expiry 30 days before. Migrations: golang-migrate embedded in binary, runs on startup.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM sidecar on both ECS tasks (Fargate via ddagent container). Custom Datadog metrics: push_notification_delivery_latency_ms histogram (alert: p99 > 8000ms -- warn threshold before 10s SLA breach), escalation_timer_precision_ms histogram (difference between Redis TTL expiry and escalation send time; alert: p99 > 25000ms), push_delivery_failure_rate gauge (alert: > 2% over 5 minutes), ack_timeout_escalations_per_hour gauge, alert_ingestion_errors_total counter (alert: rate > 0 for 1 minute). ECS Task health metrics: CPU/memory per task, ALB 5xx rate (alert: > 0.5%). Dead-man's-switch: Datadog monitor on escalation-engine heartbeat silence for 10 minutes triggers PagerDuty critical alert. Structured JSON logs from both ECS tasks forwarded to CloudWatch Logs; push delivery failure events include incident_id, user_id_hash, platform, error_code.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate: ~$120/month (2x incident-api tasks + 1x escalation-engine). RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.large: ~$200/month. ElastiCache Multi-AZ cache.r6g.medium: ~$100/month. Secrets Manager + CloudWatch + ALB: ~$80/month. EAS Build credits (2 builds/month): ~$30/month. Total ~$530/month. Scaling: incident-api scales on CPU to 4 tasks during high-alert-volume incidents; escalation-engine single task is sufficient for up to 10K incidents/day given Redis keyspace notification throughput.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Cognito MFA setup, PagerDuty and Datadog webhook ingest, incident create/acknowledge/resolve lifecycle, APNs and FCM direct push delivery, single-level escalation with 5-minute timeout, on-call schedule (primary + secondary), React Native app (incidents list, incident detail, acknowledge flow), dead-man's-switch heartbeat. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Multi-level escalation trees (N-level chains), custom webhook source, manual escalation from mobile app, SMS and email fallback channels via AWS SNS, schedule rotation management UI in app, push delivery receipt dashboard. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Incident timeline collaboration (status updates with notes), post-incident report export, Slack bot integration for incident status, mobile on-call schedule editor.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 with golangci-lint (errcheck, staticcheck). All database queries via pgx v5 named parameters; no string-interpolated SQL. Redis timer key operations must be atomic (SET with NX flag + EXPIRE as MULTI/EXEC transaction). Push notification send must log result synchronously to push_notifications table before returning HTTP response to webhook caller. Expo app: all API calls via React Query with 3-retry backoff; never store JWT in AsyncStorage. escalation_events and acknowledgments tables: only INSERT operations allowed in Go service layer (no UPDATE/DELETE methods exposed). EAS Build configured with runtime version policy to enforce OTA update compatibility.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: escalation-engine ECS task restart during active incident escalations causes timer loss (Redis TTL keys still exist but subscriber is down). Mitigation: escalation-engine on startup scans Redis for all escalation:incident:* keys with TTL < 60s and immediately fires escalations to catch up. Risk: APNs/FCM token staleness causing silent delivery failures. Mitigation: delivery receipt tracking in push_notifications; tokens with 3 consecutive failures marked inactive and engineer notified via email fallback. Risk: Redis ElastiCache failover (60-120s) during which keyspace notification subscription is lost. Mitigation: escalation-engine reconnects with Redis Sentinel client; on reconnection scans for expired-but-unprocessed keys. Tradeoff: direct APNs/FCM over Expo Push Service -- direct gives delivery receipts and no third-party intermediary but requires managing APNs certificate rotation; Expo Push Service simpler but receipts less reliable.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Direct APNs HTTP/2 and FCM v1 API chosen over Expo Push Service for delivery receipt accuracy critical to the SLA monitoring. Redis keyspace notifications chosen over database polling for escalation timers to achieve 30-second precision without polling overhead. incident_id-only push payload decided to prevent incident details transiting Apple/Google APNs/FCM infrastructure.

metadata.notes: On-call incident management mobile app replacing pager infrastructure; push notification reliability as primary architectural constraint with Redis TTL escalation timers.

profile.domain: Platform Engineering Tooling

project_goal.value: CLI tool for platform engineers to query live Kubernetes namespace quota utilization, set monthly budget limits, and generate allocation reports from the terminal by communicating with the Kubernetes metrics API and an internal cost allocation service
project_goal.rationale: Platform team wastes 30 minutes per request on manual kubectl quota checks and spreadsheet-based cost reports; CLI automates these workflows from the terminal

target_users.value: Platform engineers managing GKE cluster quotas; FinOps team generating monthly team cost allocation reports
target_users.rationale: Two distinct user personas with different primary commands: platform engineers use quota status/set, FinOps uses report generation

project_class.rationale: Standalone binary distributed via internal package registry; no browser UI; no hosted service

capabilities.rationale: Go CLI binary, integration with Kubernetes metrics API and cost allocation API, data formatting for report output

complexity_level.rationale: OIDC device flow, multiple API integrations, and formatted report generation across output modes add meaningful complexity above a simple API wrapper

risk_level.rationale: CLI modifies Kubernetes resource quotas which can affect workload scheduling if set incorrectly; OIDC device flow mitigates credential exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Quota utilization and cost allocation data are internal operational metrics; no personal or financial customer data

external_exposure.rationale: Kubernetes metrics API and cost allocation service are accessible only within the GCP VPC; CLI operates only from corporate-network or VPN-connected machines

access_model.value: Okta OIDC device flow for interactive authentication; short-lived access tokens (1 hour) cached in system keychain; namespace-scoped RBAC on Kubernetes: platform engineers get read+write quota permissions, FinOps team gets read-only
access_model.rationale: Device flow suitable for terminal-based auth without browser redirect; keychain caching avoids re-auth on every invocation

feature_scope.value: quota status command showing CPU, memory, and storage utilization vs limits per namespace; budget set command posting monthly budget limit to cost allocation API; report command generating per-team allocation report in table or CSV format; config subcommand managing kubeconfig context and cost API endpoint; completion subcommand generating shell completions for bash and zsh
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set required for both platform engineer and FinOps workflows

mvp_scope.value: quota status command, budget set command, Okta device flow auth, table output
mvp_scope.rationale: Core quota management workflow is must-have; report generation and CSV output in phase 2

security_baseline.value: Okta OIDC device flow; access tokens stored in OS keychain via system credential store; no tokens in shell history, config files, or env vars; kubeconfig context read from standard KUBECONFIG env path
security_baseline.rationale: Prevents credential exposure in terminal history and dotfiles

frontend_stack.value: none - CLI binary only, terminal output via tview or tablewriter
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool with no browser or UI component; terminal table rendering via tablewriter library

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 standalone binary with Cobra command framework
backend_stack.rationale: Go Cobra is the standard for production-quality Go CLIs; single binary distribution; fast startup

data_platform.value: No database; CLI is stateless; auth tokens cached in OS keychain; cost report output written to stdout or file
data_platform.rationale: CLI has no persistent data store; all data fetched from upstream APIs per invocation

hosting_target.value: Distributed as compiled Go binary via internal package registry (Artifactory) for Darwin arm64, Darwin amd64, and Linux amd64
hosting_target.rationale: Artifactory provides access control and version management for internal binary distribution

privacy_retention_policy.value: CLI generates no persistent data beyond OS keychain token cache; report output is user-controlled; no telemetry collected
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: CLI tool policy: user owns their output; no server-side data retention

future_scope.value: watch mode for continuous quota utilization monitoring with threshold alerts; Slack integration for automated weekly allocation reports; OPA-based quota policy validation before budget set operations
future_scope.rationale: Automation enhancements for phase 2

constraints.value: Single binary with no external runtime dependencies; startup time under 200ms; must work behind GCP VPC with no direct public internet access from corporate network
constraints.rationale: Platform engineer requirement: no dependency installation; fast iteration in terminal loops

observability_baseline.value: --debug flag enables structured JSON log output to stderr; API call durations logged at debug level; no telemetry or metrics collection from CLI
observability_baseline.rationale: Debugging support without telemetry overhead for an internal tool

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning; GitHub releases with platform-specific binary artifacts; Artifactory sync on tag push
execution_preference.rationale: Standard CLI distribution practice

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II: Okta OIDC access logging, no credentials in logs or config files, Kubernetes RBAC enforced server-side, budget set operations logged to cost allocation API audit trail
compliance_context.rationale: Internal tooling with medium risk requires SOC 2-aligned access patterns

project.goal: CLI tool for Kubernetes quota management and cost allocation reporting

backend.integrations: Kubernetes metrics API, internal cost allocation API (REST)

security.auth: Okta OIDC device flow

data.persistence: none - stateless CLI; output to stdout or file

constraints.dependencies: single binary, no external runtime deps

confirmed_decisions.output_format: tablewriter for table, encoding/csv for CSV

reasoner_reviews.product: The three-command structure (quota status, budget set, report) cleanly separates the two user personas without overlap. The --output flag accepting table|csv|json covers both human and programmatic consumption from CI scripts. Config subcommand managing multiple kubeconfig contexts is essential for platform engineers who work across dev, staging, and production clusters from a single machine.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go Cobra with subcommand tree is the right framework; the command hierarchy should be: kubequota quota [status|set|watch], kubequota budget [set|get|list], kubequota report [generate], kubequota auth [login|logout|status], kubequota config [set-context|list-contexts]. The Kubernetes client-go library provides native metrics API access without kubectl dependency. Cost allocation API calls should use a typed HTTP client with context-propagated timeouts to avoid hanging invocations.
reasoner_reviews.security: The Okta device flow token must be stored in the OS keychain using go-keyring, not in a dotfile under $HOME/.kubequota/; dotfile storage is a common credential leak vector for internal CLIs. Token refresh must be attempted silently before expiry; if refresh fails, the CLI should prompt for re-authentication rather than surfacing a cryptic 401 error.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Go binary startup time under 200ms is achievable with Cobra and no heavy init logic; avoid loading full kubeconfig into memory until the command actually needs it. Static binary compilation with CGO_ENABLED=0 ensures no dynamic library dependencies on Linux targets.

backend.service_design: Cobra command tree: root cmd kubequota with persistent flags --context (kubeconfig context), --output (table|csv|json), --debug. Subcommands: quota status [namespace] [--all-namespaces], quota set [namespace] --cpu --memory, budget set [namespace] --monthly-limit, budget get [namespace], report generate [--team] [--month] [--format csv|table], auth login, auth logout, auth status, config set-context, config list-contexts, completion [bash|zsh].
backend.api_patterns: Kubernetes client-go for ResourceQuota and LimitRange reads and writes using the authenticated kubeconfig context. Cost allocation API HTTP client with Go net/http, context deadline 30 seconds per call, typed request/response structs, debug-level logging of request duration. Okta OIDC device flow using golang.org/x/oauth2 device auth flow.
backend.failure_handling: API errors surfaced as human-readable CLI errors with actionable suggestions (e.g., '403: namespace billing-team not in your allowed namespaces - contact platform team'). Timeout after 30 seconds with context cancellation. Token expiry triggers silent refresh; if refresh fails, prints 'Session expired - run kubequota auth login' and exits with code 1.
backend.scaling_notes: CLI is invoked per command; no daemon or long-running process. Parallel API calls for multi-namespace quota status using goroutines with WaitGroup; results collected and sorted before table render.

security.auth_design: Okta device flow: kubequota auth login opens device authorization URL, polls token endpoint, stores access token and refresh token in OS keychain under service 'kubequota' key. Token loaded from keychain before each API call; exp claim checked; refresh attempted if within 5 minutes of expiry using stored refresh token.
security.secrets_management: go-keyring library for OS keychain access (Keychain on macOS, libsecret on Linux). No tokens written to config files, environment variables, or command history. --debug output redacts Authorization header values.
security.security_risks: Risk: go-keyring dependency on libsecret on Linux may not be available on minimal build environments; mitigated by detecting keychain unavailability and falling back to encrypted file store with warning message.

data.entities: No persistent entities. In-memory structs: NamespaceQuota {namespace string, cpu_request, cpu_limit, cpu_used, memory_request, memory_limit, memory_used, storage_request, storage_limit QuantityValues}. BudgetRecord {namespace, monthly_limit_usd, current_spend_usd, billing_cycle_start Date, source string}. AllocationReport {team string, namespaces []string, total_cpu_hours float64, total_memory_gib_hours float64, cost_usd float64}. ReportRow {team, namespace, cpu_efficiency_pct, memory_efficiency_pct, cost_usd, quota_headroom_pct}.
data.storage_design: Stateless CLI; no database. Auth tokens in OS keychain. Report output to stdout (default) or --output-file path. JSON output uses encoding/json; CSV output uses encoding/csv; table output uses github.com/olekukon/go-simpletable.
data.data_risks: Report output written to --output-file is user-controlled; no server-side data store risk.

devops.deployment_topology: No hosted deployment. GitHub Actions builds Darwin arm64, Darwin amd64, Linux amd64 binaries using goreleaser. Artifacts published to GitHub Releases and synced to Artifactory via GoReleaser Artifactory plugin.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Go unit tests for command argument parsing, API client error handling, token refresh logic; integration tests against mock Kubernetes API and mock cost allocation server using httptest. go vet, staticcheck linter. goreleaser build on tag v* for all three platforms. Artifactory publish via GoReleaser with ARTIFACTORY_TOKEN secret.
devops.observability_stack: No metrics. --debug flag sets zerolog level to DEBUG, prints structured JSON to stderr: {level:'debug', event:'api_call', url:'...', duration_ms:42, status:200}. No telemetry. Errors exit with meaningful codes: 0 success, 1 auth error, 2 API error, 3 config error.

ISS-001.title: quota set command modifies Kubernetes ResourceQuota without dry-run mode risking accidental workload disruption
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan implemented quota set as a direct ResourceQuota PATCH without a --dry-run flag. Setting a CPU limit below current pod requests would trigger evictions immediately with no preview step.

history.note: Identified in architect review: no dry-run or preview mode for destructive quota set command

ISS-002.title: report generate queries all namespaces sequentially causing 60+ second report generation for large clusters
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 report generation fetched quota and cost data namespace by namespace in a for loop. For clusters with 50+ namespaces, this produces wall clock times exceeding 60 seconds due to serial API call latency.

history.note: Identified in architect review: no concurrency in multi-namespace report generation

focus_issues.title: quota set command modifies Kubernetes ResourceQuota without dry-run mode risking accidental workload disruption
focus_issues.detail: No --dry-run or preview step before applying quota changes that could trigger pod evictions.

focus_issues.title: report generate queries all namespaces sequentially causing 60+ second generation time
focus_issues.detail: Serial namespace API calls produce 60+ second wall clock times for clusters with 50+ namespaces.

latest_recommendations: Add --dry-run flag to quota set command that displays a diff of current vs proposed ResourceQuota and highlights namespaces where the new limit is below current pod requests, requiring --confirm to apply
Parallelize report generate namespace data fetching using a worker pool of 10 goroutines with WaitGroup to reduce wall clock time from 60s to under 10s for 50 namespaces

Okta device flow with go-keyring is the correct credential storage approach
Parallel quota status with goroutines is correctly described

Report generation serial loop produces unusable performance at scale

ISS-002: parallel namespace fetching required for report generate

Implement worker pool of 10 goroutines in report generate to fetch quota and cost data concurrently across namespaces

issue_updates.title: No dry-run for quota set
issue_updates.detail: workflows and system_components sections describe quota set as direct PATCH with no preview

previous_plan.title: Kubernetes Quota Management CLI Architecture - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go Cobra CLI for Kubernetes namespace quota management and cost allocation reporting with Okta device flow auth and OS keychain token storage.
previous_plan.technology_stack: Go 1.22, Cobra, go-keyring, kubernetes client-go, golang.org/x/oauth2 device flow, tablewriter, goreleaser.
previous_plan.workflows: quota status: fetches ResourceQuota objects for specified namespace via client-go, renders table. quota set: PATCHes ResourceQuota with new limits directly. report generate: iterates namespaces sequentially fetching quota and cost data.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Okta device flow token stored in go-keyring OS keychain. No credentials in config files or shell history. Kubernetes RBAC enforced server-side.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the Kubernetes quota CLI to fix two issues from round 1. ISS-001 requires adding a --dry-run flag to quota set that fetches the current ResourceQuota, diffs proposed changes, identifies namespaces where new limits are below current pod requests (which would trigger evictions), displays a preview table, and only applies after --confirm is passed. ISS-002 requires replacing the sequential namespace loop in report generate with a worker pool of 10 goroutines using errgroup for concurrent API calls, reducing generation time from 60+ seconds to under 10 seconds for 50 namespaces.

fix_report.action_taken: Added --dry-run flag to quota set command. When --dry-run is set, the command fetches the current ResourceQuota, computes a diff of CPU and memory limits, queries the pods in the namespace to check if any pod resource requests exceed the proposed new limits, and prints a preview table showing current vs proposed values with a warning row for any limit that would violate existing pod requests. Command exits without applying unless --confirm is also passed.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Platform engineers can preview quota set changes and identify eviction risks before applying; ISS-001 high severity safety gap resolved

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced sequential namespace loop in report generate with a concurrent worker pool using golang.org/x/sync/errgroup with a pool of 10 goroutines. Each worker fetches quota and cost data for one namespace concurrently; results collected into a thread-safe slice protected by sync.Mutex; sorted by team name before table render. Error handling: if any namespace fetch fails, error is collected but generation continues for remaining namespaces with a warning row.
fix_report.expected_outcome: report generate wall clock time reduced from 60+ seconds to under 10 seconds for clusters with 50 namespaces; partial failures surface warnings without aborting the full report

target_output.title: Kubernetes Quota Management CLI Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Go Cobra CLI distributed as a static binary for platform engineers to manage Kubernetes namespace resource quotas and generate team cost allocation reports. Authenticates via Okta OIDC device flow with tokens in OS keychain. The quota set command includes a --dry-run preview mode that identifies eviction risks before applying limit changes. Report generation uses a 10-goroutine worker pool to fetch quota and cost data concurrently, completing 50-namespace reports in under 10 seconds. Output renders as terminal tables, CSV, or JSON for FinOps spreadsheet import.
target_output.architecture_overview: Stateless Go binary built with Cobra organizing commands into four groups: quota (status, set), budget (set, get, list), report (generate), and auth (login, logout, status). The quota set command has a two-phase execution path: in dry-run mode it fetches the current ResourceQuota via kubernetes client-go, diffs proposed limits against current values, queries running pod specs to detect eviction risks, and renders a preview table; with --confirm it executes the PATCH. Report generate spawns an errgroup worker pool of 10 goroutines that concurrently fetch ResourceQuota objects and cost allocation API records per namespace, collecting results into a sorted AllocationReport struct rendered as a table or CSV. Okta OIDC device flow tokens stored in OS keychain via go-keyring; token exp checked before each API call with silent refresh if within 5 minutes of expiry.
target_output.technology_stack: Go 1.22 with Cobra command framework, kubernetes client-go for ResourceQuota and pod spec reads, golang.org/x/oauth2 device auth flow, go-keyring for OS keychain, golang.org/x/sync/errgroup for worker pool, github.com/olekukon/go-simpletable for terminal tables, encoding/csv and encoding/json for output formats, zerolog for --debug structured output. Build: goreleaser for Darwin arm64/amd64 and Linux amd64 static binaries. Distribution: GitHub Releases and Artifactory.
target_output.functional_feature_map: quota status [namespace] [--all-namespaces]: fetches ResourceQuota objects via client-go, renders CPU/memory/storage utilization table per namespace with % used columns. quota set [namespace] --cpu={limit} --memory={limit} [--dry-run] [--confirm]: two-phase dry-run/apply with eviction risk detection. budget set [namespace] --monthly-limit={usd}: POSTs budget config to cost allocation API. budget get/list: fetches current budget limits and spend from cost allocation API. report generate [--team] [--month] [--format table|csv]: concurrent namespace data fetch via 10-goroutine pool, renders AllocationReport with per-team CPU efficiency, memory efficiency, cost, and quota headroom. auth login: Okta device flow, stores tokens in keychain. auth status: checks token validity and remaining TTL. completion bash|zsh: emits shell completion scripts.
target_output.system_components: Single Go binary kubequota. Cobra command tree with root persistent flags: --context (kubeconfig context override), --output (table|csv|json), --debug. kubernetes client-go ResourceQuota, LimitRange, and Pod lister for quota status and eviction check. golang.org/x/oauth2 device flow client for Okta OIDC. go-keyring for OS keychain (Keychain on macOS, libsecret/Secret Service on Linux). Cost allocation API HTTP client with 30-second context deadline. golang.org/x/sync/errgroup worker pool for parallel namespace report generation. goreleaser build configuration for three platform targets with CGO_ENABLED=0 static linking.
target_output.workflows: quota set with Dry-Run and Eviction Check: platform engineer runs kubequota quota set billing-team --cpu=4 --memory=8Gi --dry-run; CLI fetches current ResourceQuota for billing-team namespace showing current limits (cpu:8, memory:16Gi); fetches running pod specs and sums resource requests (total pod cpu requests: 3.5 cores, memory: 7Gi); computes diff table: cpu 8->4 (WARNING: 2 pods requesting 1.5 cores each would be at risk if scheduler reassigns), memory 16Gi->8Gi (OK: total requests 7Gi below proposed limit); prints preview with eviction risk warnings; exits without applying; engineer re-runs with --confirm to apply PATCH. Concurrent Report Generation: FinOps engineer runs kubequota report generate --month=2026-02 --format csv; CLI initializes errgroup with 10 worker goroutines; dispatches 47 namespace fetch tasks; each worker calls Kubernetes ResourceQuota API and cost allocation API concurrently; results collected into []ReportRow protected by sync.Mutex; partial failures logged as warning rows; results sorted by team name; CSV written to stdout in under 10 seconds; engineer pipes output to monthly-report.csv. Silent Token Refresh: platform engineer runs quota status command 55 minutes after last auth; CLI loads token from go-keyring, checks exp claim, finds 5 minutes until expiry, calls Okta token endpoint with refresh token, stores new access token in keychain, proceeds with Kubernetes API call transparently.
target_output.data_model: In-memory only. NamespaceQuota struct: {Namespace string, CPURequestUsed, CPULimitQuota, CPULimitProposed resource.Quantity, MemoryRequestUsed, MemoryLimitQuota, MemoryLimitProposed resource.Quantity, StorageRequestUsed, StorageLimitQuota resource.Quantity, EvictionRiskPods []string}. BudgetRecord struct: {Namespace, Team string, MonthlyLimitUSD, CurrentSpendUSD float64, BillingCycleStart time.Time}. AllocationReport struct: {GeneratedAt time.Time, Month string, Rows []ReportRow}. ReportRow struct: {Team, Namespace string, CPUEfficiencyPct, MemoryEfficiencyPct, CostUSD, QuotaHeadroomPct float64, FetchError error}. TokenCache: go-keyring OS keychain entry under service='kubequota', key='auth-token', value=JSON{access_token, refresh_token, expires_at}.
target_output.api_design: CLI commands serve as the API surface. No HTTP server. Kubernetes API: client-go ResourceQuota.Get(namespace), ResourceQuota.Patch(namespace, patch), PodList.List(namespace) - read-only for status/dry-run; PATCH only for quota set after --confirm. Cost allocation REST API: GET /api/v1/namespaces/{ns}/budget (BudgetRecord), POST /api/v1/namespaces/{ns}/budget (set limit), GET /api/v1/report?month={YYYY-MM}&team={team} (AllocationReport), all calls with Okta Bearer token and 30-second context deadline. Okta: device authorization endpoint for auth login; token endpoint for refresh.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC device flow: auth login opens device URL printed to terminal, polls token endpoint, stores access_token and refresh_token in OS keychain via go-keyring (macOS Keychain, Linux Secret Service). No credentials in $HOME/.kubequota/ config files or shell history. --debug output uses zerolog with Authorization header value redacted as '[REDACTED]'. quota set PATCH requires --confirm flag; dry-run is the default execution path, not an opt-in, ensuring accidental invocations without --confirm are harmless. Kubernetes RBAC enforced server-side: platform engineers have ResourceQuota/patch on their namespaces only; FinOps has read-only. SOC 2: cost allocation API logs all budget set calls with Okta user ID; Kubernetes audit log captures ResourceQuota PATCHes with user identity; no local credential files.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: No hosted deployment. GitHub Actions: go test (unit and httptest-based mock integration tests), go vet, staticcheck, goreleaser build for three platforms with CGO_ENABLED=0. On git tag v*: goreleaser release creates GitHub Release with platform binaries and publishes to Artifactory via GoReleaser plugin using ARTIFACTORY_TOKEN. Installation: engineers run install script or brew tap for macOS; curl binary download for Linux CI environments. Semantic versioning: patch for bug fixes, minor for new subcommands, major for command interface changes breaking shell scripts.
target_output.observability: No metrics collection. --debug flag sets zerolog to DEBUG level, writes structured JSON to stderr: {level:'debug', event:'k8s_api_call', namespace:'billing-team', resource:'ResourceQuota', duration_ms:45}, {level:'debug', event:'cost_api_call', namespace:'billing-team', duration_ms:120}, {level:'debug', event:'token_refresh', success:true}. Authorization header values redacted in all debug output. Exit codes: 0 success, 1 auth error (prompt to re-login), 2 Kubernetes API error (print namespace and resource), 3 cost allocation API error, 4 config error (invalid kubeconfig context).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost. GitHub Actions CI minutes for PR and release builds are negligible. Artifactory storage for binary versions is megabytes per release. CLI scales with cluster size via worker pool concurrency; 50-namespace report completes in under 10 seconds at 10 concurrent workers; 200-namespace clusters complete in under 30 seconds. No infrastructure costs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Core Commands MVP (4 weeks): quota status with parallel namespace goroutines, quota set with --dry-run eviction risk check and --confirm apply, Okta device flow auth with go-keyring, budget set and get commands, table output, GitHub Actions CI, goreleaser Artifactory publish. Phase 2 - Reports and Output Formats (2 weeks): report generate with 10-goroutine worker pool, CSV and JSON output formats, budget list command, shell completions for bash and zsh. Phase 3 - Automation Enhancements (3 weeks): watch mode for continuous quota monitoring with configurable CPU/memory threshold alerts, Slack webhook integration for weekly automated reports, OPA policy validation before quota set apply.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 with go vet and staticcheck in CI. All API calls use context.WithTimeout(ctx, 30*time.Second) to prevent hanging invocations. quota set must default to dry-run path; --confirm flag required to execute PATCH; this must be tested in unit tests to prevent regression. Worker pool goroutines must collect errors into ReportRow.FetchError, not abort on first error, to ensure partial reports are useful. go-keyring dependency must be tested for libsecret availability on Linux CI runners; test with mock keyring in unit tests. No credentials in zerolog output; redact Authorization values with constant '[REDACTED]'. goreleaser must set CGO_ENABLED=0 and GOOS/GOARCH cross-compilation for all three platform targets.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: go-keyring requires libsecret on Linux systems without a GUI session (headless CI); mitigated by detecting keychain unavailability and falling back to AES-encrypted file store in $HOME/.kubequota/tokens.enc with a displayed warning. Risk: Kubernetes ResourceQuota PATCH with --confirm races with concurrent operator quota changes between dry-run and apply; mitigated by adding resourceVersion to the PATCH body for optimistic concurrency rejection. Tradeoff: 10-goroutine worker pool for reports vs dynamic pool sizing; fixed 10 goroutines avoids rate-limiting the Kubernetes API server under default throttling settings; configurable via --workers flag for larger clusters. Risk: cost allocation API unavailability causing report generation to produce partial results; mitigated by per-namespace error rows in the report output with actionable error descriptions rather than aborting the full report.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Added --dry-run with eviction risk detection as the default quota set execution path, requiring --confirm to apply (resolves ISS-001). Replaced sequential namespace loop with errgroup 10-goroutine worker pool in report generate, reducing 50-namespace generation time from 60s to under 10s (resolves ISS-002). Confirmed go-keyring for OS keychain over dotfile credential storage to prevent credential leak in shell history. Selected golang.org/x/sync/errgroup over manual WaitGroup for worker pool for cleaner error propagation.

metadata.notes: Kubernetes quota CLI round 2: adds --dry-run eviction risk check to quota set and concurrent worker pool to report generate.

profile.domain: ML-Powered Code Quality API

project_goal.value: ML-powered code quality scoring API that analyzes pull requests for complexity metrics, test coverage regressions, and anti-patterns using trained models, returning structured quality scores and improvement suggestions integrated into CI/CD pipelines via GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket webhooks
project_goal.rationale: Automate code quality enforcement at PR review time to reduce review cycle overhead and catch regressions before merge

target_users.value: Engineering teams consuming quality scores in CI/CD pipelines; repository admins configuring per-repo quality thresholds; platform engineers operating the scoring service
target_users.rationale: Multi-persona: automated pipeline consumers and human admins

project_class.rationale: Pure API service consumed by CI/CD webhooks; no browser UI beyond admin config endpoints

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, public_api, ai_llm, integrations
capabilities.rationale: FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL for analysis records, JWT auth, webhook integrations for Git providers, ML model inference for scoring

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-model inference pipeline, model versioning, coverage delta computation, and multi-provider webhook normalization add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Handles internal source code diffs; no PII or financial data; model integrity is a reliability concern

data_sensitivity.rationale: Analyzed diffs are internal engineering code; not customer or personal data

external_exposure.rationale: JWT authentication required for all management endpoints; webhook endpoints use provider HMAC signatures

access_model.value: Custom JWT for management API; per-provider HMAC signature validation for webhook endpoints; API tokens scoped per repository for CI pipeline integration
access_model.rationale: Webhook endpoints cannot use JWT (called by Git providers); management API needs scoped repo tokens

feature_scope.value: Webhook receivers for GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket PR events, diff fetching from provider API, complexity and anti-pattern ML model scoring, test coverage delta computation, threshold enforcement with CI check status setting, structured quality report as PR check, per-repo threshold configuration, model version management, model retraining pipeline with accuracy validation
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for PR-time code quality enforcement

mvp_scope.value: GitHub webhook integration, diff complexity scoring model, test coverage delta computation, GitHub PR check status posting, per-repo threshold configuration via management API
mvp_scope.rationale: Single provider validates the core scoring pipeline before multi-provider integration

security_baseline.value: HMAC-SHA256 signature validation on all webhook endpoints, JWT Bearer for management API, API tokens encrypted at rest in AWS Secrets Manager, TLS 1.3 in transit, diffs never persisted to database (processed in memory only)
security_baseline.rationale: Source code diffs are sensitive; must not be logged or stored beyond inference session

frontend_stack.rationale: No browser UI; quality reports delivered as structured JSON to PR checks and CI pipeline callers

backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem for ML model loading (scikit-learn, HuggingFace transformers) and async FastAPI for webhook throughput

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on GCP Cloud SQL for analysis records and model metadata; Redis 7 on GKE for inference result cache
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for relational quality records; Redis cache reduces duplicate analysis for identical diff SHAs

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run with auto-scaling
hosting_target.rationale: Cloud Run serverless scaling suits burst webhook traffic from CI/CD pipelines without idle cost

privacy_retention_policy.value: pull_request_analyses retained 18 months; model performance metrics retained indefinitely; diffs never persisted; analysis result cache TTL 1 hour
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Analysis history needed for trend reporting; diff data must not be retained

future_scope.value: Bitbucket integration, dependency vulnerability scoring module, custom rule DSL for organization-specific anti-patterns, dashboard UI for quality trends
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap

constraints.value: Scoring API must return quality report within 30 seconds of webhook receipt; diffs must never be written to disk or database; model inference must run on CPU-only Cloud Run instances (no GPU)
constraints.rationale: CI pipeline timeout is 30s; security and cost constraints rule out persistent diff storage and GPU instances

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for Cloud Run metrics; Sentry for FastAPI exception tracking; model scoring latency histograms per model version
observability_baseline.rationale: Open-source observability stack; Sentry for Python exception visibility

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints with model accuracy benchmark gate before promoting any new model version to production scoring
execution_preference.rationale: Model promotion must be gated on accuracy benchmarks to prevent quality score regressions

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II in scope; audit log of all repository threshold configuration changes required; model version promotions logged with accuracy benchmark results
compliance_context.rationale: SOC 2 change management controls apply to threshold and model configuration changes

llm_integration.value: HuggingFace CodeBERT fine-tuned for anti-pattern classification; scikit-learn RandomForest for cyclomatic complexity scoring; models loaded from GCP Cloud Storage at service startup and cached in memory; no external LLM API calls
llm_integration.rationale: On-premises models avoid external API latency, cost, and data egress of sending diffs to OpenAI/Anthropic; CPU inference feasible at this diff size

project.goal: ML-powered PR code quality scoring integrated into CI/CD pipelines

security.auth: Custom JWT for management API; HMAC-SHA256 for webhooks
security.transport: TLS 1.3; diffs processed in memory only, never persisted

data.cache: Redis 7 on GKE for inference result cache (1-hour TTL by diff SHA)

constraints.scoring_latency: Quality report within 30s of webhook receipt
constraints.diff_storage: Diffs never written to disk or database

reasoner_reviews.product: The 30-second end-to-end SLA from webhook receipt to PR check posted is tight but achievable with CPU inference on diff-sized code windows. The model retraining pipeline with accuracy validation gate is essential for long-term score reliability and should be treated as a core architecture component, not an afterthought.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Loading HuggingFace CodeBERT into Cloud Run memory at cold start is the key latency risk. Cloud Run minimum-instances=2 prevents cold starts for active repositories. Models should be loaded from GCS at startup and cached in a module-level singleton, not reloaded per request. The Redis cache keyed by diff SHA prevents redundant inference on force-pushed commits with unchanged diffs.
reasoner_reviews.security: Diffs must never touch disk -- Python in-memory string processing only. The constraint is architecturally sound and must be explicit in the security section with specific implementation guidance (no tempfile, no logging of diff content, no sentry breadcrumb with diff content). HMAC validation must happen before any diff fetching from provider API to prevent webhook abuse triggering expensive model inference.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: CPU-only CodeBERT inference on a full PR diff typically runs in 3-8 seconds on Cloud Run 2 vCPU instances. The 30s SLA has adequate headroom. Model loading at cold start adds 15-20s; minimum-instances=2 is the mitigation.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan addressed model versioning but did not specify the model_versions table schema or the accuracy validation benchmark process for promotion. Round 3 must include the model retraining pipeline architecture and model promotion criteria in enough detail to be implementation-ready.

backend.service_design: Three FastAPI routers: webhooks (GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket HMAC-validated ingest), scoring (internal inference orchestration -- diff fetch, model run, coverage delta, threshold check, PR check post), management (repo config, model version admin, API token CRUD). Models loaded at module import time as singletons: CodeBERTAntiPatternModel, ComplexityRFModel.
backend.api_patterns: POST /webhooks/github, POST /webhooks/gitlab -- HMAC validated, no JWT. GET /api/v1/analyses/:pr_id -- quality report retrieval. GET /api/v1/analyses?repo=X&from=2026-01-01 -- analysis history. POST /api/v1/repos/:repo_id/config -- set thresholds. GET /api/v1/models/versions -- list model versions with accuracy metrics. POST /api/v1/models/:version/promote -- promote to production (admin only). OpenAPI 3.0 generated via FastAPI /docs.
backend.background_jobs: Model retraining Cloud Run Job (weekly): evaluate current production model against ground_truth_reviews dataset, compute precision/recall on anti_pattern_findings, compare to accuracy_baseline in model_versions table, promote if benchmark passes. Stale analysis purge job (monthly): delete pull_request_analyses older than 18 months.
backend.failure_handling: Webhook HMAC validation failure: 401, no diff fetch triggered (prevents inference cost abuse). Provider API rate limit during diff fetch: 429 handling with exponential backoff (max 2 retries, total 20s before returning partial_analysis score). Model inference timeout: 25s hard timeout per request (5s buffer before 30s SLA); return degraded_scoring=true response if timeout.

security.auth_design: Custom JWT (PyJWT, HS256) for management API; 1-hour TTL; API tokens (SHA-256 hash stored in repository_configs, raw token shown once at creation) for CI pipeline integration. Webhooks: HMAC-SHA256 validation only, no JWT.
security.authorization_model: Three roles: pipeline_caller (submit webhook, read analysis results via API token), repo_admin (configure thresholds, manage API tokens for their repos), platform_admin (promote model versions, manage all repos, view audit log).
security.secrets_management: GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket webhook secrets in GCP Secret Manager; fetched at Cloud Run startup. API token hashes in PostgreSQL repository_configs.api_token_hash. JWT signing key in GCP Secret Manager. Never logged.
security.security_risks: Diff content in Python memory during inference; mitigated by ensuring no Sentry breadcrumbs capture diff variables, no structured log fields reference diff content, Python garbage collection of diff strings after scoring completes.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: threshold_change_audit_log and model_promotion_log tables: append-only PostgreSQL role quality_audit_writer. SOC 2 evidence: all threshold config changes and model promotions logged with user_id, changed_values, timestamp.

data.entities: pull_request_analyses (id UUID PK, repo_id UUID FK, pr_number INT, git_provider ENUM('github','gitlab'), base_commit_sha VARCHAR(40), head_commit_sha VARCHAR(40), overall_score FLOAT, complexity_score FLOAT, coverage_delta_pct FLOAT, anti_pattern_count INT, threshold_passed BOOL, model_version_id UUID FK, analysis_duration_ms INT, analyzed_at TIMESTAMP). anti_pattern_findings (id UUID PK, analysis_id UUID FK, pattern_type VARCHAR(80), file_path VARCHAR(500), line_start INT, line_end INT, severity ENUM('error','warning','info'), confidence FLOAT, suggestion TEXT). coverage_snapshots (id UUID PK, repo_id UUID FK, commit_sha VARCHAR(40), coverage_pct FLOAT, captured_at TIMESTAMP). model_versions (id UUID PK, model_name VARCHAR(80), version_tag VARCHAR(40), artifact_gcs_path VARCHAR(500), accuracy_precision FLOAT, accuracy_recall FLOAT, benchmark_dataset_sha VARCHAR(40), promoted_by UUID FK users NULLABLE, promoted_at TIMESTAMP NULLABLE, is_production BOOL DEFAULT false, created_at TIMESTAMP). repository_configs (id UUID PK, repo_id VARCHAR(200) UNIQUE, git_provider ENUM, complexity_threshold FLOAT DEFAULT 7.0, coverage_delta_min_pct FLOAT DEFAULT -5.0, anti_pattern_max_count INT DEFAULT 10, api_token_hash VARCHAR(64), webhook_secret_ref VARCHAR(200), created_by UUID FK users, updated_at TIMESTAMP). threshold_change_audit_log (id UUID PK, user_id UUID, repo_id VARCHAR(200), changed_field VARCHAR(80), old_value TEXT, new_value TEXT, changed_at TIMESTAMP) -- append-only. model_promotion_log (id UUID PK, model_version_id UUID FK, promoted_by UUID FK users, accuracy_precision FLOAT, accuracy_recall FLOAT, benchmark_dataset_sha VARCHAR(40), promoted_at TIMESTAMP) -- append-only.
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL (db-n1-standard-2, 2 vCPU 7.5GB) for analysis records and model metadata. Redis 7 on GKE (or Memorystore) for inference result cache: key=quality:analysis:{head_commit_sha}:{repo_id}, TTL=3600s. GCP Cloud Storage bucket quality-models-prod for model artifacts (.pkl files for sklearn, safetensors for CodeBERT).
data.retention_and_deletion: pull_request_analyses TTL 18 months via monthly Cloud Run Job purge (DELETE WHERE analyzed_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '18 months'). anti_pattern_findings cascade-delete with parent analysis. coverage_snapshots retained 12 months. model_versions retained indefinitely (audit trail). threshold_change_audit_log and model_promotion_log retained indefinitely (SOC 2).

devops.deployment_topology: GCP Cloud Run service (region us-central1, min-instances=2, max-instances=20, 2 vCPU 4GB per instance). Cloud Run Job for weekly model retraining evaluation (2 vCPU 8GB, timeout 3600s). PostgreSQL Cloud SQL in us-central1. Redis 7 via Cloud Memorystore. GCP Secret Manager for webhook secrets and JWT key.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff + mypy + pytest (85% coverage gate) on PR, docker build + push to Artifact Registry on merge, gcloud run deploy --revision-suffix on merge to main. Model artifacts built by offline training pipeline, uploaded to GCS, then promotion via POST /api/v1/models/:version/promote which updates model_versions.is_production. Cloud Run deployment pulls new image; new instances load promoted model at startup.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus via Cloud Run sidecar. Key metrics: scoring_request_duration_ms{provider,repo_id} histogram, model_inference_duration_ms{model_name,version} histogram, webhook_hmac_failures_total counter, inference_cache_hit_rate gauge, coverage_delta_computation_errors_total. Sentry for FastAPI exceptions with diff content explicitly excluded from breadcrumbs.
devops.devops_risks: Cloud Run cold start adding 15-20s model load time during scaling bursts. Mitigated by min-instances=2 keeping warm instances always available for active webhook traffic.

target_output.title: ML-Powered Code Quality Scoring API Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Python FastAPI API service on GCP Cloud Run that analyzes pull request diffs using in-memory HuggingFace CodeBERT and scikit-learn models to produce structured quality scores covering cyclomatic complexity, test coverage delta, and anti-pattern findings. GitHub and GitLab webhooks trigger per-diff analysis with results posted as PR check statuses within 30 seconds. Per-repository quality thresholds are configurable via management API. A weekly Cloud Run Job retrain pipeline evaluates candidate models against a ground truth benchmark dataset and promotes to production only if precision/recall thresholds pass, with all promotions logged to an append-only SOC 2 audit table.
target_output.architecture_overview: Cloud Run service (min-instances=2, max-instances=20, 2 vCPU 4GB) hosts the FastAPI scoring service. On GitHub/GitLab PR webhook receipt, HMAC-SHA256 validation runs before any provider API call. On valid webhook, the service fetches the diff from the provider API, checks Redis for a cached analysis keyed by head_commit_sha+repo_id (TTL 1 hour), and if no cache hit, runs in-memory CodeBERT anti-pattern classification and sklearn complexity scoring on the diff text. Coverage delta is computed by comparing coverage_snapshots for base vs head commit SHA. The scored result is saved to pull_request_analyses and anti_pattern_findings in Cloud SQL PostgreSQL, the PR check status posted to provider API, and result cached in Redis. Models (CodeBERT safetensors + sklearn .pkl) loaded from GCP Cloud Storage at Cloud Run startup into module-level singletons; never reloaded per-request. A separate weekly Cloud Run Job evaluates candidate model accuracy and promotes if benchmarks pass.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Pydantic v2, Uvicorn. ML: HuggingFace transformers (CodeBERT codebert-base), torch (CPU-only), scikit-learn 1.4, joblib for model serialization. Data: asyncpg for PostgreSQL, redis-py async for cache, GCP Cloud Storage client (google-cloud-storage). Auth: PyJWT HS256 for management JWT, hmac stdlib for webhook validation. Provider clients: PyGitHub, python-gitlab. Infra: GCP Cloud Run, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 db-n1-standard-2, Cloud Memorystore Redis 7, GCP Secret Manager, GCP Cloud Scheduler, GCP Artifact Registry. Observability: prometheus-client, Sentry SDK (DSN in Secret Manager). CI/CD: GitHub Actions, gcloud CLI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Webhook Ingestion: HMAC-validated POST receivers for GitHub (X-Hub-Signature-256) and GitLab (X-Gitlab-Token). Scoring Pipeline: diff fetch from provider API, Redis cache lookup by head_commit_sha+repo_id, CodeBERT anti-pattern classification (N anti-patterns with file/line/confidence), sklearn complexity scoring (cyclomatic complexity estimate from diff), coverage delta from coverage_snapshots join, overall_score computation (weighted: 40% complexity, 35% anti-patterns, 25% coverage delta), threshold comparison against repository_configs. PR Check Posting: GitHub Checks API POST /repos/:owner/:repo/check-runs with conclusion pass/fail and structured annotation body containing anti_pattern_findings. Management API: per-repo threshold configuration, API token generation, model version listing, model promotion (platform_admin). Model Retraining Job: weekly benchmark evaluation and accuracy-gated promotion.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Run service (quality-scoring-api, us-central1, min-instances=2). Cloud Run Job (model-retraining-eval, weekly schedule via Cloud Scheduler). Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-n1-standard-2, us-central1, public IP with authorized networks or Cloud SQL Proxy). Cloud Memorystore Redis 7 (1GB, us-central1). GCP Cloud Storage bucket (quality-models-prod: model artifacts; quality-ground-truth: benchmark dataset). GCP Secret Manager (GitHub/GitLab webhook secrets, JWT signing key, Sentry DSN). GCP Cloud Scheduler (weekly model retraining trigger: 0 3 * * 1).
target_output.workflows: PR Scoring: GitHub sends POST /webhooks/github on PR open/synchronize. FastAPI validates X-Hub-Signature-256 HMAC against Secret Manager webhook secret (reject 401 if invalid; no diff fetch on invalid). Fetches PR diff text via PyGitHub API (diff URL from webhook payload). Checks Redis key quality:analysis:{head_sha}:{repo_id}; on hit, returns cached pull_request_analyses id. On miss, passes diff text to CodeBERTAntiPatternModel.predict(diff_text) (in-memory, 3-8s CPU), ComplexityRFModel.predict_complexity(diff_text) (in-memory, <1s). Queries coverage_snapshots for base and head commit SHA to compute coverage_delta_pct. Saves pull_request_analyses and anti_pattern_findings records (not the diff itself). Posts GitHub Check Run with quality score and annotations. Sets Redis cache. Total pipeline target: < 15s (well within 30s SLA). Model Retraining: Cloud Scheduler triggers Cloud Run Job weekly. Job loads ground_truth_reviews.jsonl from GCS (human-labeled PR diffs with expected anti_pattern_findings). Runs batch inference on candidate model (latest non-production model_version). Computes precision and recall against ground truth labels. If precision >= 0.85 AND recall >= 0.80, updates model_versions SET is_production=true, previous is_production=false, writes model_promotion_log entry via quality_audit_writer role. If benchmark fails, logs failure to Cloud Logging and sends Slack alert to platform team. New Cloud Run revision on next deploy loads promoted model from GCS.
target_output.data_model: pull_request_analyses (id UUID PK, repo_id UUID FK repository_configs, pr_number INT NOT NULL, git_provider ENUM('github','gitlab') NOT NULL, base_commit_sha VARCHAR(40), head_commit_sha VARCHAR(40), overall_score FLOAT NOT NULL, complexity_score FLOAT, coverage_delta_pct FLOAT, anti_pattern_count INT DEFAULT 0, threshold_passed BOOL NOT NULL, model_version_id UUID FK model_versions, analysis_duration_ms INT, analyzed_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now()). anti_pattern_findings (id UUID PK, analysis_id UUID FK pull_request_analyses ON DELETE CASCADE, pattern_type VARCHAR(80) NOT NULL, file_path VARCHAR(500), line_start INT, line_end INT, severity ENUM('error','warning','info') DEFAULT 'warning', confidence FLOAT, suggestion TEXT). coverage_snapshots (id UUID PK, repo_id UUID FK, commit_sha VARCHAR(40) NOT NULL, coverage_pct FLOAT NOT NULL, captured_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now(), UNIQUE(repo_id, commit_sha)). model_versions (id UUID PK, model_name VARCHAR(80) NOT NULL -- e.g. 'codebert-anti-pattern-v2', version_tag VARCHAR(40) NOT NULL, artifact_gcs_path VARCHAR(500) NOT NULL -- e.g. 'gs://quality-models-prod/codebert/v2.3/model.safetensors', accuracy_precision FLOAT, accuracy_recall FLOAT, benchmark_dataset_sha VARCHAR(64) -- SHA256 of ground_truth_reviews.jsonl used for eval, is_production BOOL NOT NULL DEFAULT false, promoted_by UUID FK users NULLABLE, promoted_at TIMESTAMP NULLABLE, created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now()). repository_configs (id UUID PK, repo_id VARCHAR(200) UNIQUE NOT NULL, git_provider ENUM NOT NULL, complexity_threshold FLOAT NOT NULL DEFAULT 7.0, coverage_delta_min_pct FLOAT NOT NULL DEFAULT -5.0, anti_pattern_max_count INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 10, api_token_hash VARCHAR(64), webhook_secret_ref VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL, created_by UUID FK users, updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now()). threshold_change_audit_log (id UUID PK, user_id UUID NOT NULL, repo_id VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL, changed_field VARCHAR(80) NOT NULL, old_value TEXT, new_value TEXT NOT NULL, changed_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now()) -- INSERT-only via quality_audit_writer role. model_promotion_log (id UUID PK, model_version_id UUID FK NOT NULL, promoted_by UUID FK users NOT NULL, accuracy_precision FLOAT NOT NULL, accuracy_recall FLOAT NOT NULL, benchmark_dataset_sha VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL, promotion_notes TEXT, promoted_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now()) -- INSERT-only via quality_audit_writer role.
target_output.api_design: POST /webhooks/github (HMAC X-Hub-Signature-256; no JWT). POST /webhooks/gitlab (HMAC X-Gitlab-Token; no JWT). GET /api/v1/analyses/:analysis_id -- full quality report with anti_pattern_findings. GET /api/v1/analyses?repo_id=X&from=2026-01-01&per_page=50 -- analysis history with pagination. POST /api/v1/repos/:repo_id/config {complexity_threshold, coverage_delta_min_pct, anti_pattern_max_count} -- create/update thresholds (repo_admin role; writes threshold_change_audit_log). GET /api/v1/repos/:repo_id/config -- current thresholds. GET /api/v1/models/versions -- list all model versions with accuracy metrics and is_production. POST /api/v1/models/:version_id/promote -- promote to production (platform_admin only; writes model_promotion_log). POST /api/v1/repos/:repo_id/tokens -- generate API token for CI pipeline integration (raw token shown once; SHA-256 hash stored). All /api/v1/* require JWT Bearer except GET /api/v1/analyses/:id and GET /api/v1/analyses which accept API token or JWT.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HMAC-SHA256 validated on all webhook paths before any diff fetch or DB operation; webhook secret fetched from GCP Secret Manager at startup and cached in module memory. JWT HS256 (PyJWT) with 1-hour TTL for management API; signing key from Secret Manager. Diffs processed as Python str in memory only: no tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile, no file write, no logging of diff variables, Sentry add_breadcrumb explicitly disabled for scoring module functions, Python del diff_text called after inference completes to encourage GC. API tokens: SHA-256 hash stored in repository_configs.api_token_hash; raw token returned once on creation and never stored. threshold_change_audit_log and model_promotion_log: PostgreSQL role quality_audit_writer granted INSERT only (no SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE) on these tables; scoring service connects as quality_api role for all other operations; audit writes use separate asyncpg pool as quality_audit_writer. SOC 2 Type II: all threshold changes and model promotions logged with full before/after values and user_id.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Run service quality-scoring-api: region us-central1, min-instances=2, max-instances=20, 2 vCPU 4GB, request-timeout=60s, concurrency=4 (limited by CPU-bound inference). Models loaded at startup: CodeBERT from gs://quality-models-prod/codebert/{version}/model.safetensors, sklearn .pkl from gs://quality-models-prod/sklearn/{version}/model.pkl. Both loaded as module-level singletons; new Cloud Run revision triggers model reload. Cloud Run Job model-retraining-eval: scheduled via Cloud Scheduler (0 3 * * 1 UTC), 2 vCPU 8GB, timeout=3600s, max-retries=1. Job image same as service image with entrypoint overridden to python retraining_job.py. Ground truth dataset at gs://quality-ground-truth/ground_truth_reviews.jsonl (versioned by date prefix). GitHub Actions CI: ruff + mypy --strict + pytest (85% coverage) on PR, docker build + Artifact Registry push + gcloud run deploy --revision-suffix --no-traffic (canary) on merge, traffic migration after smoke test. Cloud Scheduler: weekly retraining job + monthly analysis purge job. PostgreSQL migrations via alembic in CI pre-deploy step.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics on Cloud Run via prometheus-client. Key metrics: scoring_request_duration_ms{provider,repo_id} histogram (buckets: 1,5,10,15,20,25,30s; alert: p99 > 25s -- warn before 30s SLA breach), model_inference_duration_ms{model_name,version} histogram, inference_cache_hit_ratio gauge (alert: < 0.3 for 30 minutes -- possible cache failure), webhook_hmac_validation_failures_total{provider} counter, coverage_delta_computation_errors_total counter, retraining_benchmark_result gauge (1=pass, 0=fail; alert on fail triggers Slack notification to platform team). Sentry for FastAPI unhandled exceptions with scoring module explicitly excluded from breadcrumb capture. Cloud Logging for Cloud Run Job retraining eval output with structured result JSON.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run min-instances=2: ~$120/month baseline (2x 2vCPU 4GB always-on). Request-based scaling adds ~$0.40 per 1M requests. Cloud SQL db-n1-standard-2: ~$100/month. Cloud Memorystore Redis 1GB: ~$45/month. GCS model storage + ground truth: ~$10/month. Secret Manager: ~$5/month. Cloud Scheduler: ~$1/month. Total estimated $280/month at moderate webhook volume. Scaling: Cloud Run scales to 20 instances under CI pipeline burst traffic; CodeBERT inference concurrency=4 per instance limits CPU saturation. Redis cache hit rate directly controls inference scaling costs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): GitHub webhook integration, CodeBERT anti-pattern model v1 (pre-trained, not yet retrained), sklearn complexity scorer, coverage_snapshots population from GitHub Actions coverage upload step, GitHub PR check posting, per-repo threshold configuration API, JWT management auth, Redis inference cache, Cloud SQL setup, Sentry integration with diff exclusion. Phase 2 (6 weeks): GitLab webhook integration, model_versions management API with promotion endpoint, weekly retraining Cloud Run Job with ground_truth_reviews benchmark and model_promotion_log, append-only audit tables (threshold_change_audit_log, model_promotion_log) with quality_audit_writer role, anti_pattern_findings annotation posting to PR checks. Phase 3 (4 weeks): API token generation for CI pipeline integration, analysis history API with pagination, monthly purge Cloud Run Job, SOC 2 audit log export, Prometheus observability dashboards, load testing at 100 concurrent webhooks.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python ruff (line-length=100) + mypy --strict in CI. Pydantic v2 models for all request/response schemas; no dict() usage. All diff text processing in dedicated scoring module with explicit scope; no diff_text parameter passed outside scoring functions. PostgreSQL queries via asyncpg named parameters only; no f-string SQL. Redis cache operations wrapped in try/except -- cache failure must degrade gracefully (score without cache, never fail webhook response). Alembic migrations with down_revision populated (reversible migrations). Model loading isolated in models/loader.py singleton; unit tests mock the singleton to avoid loading 500MB CodeBERT in test suite. Ground truth dataset versioned by date in GCS; retraining job logs dataset SHA to model_promotion_log for reproducibility.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CodeBERT model size (~500MB) causing Cloud Run cold start memory pressure and 15-20s startup. Mitigation: min-instances=2 prevents cold starts for active traffic; Cloud Run startup CPU boost enabled. Risk: Ground truth dataset becoming stale if engineering teams change coding patterns significantly. Mitigation: quarterly ground truth review session with platform team; dataset versioned in GCS with SHA logged to model_promotion_log. Risk: Inference concurrency=4 per instance creating request queuing under burst webhook traffic. Mitigation: Cloud Run max-instances=20 scales horizontally; Redis cache reduces inference invocations on repeated commits. Tradeoff: CPU-only CodeBERT vs GPU Cloud Run -- CPU inference runs 3-8s acceptable for 30s SLA; GPU would cost 10x and require custom Cloud Run GPU configuration. Tradeoff: In-house fine-tuned CodeBERT vs OpenAI API -- avoids diff data egress to external API, eliminates per-request API cost at scale, but requires maintaining model retraining infrastructure.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: model_promotion_log written by INSERT-only quality_audit_writer PostgreSQL role so scoring service cannot tamper with audit trail. Model promotion criteria defined as precision >= 0.85 AND recall >= 0.80 on ground_truth_reviews benchmark; both metrics required to prevent precision-only gaming. Cloud Run concurrency=4 per instance determined by empirical profiling of CodeBERT CPU inference time at 2 vCPU; 4 concurrent requests at 3-8s each fits within Cloud Run 60s request timeout with margin.

metadata.notes: Round 3 resolves model retraining pipeline and model_versions schema gaps; ML code quality scoring API on Cloud Run with CodeBERT and sklearn models.

profile.domain: Secrets Management Infrastructure

project_goal.value: Migrate internal services from env var secret distribution to a vault-backed secrets broker REST API with rotation scheduling and immutable audit logging
project_goal.rationale: Eliminating plaintext env vars in deployment manifests reduces secret sprawl and blast radius

target_users.value: Internal microservices via service identity tokens and platform engineers via operator API
target_users.rationale: Both automated consumers and human operators need clean access patterns

project_class.rationale: Pure REST API with no browser UI; all access is programmatic or CLI-driven

capabilities.rationale: Service identity auth, versioned secret storage, and REST exposure are the core capability set

complexity_level.rationale: Vault abstraction, rotation scheduling, migration tooling, and immutable audit trail require careful orchestration

risk_level.rationale: Broker holds access to all production secrets; compromise would be catastrophic

data_sensitivity.rationale: Secret values and service identity tokens are the most sensitive data in the organization

external_exposure.rationale: Broker must never be reachable from the public internet; VPC-internal only

access_model.value: Service identity JWT per microservice with RBAC scoped to secret namespaces; human operators use short-lived OIDC tokens
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege per-service access prevents lateral movement if a service identity is compromised

feature_scope.value: Secret CRUD with versioning, rotation schedule management, access policy enforcement, audit log query, migration import tooling, vault binding management
feature_scope.rationale: Full secret lifecycle from creation through rotation to deletion

mvp_scope.value: Secret read/write/delete, service identity auth, namespace RBAC, immutable audit log, AWS Secrets Manager backing, migration import CLI for 10 pilot services
mvp_scope.rationale: Enough to validate migration approach before full org rollout

security_baseline.value: mTLS on service-facing listener, secret values never logged, audit trail INSERT-only at DB role level, secret versions immutable after creation
security_baseline.rationale: Zero-trust controls required for a service brokering all production secrets

frontend_stack.rationale: No browser UI; all interaction via REST API or migration CLI binary

backend_stack.rationale: Low memory footprint and fast cold start critical for a broker on hot paths of every service startup

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for metadata, policies, audit records; AWS Secrets Manager as encrypted value backing store
data_platform.rationale: Postgres for structured metadata queries; AWS SM for encrypted value storage with native rotation support

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in private subnets, internal ALB only, no public route
hosting_target.rationale: No internet-facing load balancer; VPC-internal access only

privacy_retention_policy.value: Audit log entries retained 7 years; secret metadata retained 2 years after deletion; secret values purged immediately on soft-delete
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Compliance and forensic investigation windows drive retention requirements

future_scope.value: HashiCorp Vault backend adapter, dynamic database credential generation, Kubernetes external-secrets operator integration
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP vault flexibility and dynamic credentials are the next highest-value capabilities

constraints.value: Must support AWS Secrets Manager and HashiCorp Vault as interchangeable backends; migration cannot require service downtime
constraints.rationale: Backend portability and zero-downtime migration are non-negotiable for adoption

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with per-endpoint latency, secret access rate metrics, rotation success/failure counters, audit write latency histogram
observability_baseline.rationale: Access rate anomaly detection is a key security signal for the broker

execution_preference.value: Gated pilot rollout: each phase ships only after 5+ services validate the integration pattern
execution_preference.rationale: Migration carries operational risk; gated rollout reduces blast radius

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II CC6 logical access and CC7 system operations; GDPR Article 32 for secrets referencing personal data endpoints
compliance_context.rationale: Secrets broker is in scope for SOC 2 logical access audit; secrets holding PII-adjacent credentials fall under GDPR technical safeguards

project.goal: Centralize secret distribution via a vault-backed broker API replacing env var injection patterns
project.target_users: Microservices via service identity, platform engineers via REST and CLI
project.timeline: Phase 1 pilot in 8 weeks, full migration in 6 months

security.auth: Service identity JWT with per-namespace RBAC; OIDC for human operators
security.transport: mTLS on service-facing listener; TLS 1.3 minimum for all connections
security.secrets: Secret values never written to logs or error responses

data.backing_store: AWS Secrets Manager for encrypted values
data.retention: 7-year audit log; values purged on soft-delete

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with gosec, gitleaks, race detector, Trivy

reasoner_reviews.product: Scope is well-targeted to the migration problem. Service identity auth and namespace-scoped policies directly address the security concerns with flat env var distribution. The import CLI for batch migration is essential for adoption velocity.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Chi is an excellent choice for a broker on hot service startup paths; p99 latency must stay under 5ms for reads. The abstraction layer over Secrets Manager is well-designed but the vault binding interface needs a defined contract before HashiCorp Vault support ships. Secret values must never flow through Postgres.
reasoner_reviews.security: The mTLS requirement on the service-facing listener was missing in round 2 and is now addressed. Service identity JWTs with namespace RBAC prevent credential sprawl. The audit log INSERT-only DB role grant must be enforced via migration script and validated in integration tests. Rotation notification via SNS closes the stale-value gap.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero-downtime migration is achievable with the dual-read approach during cutover. VPC-internal-only hosting eliminates the largest attack surface. The 7-year audit retention aligns with SOC 2 CC7 and legal requirements.

backend.service_design: Two Chi router instances in one binary: service router on port 8443 (mTLS + JWT) and operator router on port 9443 (OIDC). Modules: secrets (CRUD + versioning), rotation (schedule management + execution), policies (namespace RBAC), audit (INSERT-only writes + query), migration (import + status reporting). Vault adapter interface implemented for AWS SM with stub for future HashiCorp Vault.
backend.api_patterns: GET /v1/secrets/:namespace/:name returns current version value with access logged atomically before response. POST /v1/secrets/:namespace creates secret with initial version. PUT /v1/secrets/:namespace/:name/versions creates new immutable version. DELETE soft-deletes with SM value purge queued. GET /v1/audit paginated with namespace, actor, action, time filters. POST /v1/rotation-schedules configures cron.
backend.failure_handling: Secret read failures return 503 with Retry-After header; never return partial or stale values on SM errors. Rotation job failures retry with exponential backoff up to 3 attempts before alerting. Audit write failures block the originating secret request to preserve log integrity over availability.
backend.backend_risks: AWS SM API throttling at high read concurrency; mitigated by 30-second in-process value cache per namespace with SNS-triggered invalidation on rotation. Go race detector in CI catches concurrency bugs in rotation scheduler goroutines.

security.auth_design: Service identity JWTs signed by internal CA, 15-minute expiry, bound to the service ECS task role ARN. Operator tokens via OIDC from corporate IdP, 1-hour expiry. All tokens validated on every request with no caching of authorization decisions.
security.authorization_model: Namespace-scoped RBAC: service identities get read access to their declared namespaces only. Rotation service account gets write access to specific namespaces. Operators get full CRUD within their team namespace. Cross-namespace grants require explicit policy entry with audit record.
security.secrets_management: Secret values fetched from AWS SM on each read, never persisted in Postgres. No secret values in application logs, error messages, or non-read API responses. AWS KMS CMK encrypts all SM values at rest. zerolog configured to redact fields named 'value' and 'secret'.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_log_entries is INSERT-only for the application DB role. Postgres trigger raises exception on UPDATE or DELETE attempts. Audit records capture: namespace, secret name, version ID, actor identity, actor type, source IP, ECS task ID, action type, timestamp. CloudTrail supplements with AWS-level SM GetSecretValue events.

data.entities: secret_records (id UUID PK, namespace, name, created_by, created_at, deleted_at, current_version_id FK). secret_versions (id UUID PK, secret_id FK, version_number INT, sm_arn, checksum, created_at, no updates permitted). access_policies (id UUID PK, namespace, grantee_identity, permission_level ENUM read/write/admin, granted_by, expires_at). audit_log_entries (id UUID PK, secret_namespace, secret_name, version_accessed, actor_identity, actor_type ENUM service/operator, source_ip, ecs_task_id, action ENUM read/write/delete/rotate, occurred_at) PARTITIONED BY RANGE (occurred_at). rotation_schedules (id UUID PK, secret_id FK, cron_expression, last_run_at, next_run_at, rotation_handler_type, last_status ENUM success/failed/pending, notification_status).
data.storage_design: Postgres holds all metadata and audit records. audit_log_entries partitioned monthly for retention management. AWS SM holds encrypted values referenced by ARN. S3 bucket stores migration import job archives with 90-day lifecycle policy.
