data.retention_and_deletion: Monthly partition archival for audit_log_entries older than 2 years to S3 Glacier with Athena query support. Secret values in AWS SM deleted immediately on soft-delete. Rotation history retained as long as parent secret exists.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate service in private subnets. Internal ALB (not internet-facing) with mTLS listener for port 8443 and standard TLS listener for port 9443. No public route table association for broker subnets.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint, gosec SAST, gitleaks secrets scan, race-detector test run, integration tests against dockerized Postgres 16 and LocalStack for SM, Trivy container scan, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with health check gate.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM on each ECS task. Custom metrics: secret_reads_total by namespace, rotation_job_duration_seconds histogram, audit_write_latency_ms histogram, policy_eval_duration_ms. Alerts: rotation failure count > 0 in 5-min window (P1), audit write p99 > 100ms (P2), secret read error rate > 0.1% (P1).
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS blue/green deploy with ALB weighted routing. Rollback triggered by consecutive health check failures. Postgres migrations are additive-only; rollback does not require schema changes.

ISS-001.title: mTLS not enforced on service-to-broker transport
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 specified TLS 1.3 but omitted mutual client certificate authentication. JWT alone is insufficient at the transport layer.

ISS-002.title: Rotation notification gap leaves bound services holding stale values
ISS-002.detail: Previous plans updated SM values without a mechanism for bound services to learn rotation occurred. Services would hold stale values until restart.

ISS-003.title: Audit log INSERT-only enforcement not specified at DB role level
ISS-003.detail: Round 2 described an audit log but did not specify that the application DB role should have INSERT-only grants, leaving audit records vulnerable to tampering.

focus_issues.title: mTLS not enforced on service-to-broker transport
focus_issues.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 specified TLS 1.3 but omitted mutual client certificate authentication.

focus_issues.title: Rotation notification gap leaves bound services holding stale values
focus_issues.detail: No mechanism for bound services to learn rotation occurred.

latest_recommendations: Enforce mTLS at transport layer for service-to-broker calls in addition to JWT validation
Add rotation event notification via SNS so bound services can flush stale cached values
Grant INSERT-only on audit_log_entries to the application DB role

convergence_state.reason: Critical mTLS gap and rotation notification gap both unresolved after round 2

previous_audits.summary: Plan demonstrates solid broker pattern understanding but fails on two critical security controls non-negotiable for a service holding production secrets.
strengths: Clean vault adapter abstraction separating metadata from value storage
Namespace-scoped RBAC model is well-specified
Migration import tooling design is practical
concerns: mTLS absent from service-to-broker transport
No rotation notification mechanism for bound services
Audit log INSERT-only enforcement not specified
blocking_issues: ISS-001: mTLS required on service-to-broker path
ISS-002: Rotation must notify bound services
recommendations: Add mTLS client certificate requirement to internal ALB listener and Go TLS config
Implement rotation notification via SNS topic with per-namespace subscriptions
Document DB role grants: INSERT-only on audit_log_entries

issue_updates.title: mTLS not enforced
issue_updates.detail: Security section describes TLS 1.3 but does not mention client certificate requirement

issue_updates.title: Rotation notification gap
issue_updates.detail: Rotation section does not describe how services learn a rotation has occurred

previous_plan.title: Secrets Broker API v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Go-based vault abstraction API on ECS Fargate that centralizes secret distribution for internal microservices, replacing environment variable patterns with namespace-scoped REST access backed by AWS Secrets Manager.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Chi router serving two listener groups on ECS Fargate private subnet: service API on port 8443 requiring service identity JWT, and management API on port 9443 for operators. AWS Secrets Manager holds encrypted values. PostgreSQL 16 stores metadata, policies, rotation schedules, and audit log.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: TLS 1.3 on all connections. Service identity JWTs validated on every request. Namespace-scoped RBAC enforcement. Audit log records all secret access events. SOC 2 CC6 controls documented.
previous_plan.data_model: secret_records, secret_versions with SM ARN references, access_policies with namespace scoping, audit_log_entries, rotation_schedules. Values never stored in Postgres.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 must close the critical mTLS gap and the rotation notification gap that blocked passing in round 2. Adding mTLS to the internal ALB listener and Go TLS config for the service-facing router. Rotation notification uses SNS per namespace so bound services can flush in-process caches. The audit INSERT-only DB role grant is now explicitly documented and enforced via migration script. All three focus issues resolved.

fix_report.action_taken: Added mTLS configuration to the internal ALB listener requiring client certificates signed by the internal CA, and to the Go TLS server config with ClientAuth=RequireAndVerifyClientCert. Service identity JWT validation now layers on top of mutual TLS for defense in depth.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Every service-to-broker connection authenticated at both transport and application layers, eliminating risk of rogue VPC process reaching the broker without a valid client certificate.

fix_report.action_taken: Added rotation notification subsystem: on successful rotation the broker publishes a rotation_completed event to an SNS topic scoped to the secret namespace. Each bound service subscribes via SQS and flushes its in-process cache on receipt. rotation_schedules table now tracks notification_status.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Bound services refresh to new secret version within seconds of rotation, eliminating the stale-value window that previously lasted until service restart.

fix_report.action_taken: Explicitly documented DB role permission model: application role has INSERT-only on audit_log_entries with no UPDATE or DELETE grants. A Postgres trigger raises an exception on any UPDATE or DELETE attempt as a secondary guard. Enforced in migration scripts and validated in integration tests.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Audit log entries are tamper-proof at the database layer, satisfying SOC 2 CC7 immutability requirement.

target_output.title: Secrets Broker API - Centralized Vault Abstraction Layer
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 secrets management API on AWS ECS Fargate that abstracts AWS Secrets Manager behind a unified REST interface, enabling internal microservices to migrate from environment variable secret distribution to namespace-scoped mTLS-authenticated access with rotation scheduling, SNS-driven refresh notifications, and an immutable audit trail enforced at the DB role level. Two listeners serve separate concerns: a service-facing mTLS endpoint on port 8443 and an OIDC-authenticated operator management endpoint on port 9443.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-listener architecture on ECS Fargate private subnet: service-facing listener on port 8443 with mTLS (client certs signed by internal CA) layered under service identity JWT validation, and operator management listener on port 9443 gated by OIDC tokens from the corporate IdP. Chi router with middleware stack handles routing, auth, and synchronous audit logging. AWS Secrets Manager stores encrypted secret values referenced by ARN from PostgreSQL 16 metadata records. Rotation scheduler runs as a separate ECS scheduled task and publishes rotation_completed events to per-namespace SNS topics on success. Bound services consume events from SQS subscriptions and flush in-process caches. All audit writes are synchronous and block the originating request to ensure log integrity.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22 with Chi router, aws-sdk-go-v2 for Secrets Manager and SNS, golang-jwt/jwt for token validation, crypto/tls for mTLS config, pgx/v5 for Postgres. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with monthly partitioning on audit_log_entries. Messaging: AWS SNS for rotation notifications, SQS for per-service subscription queues. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate private subnets, internal ALB with mTLS listener, AWS KMS CMK for SM encryption, ACM Private CA for service certificates. Observability: Datadog APM sidecar. CI: GitHub Actions with gosec, staticcheck, race detector, gitleaks, Trivy.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Secret Lifecycle: create with initial version, read current version value plus metadata, create new immutable version, soft-delete with immediate SM purge queue. Rotation Management: configure cron-based schedules, execute rotation with new SM version creation, publish SNS completion event, track notification delivery status per rotation. Access Policies: grant and revoke namespace-scoped RBAC, enumerate active policies, cross-namespace grant with mandatory audit record. Audit Query: paginated log with filters by namespace, actor identity, action type, and time range. Migration Import: batch import from env var manifest YAML, parallel secret creation with progress tracking, onboarding status report by service name.
target_output.system_components: Internal ALB with mTLS listener (client cert CA pinned to internal PKI CA). ECS Fargate service: single Go binary exposing two Chi router instances on separate ports. Rotation ECS scheduled task publishing to per-namespace SNS topics. PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ in private subnet with audit_log_entries partitioned by month. AWS Secrets Manager as value store with KMS CMK. SNS topics per secret namespace for rotation events. SQS queues (consumer-managed) for rotation subscriptions. S3 bucket for migration import job archives with 90-day lifecycle. Datadog agent sidecar on each Fargate task.
target_output.workflows: Secret Read by Microservice: service presents client certificate and service identity JWT on connection to port 8443; Chi mTLS middleware validates cert against internal CA cert pool; auth middleware validates JWT and extracts namespace claims; RBAC middleware checks access_policies for the requested namespace; handler fetches sm_arn from Postgres, calls SM GetSecretValue, writes audit_log_entry INSERT atomically, returns value in response. Target p99 under 5ms with in-process cache. Rotation Execution: ECS scheduled task reads due rotation_schedules records; calls rotation handler to generate new value in SM; writes new secret_version record with new SM ARN; updates rotation_schedule last_run_at, next_run_at, and last_status; publishes rotation_completed SNS message with namespace and secret name; updates notification_status on rotation record. Bound service SQS consumer flushes in-process cache entry on receipt. Migration Import: operator uploads env var manifest YAML to S3 import bucket; migration ECS task reads manifest, calls POST /v1/secrets in batches of 20 with retry, records onboarding status per service, generates migration report JSON with created/failed/skipped counts and posts to operator API.
target_output.data_model: secret_records (id UUID PK, namespace VARCHAR NOT NULL, name VARCHAR NOT NULL, created_by VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, current_version_id UUID FK, UNIQUE(namespace, name)). secret_versions (id UUID PK, secret_id UUID FK NOT NULL, version_number INT NOT NULL, sm_arn VARCHAR NOT NULL, checksum VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL; no updates permitted by app role). access_policies (id UUID PK, namespace VARCHAR NOT NULL, grantee_identity VARCHAR NOT NULL, permission_level permission_level_enum NOT NULL, granted_by VARCHAR, granted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_log_entries (id UUID PK, secret_namespace VARCHAR, secret_name VARCHAR, version_accessed UUID, actor_identity VARCHAR, actor_type actor_type_enum, source_ip INET, ecs_task_id VARCHAR, action action_enum, occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL) PARTITIONED BY RANGE (occurred_at); app role has INSERT-only grant; trigger raises exception on UPDATE or DELETE. rotation_schedules (id UUID PK, secret_id UUID FK, cron_expression VARCHAR, last_run_at TIMESTAMPTZ, next_run_at TIMESTAMPTZ, rotation_handler_type VARCHAR, last_status rotation_status_enum, notification_status VARCHAR). service_identities (id UUID PK, service_name VARCHAR, task_role_arn VARCHAR, allowed_namespaces TEXT[], created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Service API port 8443 (mTLS + JWT): GET /v1/secrets/:namespace/:name returns current version value and metadata, audit logged. POST /v1/secrets/:namespace creates secret with initial version, returns secret_id and version_id. PUT /v1/secrets/:namespace/:name/versions creates new immutable version. DELETE /v1/secrets/:namespace/:name soft-deletes, queues SM value purge. GET /v1/secrets/:namespace lists secret names without values. Operator API port 9443 (OIDC): POST /v1/policies grants namespace access. DELETE /v1/policies/:id revokes. GET /v1/audit?namespace=&from=&to=&actor=&action=&page= returns paginated audit entries. POST /v1/rotation-schedules/:namespace/:name configures cron. POST /v1/migration/import submits import job from S3 key. GET /v1/migration/status/:job_id returns progress and onboarding report.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS: internal ALB listener requires client certificates from internal PKI CA; Go TLS config sets ClientAuth=RequireAndVerifyClientCert with internal CA cert pool loaded at startup. Service identity JWT: HS256 tokens with sub=service_name and namespaces claims, 15-minute expiry, validated on every request with no caching. RBAC: Chi middleware evaluates access_policies before handler; 403 with no detail on policy miss to avoid namespace enumeration. Audit immutability: application DB role has INSERT-only grant on audit_log_entries; Postgres trigger raises exception on UPDATE or DELETE; enforced in migration scripts and validated in integration tests. Secret values never in logs: zerolog configured to redact fields named value and secret; error responses contain only error codes. SOC 2 CC6: all access logged, least-privilege service identities, policy grants audited. SOC 2 CC7: immutable audit trail, rotation events logged with notification status. GDPR Art.32: KMS CMK encryption for values at rest, TLS 1.3 minimum in transit.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline: go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint, gosec SAST, gitleaks secrets scan, race-detector test run, integration tests against dockerized Postgres 16 and LocalStack for SM simulation, Trivy container scan, ECR push tagged with git SHA, ECS rolling deploy with 100% minimum healthy. Environments: dev (single AZ Fargate 1 task, shared RDS dev), staging (multi-AZ Fargate 2 tasks, dedicated RDS), production (multi-AZ Fargate 3 tasks min / 6 max auto-scaling, RDS Multi-AZ with automated daily backups). Internal ALB only with no public route table entries for broker subnets. mTLS listener cert rotation: 90-day CA-signed cert with 30-day pre-expiry alert and automated ACM Private CA renewal. DB migrations via golang-migrate applied in a pre-deploy ECS one-off task. Broker's own DB password stored in Secrets Manager with 30-day automated rotation.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces every secret read and rotation execution with namespace and actor_type tags. Custom metrics: secret_reads_total counter by namespace, rotation_job_duration_seconds histogram, rotation_notification_delivery_latency_ms histogram, audit_write_latency_ms histogram, policy_eval_duration_ms histogram. Alerts: rotation failure count > 0 in any 5-minute window triggers PagerDuty P1; audit write p99 > 100ms triggers P2; secret read error rate > 0.1% triggers P1; SNS delivery failure rate > 1% triggers P2. CloudTrail captures all AWS SM GetSecretValue calls as a secondary audit layer independent of application logs.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate 3 tasks at 0.5 vCPU / 1GB each approximately $120/month. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ db.t3.medium approximately $180/month. AWS Secrets Manager at 400 secrets with 10K reads/day approximately $40/month. SNS and SQS rotation notifications approximately $5/month. Total under $400/month at pilot scale. Scale path: Fargate task count auto-scales on CPU target 50%. SM read throttling at high concurrency handled by 30-second in-process value cache per namespace reducing SM API calls by approximately 80%, with SNS-triggered cache invalidation on rotation events.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP Pilot (8 weeks): service identity JWT auth with mTLS, namespace RBAC, secret CRUD with AWS SM backing, immutable audit log with INSERT-only DB role enforcement, migration import CLI, onboard 10 pilot services with migration report. Phase 2 Rotation and Notifications (4 weeks): rotation schedule engine with cron evaluation, SNS notification per namespace on rotation completion, SQS subscription helper library for consuming services, rotation history on operator API. Phase 3 Full Migration (8 weeks): all internal services onboarded, migration completion reports by team, decommission env var injection in service manifests, HashiCorp Vault backend adapter. Phase 4 Dynamic Credentials (roadmap): database credential generation via Vault dynamic secrets, Kubernetes external-secrets operator integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 strict golangci-lint config including errcheck, govet, staticcheck, gosec. Race detector enabled in all CI test runs. Secret values zeroed from memory after use with explicit byte slice zeroing. No secret values in struct fields implementing fmt.Stringer. Integration tests use dockertest for Postgres 16 and LocalStack for SM. PR requires two approvals for any change touching auth middleware, audit log writes, or policy evaluation logic. ADR document required for every decision affecting secret value handling paths.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: AWS SM API throttling at high read concurrency from hundreds of services simultaneously starting up. Mitigation: 30-second in-process TTL cache per namespace with SNS-triggered invalidation on rotation events reducing SM calls by ~80%. Risk: Internal CA certificate expiry causing mTLS failures across all services. Mitigation: 90-day cert rotation with 30-day pre-expiry alert and automated ACM Private CA renewal. Tradeoff: synchronous audit write blocking secret reads provides stronger integrity than async at cost of p99 latency; acceptable given SOC 2 CC7 requirement. Tradeoff: INSERT-only audit log prevents correction of erroneous entries; compensated by correction_notes addendum pattern for post-hoc documentation.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed AWS Secrets Manager over HashiCorp Vault for MVP given existing AWS footprint; Vault adapter added post-pilot. Confirmed SNS plus SQS over polling for rotation notifications to eliminate stale-value windows. Confirmed synchronous audit write over async to satisfy SOC 2 CC7 immutability. mTLS client cert CA pinned to internal PKI rather than public CA to avoid external dependency.

metadata.notes: Round 3 closes critical mTLS and rotation notification gaps; all 3 focus issues resolved with detailed fix report.

profile.domain: Developer Tooling / AI Documentation

project_goal.value: AI-powered developer documentation assistant that ingests internal wikis, API specs, and ADRs to answer natural language questions about system architecture and coding conventions using RAG over a vector store
project_goal.rationale: Reducing onboarding time and Slack question load by surfacing institutional knowledge on demand

target_users.value: Internal software engineers, new hires onboarding, and team leads verifying architecture decisions
target_users.rationale: Engineering org is the primary beneficiary of faster knowledge discovery

project_class.rationale: RAG pipeline with LLM generation, vector store, and ingestion jobs is an AI system architecture

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Chat UI, RAG API, vector store, Okta auth, Confluence and GitHub ingestion integrations

complexity_level.rationale: RAG pipeline with chunking strategy, vector search tuning, citation assembly, and feedback loop adds significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Ingests proprietary internal documentation; LLM output quality risk manageable with citation and feedback mechanisms

data_sensitivity.rationale: Internal wikis and ADRs contain proprietary architecture decisions and unreleased roadmap information

external_exposure.rationale: Okta SSO required; only authenticated employees can query the assistant

access_model.value: Okta SSO with group-based source visibility; engineers see only documents from their team namespaces plus global shared docs
access_model.rationale: Prevents cross-team proprietary architecture leakage in answers

feature_scope.value: Confluence and GitHub wiki ingestion, document chunking and embedding, semantic search, LLM answer generation with citations, feedback rating, answer history, admin source management
feature_scope.rationale: Full RAG lifecycle from ingestion through answer delivery and quality feedback

mvp_scope.value: Confluence space ingestion, pgvector semantic search, GPT-4o answer generation with source citations, thumbs up/down feedback, basic chat history
mvp_scope.rationale: Sufficient to validate answer quality with a pilot engineering team before org-wide rollout

security_baseline.value: Okta OIDC for authentication, namespace-scoped vector search to enforce source visibility, no proprietary document content in application logs
security_baseline.rationale: Source visibility scoping prevents confidential architecture details leaking across team boundaries

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise and Vercel deployment option; streaming response support via Next.js App Router

backend_stack.rationale: LangChain and OpenAI SDK ecosystem maturity in Python; async FastAPI for streaming responses

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension for vector storage; S3 for raw document storage
data_platform.rationale: pgvector consolidates relational metadata and vector search in a single managed service, avoiding a separate vector DB

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate for backend and ingestion workers; CloudFront plus S3 for Next.js static export
hosting_target.rationale: Fargate for backend flexibility; CloudFront for global low-latency frontend delivery

privacy_retention_policy.value: Query sessions retained 90 days; feedback ratings retained indefinitely for model quality tracking; ingested document chunks deleted on source removal
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 90-day query history supports debugging and quality analysis without indefinite personal data retention

future_scope.value: GitHub wiki and Notion ingestion connectors, Slack thread ingestion, answer confidence scoring, proactive architecture change notifications
future_scope.rationale: Expanding source coverage is the highest-value post-MVP capability

constraints.value: All document content must remain within AWS us-east-1; no document text sent to external services other than OpenAI API with DPA in place
constraints.rationale: Data residency requirement for proprietary documentation; OpenAI DPA satisfies legal review

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for API latency; custom metrics for query latency, embedding generation throughput, feedback score distribution, and retrieval chunk count per query
observability_baseline.rationale: RAG pipeline quality metrics are as important as system latency for ongoing tuning

execution_preference.value: Fortnightly sprints with pilot team feedback gates before broader rollout
execution_preference.rationale: LLM answer quality requires iterative tuning with real user feedback before wide deployment

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II in scope for data handling; OpenAI DPA signed covering document text transmission; internal data handling policy for proprietary documentation
compliance_context.rationale: System processes confidential internal documentation with an external LLM API

llm_integration.value: OpenAI GPT-4o for answer generation; text-embedding-3-small for document and query embeddings; 8K token context window with top-5 chunk retrieval strategy
llm_integration.rationale: GPT-4o provides best citation quality; text-embedding-3-small balances cost and retrieval accuracy at this document volume

project.goal: RAG-powered internal documentation assistant answering natural language architecture and convention questions
project.timeline: MVP pilot in 6 weeks, org rollout in 12 weeks

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for chat session state

backend.api: REST with streaming SSE for answer generation

security.auth: Okta OIDC with group-based namespace scoping
security.data: No document content in application logs

data.storage: S3 for raw document files and chunk archives

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate backend, CloudFront plus S3 frontend

reasoner_reviews.product: The RAG scope is well-defined for the pilot. Confluence ingestion plus pgvector search plus GPT-4o generation covers the core value proposition. The feedback rating loop is essential for quality tracking and should be captured from day one, not added later.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: pgvector is the right consolidation choice for this document volume; a separate vector DB like Pinecone adds operational complexity with marginal retrieval quality gain at under 1M chunks. Namespace-scoped vector search via metadata filters on the embedding table is the critical control for source visibility. The streaming SSE response path needs careful handling to avoid buffering entire LLM outputs before sending.
reasoner_reviews.security: Namespace-scoped retrieval is the critical security control - the pgvector query must always include a namespace filter derived from the authenticated user's Okta groups, never user-supplied. No document chunk content should appear in structured logs. The OpenAI DPA must be confirmed before any document text is sent.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: OpenAI API latency adds 1-3 seconds to answer generation; streaming SSE mitigates perceived latency. Embedding generation for a large Confluence space can take hours for initial ingestion; use incremental ingestion with change detection to keep re-ingestion fast.

backend.service_design: FastAPI app with routers: query (RAG pipeline endpoint with streaming SSE), ingestion (job trigger and status), feedback (rating submission), sources (admin source management), auth (Okta callback). Separate ECS worker service for ingestion jobs. LangChain RAG chain with custom retriever enforcing namespace filter.
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/query streams answer tokens via SSE with citation chunks in final event. GET /v1/history returns paginated query sessions for authenticated user. POST /v1/feedback/:query_id submits thumbs rating. POST /v1/admin/sources triggers ingestion job for a Confluence space or GitHub wiki URL. GET /v1/admin/sources/:job_id returns ingestion progress.
backend.llm_integration_design: LangChain RetrievalQA chain: query embedding via text-embedding-3-small, pgvector similarity search with namespace metadata filter returning top-5 chunks, context window assembly with source metadata, GPT-4o generation with system prompt enforcing citation format. Streaming via LangChain streaming callback writing to SSE response. Token usage logged per query for cost tracking.
backend.failure_handling: OpenAI API timeout after 30 seconds returns graceful error with suggestion to rephrase. pgvector query timeout after 2 seconds. Ingestion job failures captured in ingestion_jobs table with error detail; admin notified via email on failure.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router with server components for initial history render. Chat interface as client component with streaming token display. Auth via NextAuth.js with Okta provider.
frontend.pages_and_flows: / redirects to /chat. /chat shows query input, streaming answer display with citation cards, thumbs feedback buttons, and session history sidebar. /admin/sources shows ingestion job list and trigger form for platform admins.
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for active chat session tokens and citation state. React Query for history pagination and source list. SSE handled via native EventSource API with token accumulation into display buffer.
frontend.performance_notes: Streaming SSE renders tokens progressively to avoid blank wait screen during LLM generation. Citation cards rendered lazily after answer stream completes. History list virtualized for users with long query history.

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC via NextAuth.js. JWT contains group membership claims used to derive allowed_namespaces list. Token refreshed every hour. Backend validates JWT on every request and extracts namespaces for vector search filter.
security.authorization_model: Namespace filter injected into every pgvector query from the authenticated user's group claims. Admin endpoints gated by membership in platform-admins Okta group. Users can only see their own query history.
security.privacy_controls: Document chunk content never written to structured application logs. Query text logged only as SHA-256 hash for deduplication metrics. Feedback ratings stored without query text linkage after 90-day session expiry.

data.entities: knowledge_sources (id UUID PK, source_type ENUM confluence/github_wiki/notion, source_url, namespace, team_id, last_ingested_at, chunk_count). document_chunks (id UUID PK, source_id FK, chunk_index INT, content_hash VARCHAR, namespace VARCHAR, embedding vector(1536), metadata JSONB, created_at). query_sessions (id UUID PK, user_id VARCHAR, query_text_hash VARCHAR, answer_text TEXT, citation_chunk_ids UUID[], model_version VARCHAR, token_usage INT, created_at). user_feedback_ratings (id UUID PK, query_session_id FK, rating ENUM positive/negative, comment TEXT, created_at). ingestion_jobs (id UUID PK, source_id FK, status ENUM queued/running/completed/failed, chunks_processed INT, error_detail TEXT, started_at, completed_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector for document_chunks with HNSW index on embedding column for fast ANN search. IVFFlat index tuned at ingestion with lists=100 for initial dataset size. S3 stores raw document files and full chunk export archives for re-ingestion.
data.analytics_events: query_submitted (user_id hashed, namespace, chunk_count retrieved, token_usage, latency_ms), feedback_submitted (query_id, rating), ingestion_completed (source_id, chunk_count, duration_ms).

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate API service behind ALB with Okta-validated JWT. Separate ECS Fargate worker service for ingestion jobs triggered by SQS queue. CloudFront distribution for Next.js static export from S3. RDS PostgreSQL with pgvector in private subnet.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Python ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest with 80% coverage gate, Next.js build, Docker build and Trivy scan, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy. Ingestion worker deployed as separate ECS service updating independently.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM for API and worker. Custom metrics: query_latency_ms p50/p95/p99, chunks_retrieved_per_query histogram, embedding_generation_throughput, feedback_positive_rate, ingestion_chunks_per_minute, openai_token_spend_daily.

ISS-001.title: Namespace filter not enforced at pgvector query level
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described namespace scoping conceptually but did not specify that the namespace filter must be injected from JWT claims server-side and never derived from user input.

ISS-002.title: Ingestion job has no incremental change detection
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 described full re-ingestion on each run, which would re-embed unchanged documents and spike OpenAI embedding costs for large Confluence spaces.

focus_issues.title: Namespace filter not enforced at pgvector query level
focus_issues.detail: Namespace must be derived from JWT claims server-side, never from user input.

latest_recommendations: Enforce namespace filter injection from JWT claims in the pgvector retriever, document this explicitly in security section
Add content_hash-based change detection to ingestion jobs to skip unchanged chunks

convergence_state.reason: Critical namespace filter enforcement gap unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Solid RAG architecture with good LangChain integration design, but the critical namespace security control is underspecified and the ingestion cost risk is unaddressed.
strengths: pgvector consolidation avoids separate vector DB operational overhead
Citation assembly design is well-thought-out
Feedback loop included from day one
concerns: Namespace filter injection mechanism not specified at code level
Full re-ingestion on every run will spike embedding costs for large spaces
Streaming SSE error handling path not described
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Namespace filter must be server-enforced from JWT, never user-supplied
recommendations: Explicitly document that namespace is extracted from Okta JWT group claims in the retriever middleware, not accepted as a query parameter
Add content_hash comparison in ingestion worker to skip re-embedding unchanged chunks

issue_updates.title: Namespace filter not enforced at pgvector query level
issue_updates.detail: Security section mentions namespace scoping but does not specify server-side injection from JWT claims

previous_plan.title: Developer Documentation AI Assistant v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A RAG-based documentation assistant using Next.js frontend, FastAPI backend, pgvector on PostgreSQL 16, and GPT-4o to answer natural language questions about internal architecture and coding conventions from Confluence and GitHub wiki sources.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 SPA communicating via REST to FastAPI backend on ECS Fargate. LangChain RetrievalQA chain performs semantic search over pgvector index and generates GPT-4o answers with citations. Ingestion worker ECS service crawls Confluence spaces and embeds chunks.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC authentication. Namespace-scoped vector search described conceptually. No document content in logs. OpenAI DPA in place.
previous_plan.data_model: knowledge_sources, document_chunks with pgvector embeddings, query_sessions, user_feedback_ratings, ingestion_jobs.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must close the critical namespace filter gap by explicitly specifying server-side JWT claim injection into every pgvector query, and add content_hash change detection to ingestion jobs. The namespace filter is the primary access control preventing cross-team document leakage - it must be architecturally impossible to bypass via user input. The incremental ingestion fix reduces OpenAI embedding costs significantly for large Confluence spaces.

fix_report.action_taken: Explicitly specified that the pgvector retriever extracts the allowed_namespaces list from the validated Okta JWT group claims in FastAPI middleware, injects it as a mandatory metadata filter on every pgvector similarity_search call, and that the namespace parameter is never accepted from request body or query string. Added a custom LangChain retriever subclass that enforces this pattern and raises an AuthorizationError if namespace claims are missing.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No vector search query can execute without a server-derived namespace filter, making cross-team document leakage architecturally impossible regardless of frontend input.

target_output.title: Developer Documentation AI Assistant - RAG Architecture with Namespace-Scoped Retrieval
target_output.executive_summary: A RAG-powered internal documentation assistant built with Next.js 14, FastAPI, and pgvector on PostgreSQL 16 that answers natural language questions about system architecture, coding conventions, and ADRs from Confluence and GitHub wiki sources. GPT-4o generates answers with inline citations; namespace-scoped vector retrieval derived from Okta JWT group claims prevents cross-team document leakage. Incremental ingestion with content_hash change detection minimizes OpenAI embedding costs on re-ingestion runs.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture: Next.js 14 frontend served via CloudFront plus S3 static export, communicating over HTTPS to FastAPI on ECS Fargate behind an ALB with Okta JWT validation at the ALB listener level. FastAPI RAG pipeline uses a custom LangChain retriever that injects namespace filters from JWT claims into every pgvector similarity search. Ingestion ECS worker service pulls crawl jobs from SQS, fetches Confluence pages via REST API, chunks documents, generates text-embedding-3-small embeddings via OpenAI, and upserts to pgvector with content_hash change detection skipping unchanged chunks. Answer tokens stream to the frontend via SSE. User feedback ratings feed a quality tracking dashboard visible to documentation owners.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, NextAuth.js with Okta provider, Zustand, React Query. Backend: Python 3.12 FastAPI with async endpoints and SSE streaming, LangChain RetrievalQA with custom namespace-enforcing retriever, openai Python SDK. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension, HNSW index on document_chunks.embedding. Storage: S3 for raw Confluence exports and chunk archives. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate for API and worker, ALB with Okta JWT authorizer, SQS for ingestion job queue, CloudFront for frontend. Observability: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Query Interface: natural language question input with streaming answer display and inline citation cards linking to source documents. Answer History: paginated session history with previous questions and answers per user. Feedback Loop: thumbs up/down rating per answer with optional comment, stored for documentation owner review. Source Management: admin UI for adding Confluence spaces or GitHub wiki repos as knowledge sources, triggering ingestion jobs, and monitoring ingestion progress. Incremental Ingestion: content_hash comparison skips re-embedding unchanged chunks; only new or modified pages generate new embedding API calls.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront distribution serving Next.js static export from S3. ALB with Okta JWT authorizer validating tokens before forwarding to ECS. FastAPI ECS Fargate service with custom LangChain namespace-enforcing retriever. PostgreSQL 16 RDS with pgvector HNSW index in private subnet. Ingestion worker ECS Fargate service consuming SQS crawl jobs. OpenAI API for GPT-4o generation and text-embedding-3-small embeddings (external, DPA in place). S3 bucket for raw document exports and chunk archives. Datadog agent sidecar on API and worker tasks.
target_output.workflows: Query and Answer: engineer submits question via chat UI; NextAuth JWT forwarded to FastAPI; middleware extracts allowed_namespaces from Okta group claims; custom retriever calls pgvector similarity_search with namespace metadata filter IN allowed_namespaces and top_k=5; top chunks assembled into context window with source metadata; LangChain chain sends system prompt plus context plus question to GPT-4o with streaming enabled; tokens written to SSE response as they arrive; citation chunk IDs appended in final SSE event; query_session record written with token_usage and latency. Incremental Ingestion: admin triggers ingestion for a Confluence space; SQS job created; worker fetches all pages in space via Confluence REST API; for each page computes content_hash and compares to stored hash in document_chunks; unchanged pages skipped; new or modified pages chunked by section boundary, each chunk sent to text-embedding-3-small API, upserted to pgvector with new content_hash; ingestion_job progress updated; admin notified on completion.
target_output.data_model: knowledge_sources (id UUID PK, source_type ENUM confluence/github_wiki, source_url VARCHAR, namespace VARCHAR NOT NULL, team_id VARCHAR, last_ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, chunk_count INT, created_at). document_chunks (id UUID PK, source_id UUID FK, chunk_index INT, content_hash VARCHAR NOT NULL, namespace VARCHAR NOT NULL, content_text TEXT NOT NULL, embedding vector(1536) NOT NULL, metadata JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ; HNSW index on embedding with ef_construction=128). query_sessions (id UUID PK, user_id VARCHAR NOT NULL, query_text_hash VARCHAR, answer_text TEXT, citation_chunk_ids UUID[], model_version VARCHAR, token_usage INT, latency_ms INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). user_feedback_ratings (id UUID PK, query_session_id UUID FK, user_id VARCHAR, rating rating_enum NOT NULL, comment TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ingestion_jobs (id UUID PK, source_id UUID FK, status ingestion_status_enum NOT NULL, chunks_processed INT DEFAULT 0, chunks_skipped INT DEFAULT 0, error_detail TEXT, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/query body={question: str} streams answer tokens via SSE; final event includes citation_chunks array with source_url, chunk_index, and excerpt. GET /v1/history?page=&limit= returns paginated query_sessions for authenticated user. POST /v1/feedback/:query_session_id body={rating: positive|negative, comment: str}. POST /v1/admin/sources body={source_type, source_url, namespace, team_id} triggers ingestion SQS job. GET /v1/admin/sources lists all knowledge_sources with last_ingested_at and chunk_count. GET /v1/admin/sources/:job_id returns ingestion_job status and progress counters. All endpoints require Okta JWT; admin endpoints require platform-admins group membership.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC via NextAuth.js; ALB Okta JWT authorizer validates tokens before requests reach ECS. Namespace enforcement: FastAPI middleware extracts allowed_namespaces list from validated JWT group claims; custom LangChain retriever subclass injects namespace filter as mandatory pgvector metadata filter on every similarity_search call; namespace is never accepted from request parameters. No document chunk text written to structured logs; query text stored only as SHA-256 hash in query_sessions. OpenAI API calls include document chunk text; OpenAI DPA signed and confirmed. SOC 2 Type II: access logged via ALB access logs and Datadog APM; data handling policy documented for proprietary documentation. GDPR: 90-day session retention policy enforced by scheduled cleanup job; users can delete their own query history via DELETE /v1/history.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions: Python ruff lint, mypy strict type check, pytest with 80% coverage, Next.js build, Docker build and Trivy scan, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with health check on GET /health. Environments: dev (single AZ Fargate, shared RDS dev with pgvector), staging (multi-AZ Fargate, dedicated RDS), production (multi-AZ Fargate 2 tasks min / 6 max auto-scaling, RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.large with pgvector). Ingestion worker deployed as a separate ECS service with independent scaling from API service. SQS queue with DLQ for failed ingestion jobs. DB migrations via alembic applied in a pre-deploy ECS one-off task. Secrets in AWS Secrets Manager; Fargate task roles via IAM.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces for query endpoint with spans for JWT validation, pgvector retrieval, OpenAI call, and SSE streaming. Custom metrics: query_latency_ms p50/p95/p99 by namespace, chunks_retrieved_per_query histogram, embedding_generation_throughput chunks/min, feedback_positive_rate rolling 7-day, ingestion_chunks_per_minute, openai_token_spend_daily tracked against budget. Alerts: query p99 > 10s (PagerDuty P2), ingestion job failure count > 0 (email to admin), feedback_positive_rate < 0.6 over 24 hours (Slack notification to documentation team).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate API 2 tasks at 1 vCPU / 2GB approximately $150/month. RDS db.r6g.large Multi-AZ approximately $350/month. OpenAI embedding at 500K chunks initial ingestion approximately $10 one-time; incremental re-ingestion with change detection estimated $2/day ongoing. GPT-4o answer generation at 1000 queries/day with 3K input tokens each approximately $180/month. Total approximately $700/month at pilot scale. Scale path: ECS auto-scales on CPU target 60%; RDS read replica for history queries at higher load.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP Pilot (6 weeks): Confluence ingestion with incremental change detection, pgvector HNSW index, server-side namespace filter enforcement from JWT, GPT-4o streaming answers with citations, feedback rating capture, Okta auth, pilot with one engineering team. Phase 2 Org Rollout (6 weeks): GitHub wiki ingestion connector, multi-team namespace onboarding, feedback quality dashboard for documentation owners, admin source management UI, 90-day session cleanup job. Phase 3 Quality Improvements (ongoing): answer confidence scoring, proactive stale answer detection when source documents change, Slack thread ingestion connector.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python ruff and mypy strict in CI. Namespace filter injection must be covered by integration tests asserting that queries without valid JWT namespace claims return 403 and never reach pgvector. LangChain retriever subclass must be unit tested with mock pgvector calls verifying namespace filter is always present. No document chunk text in any logger.info/debug/warning calls. PR requires two approvals for changes to the retriever or JWT middleware. OpenAI token usage logged per query for cost attribution to teams.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: pgvector HNSW index recall degrades at very high chunk counts above 5M chunks. Mitigation: IVFFlat fallback with periodic index rebuild; monitor recall quality via weekly benchmark against held-out evaluation set. Risk: OpenAI API latency spikes causing poor perceived answer quality. Mitigation: streaming SSE masks generation latency; 30-second hard timeout with graceful degradation message. Tradeoff: pgvector over dedicated vector DB (Pinecone/Weaviate) simplifies operations but caps throughput at PostgreSQL connection limits; acceptable at current scale, revisit above 100K daily queries. Risk: Confluence API rate limits slowing large space ingestion. Mitigation: exponential backoff retry with jitter; ingestion jobs designed for resumability via chunk offset tracking.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed pgvector over Pinecone to avoid additional managed service at current scale. Confirmed text-embedding-3-small over ada-002 for better retrieval quality at similar cost. Confirmed namespace derived from Okta group claims server-side, never from user request. Confirmed 90-day session retention aligns with GDPR data minimization principle.

metadata.notes: Round 2 closes critical namespace filter enforcement gap; incremental ingestion with content_hash detection added to address cost risk.

profile.domain: Developer Tooling / Database Schema Management

project_goal.value: Native desktop application for database schema management allowing developers to visually design, migrate, and compare schemas across PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite with team collaboration via a shared backend API
project_goal.rationale: Replacing scattered migration script management and mental schema models with a visual tool that supports team review workflows

target_users.value: Backend developers and database administrators managing schema evolution across multiple database engines
target_users.rationale: Primary users are developers who write migrations and DBAs who review schema changes before production apply

project_class.rationale: Tauri-based native desktop app with local database connection management and a backend API for team collaboration features

capabilities.rationale: Desktop GUI, Rust Axum API, schema storage, JWT auth, and live DB introspection integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-engine schema introspection and diff computation are complex but the scope is well-bounded

risk_level.rationale: App connects to production databases from developer machines; connection credential handling is the primary risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: Schema structures and migration scripts are internal engineering artifacts; not customer PII

external_exposure.rationale: Backend API requires JWT authentication; desktop app authenticates on first launch

access_model.value: JWT authentication per developer; team workspace membership controls schema snapshot visibility and review request assignment
access_model.rationale: Team workspace scoping prevents schema leakage across unrelated product teams

feature_scope.value: Schema introspection from live databases, visual ER diagram, schema snapshot sync to team workspace, two-snapshot diff with color-coded output, migration script review requests with inline comments, peer approval workflow
feature_scope.rationale: Core developer workflow from schema exploration through migration review to apply

mvp_scope.value: PostgreSQL introspection, schema snapshot sync, two-snapshot diff API, migration script submission for review, comment threads, single-approver sign-off
mvp_scope.rationale: PostgreSQL-first covers the majority of users; MySQL and SQLite can follow in post-MVP

security_baseline.value: Database connection credentials stored in OS keychain via Tauri keyring plugin, never transmitted to backend API; JWT tokens stored in Tauri secure storage
security_baseline.rationale: Connection credentials must never leave the developer machine

frontend_stack.rationale: Tauri provides native desktop shell with Rust backend process; React for UI components; smaller binary than Electron

backend_stack.value: Rust with Axum for the collaboration API
backend_stack.rationale: Rust matches the Tauri native process runtime; Axum is async and performant for the API surface

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for team collaboration data (snapshots, reviews, comments); SQLite embedded in Tauri for local app state
data_platform.rationale: Postgres for shared collaboration state; SQLite for local connection profiles and offline-capable workspace cache

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate for Axum collaboration API; desktop app distributed via GitHub Releases as signed binaries for macOS, Windows, Linux
hosting_target.rationale: API on Fargate for managed hosting; desktop binary via GitHub Releases with Tauri updater for auto-updates

privacy_retention_policy.value: Schema snapshots retained as long as workspace exists; deleted workspace data purged within 30 days; migration scripts and review threads retained indefinitely as audit trail
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Migration history is engineering audit trail; workspace data follows workspace lifecycle

future_scope.value: MySQL and SQLite engine support, AI-assisted migration script suggestions, schema drift detection with alerting, GitOps export to Liquibase or Flyway format
future_scope.rationale: Multi-engine support and GitOps export are the most requested post-MVP features

constraints.value: Database connection credentials must never be transmitted to the backend API; app must function offline for local connection introspection and diff without API connectivity
constraints.rationale: Credential security and offline capability are baseline requirements for developer tool adoption

observability_baseline.value: Grafana plus Prometheus for API latency and error rate; structured JSON logging for diff computation errors and introspection failures
observability_baseline.rationale: API monitoring sufficient for a developer tool at this scale

execution_preference.value: Fortnightly releases with beta channel via Tauri updater for early adopters
execution_preference.rationale: Beta channel allows rapid iteration with willing testers before stable channel promotion

compliance_context.value: Internal security baseline policy: connection credentials in OS keychain; schema data classified as internal; no customer PII in schema snapshots
compliance_context.rationale: risk_level=medium with internal data sensitivity triggers compliance context requirement

project.goal: Visual desktop schema management with team collaboration via shared API
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks, stable release in 16 weeks

frontend.state: Zustand for local app state, React Query for API calls
frontend.storage: SQLite via Tauri plugin for local profiles and workspace cache

security.auth: Custom JWT issued by Axum API
security.credential_storage: OS keychain via Tauri keyring plugin

constraints.offline: Local introspection and diff without API

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope split between local-only operations (introspection, diff) and API-required operations (snapshot sync, review) is the right architectural boundary. Offline capability for the core developer workflow is critical for adoption. The review and approval flow mirrors pull request patterns that developers already understand.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Tauri plus Axum in the same language ecosystem is a strong choice for maintainability. The diff algorithm for schema comparison needs to handle column renames (as opposed to drop-plus-add) intelligently; a naive approach will generate noisy migration scripts. The two-database diff should be computable entirely client-side using the local SQLite snapshot cache without API round-trips.
reasoner_reviews.security: Credential storage in the OS keychain via Tauri keyring is correct and must be enforced in the Tauri Rust backend process, not the React frontend. The JWT for API auth must be separate from any database credential. Review request notifications can use desktop OS notifications rather than email to avoid storing user email addresses in the backend.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Offline introspection is achievable since the Tauri Rust process connects directly to the database. Diff computation on cached snapshots in local SQLite is also offline-capable. Only snapshot push and review workflows require API connectivity.

backend.service_design: Axum app with modules: workspaces (team workspace CRUD), snapshots (snapshot upload and versioning), diffs (diff computation API for remote snapshots), reviews (migration script review request lifecycle), comments (inline comment threads), auth (JWT issue and validate). Tauri Rust sidecar process handles local DB introspection, local diff computation, and keychain access separately from the collaboration API.
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/workspaces creates team workspace. POST /v1/snapshots/:workspace_id uploads schema snapshot JSON. GET /v1/diffs?base=:snap_id&head=:snap_id returns structured diff. POST /v1/reviews submits migration script for review. GET /v1/reviews/:id returns review with comment threads. POST /v1/reviews/:id/comments adds inline comment. POST /v1/reviews/:id/approve signs off with approver identity.
backend.failure_handling: Snapshot upload conflict (same schema hash already exists) returns 409 with existing snapshot ID. Diff computation timeout after 10 seconds returns 408 with partial result flag. Review approval on already-approved review returns 422 with current state.

frontend.app_structure: Tauri 2.0 shell with React 18 SPA. Tauri commands expose Rust sidecar functions: introspect_schema, compute_local_diff, read_connection_profiles, save_connection_profile_to_keychain. React routes: /connections (profile manager), /schema/:conn_id (ER diagram), /diff (two-snapshot diff viewer), /reviews (review list and detail), /workspaces (team workspace switcher).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Connection Manager: add/edit/test database connections stored in OS keychain. Schema Explorer: ER diagram of introspected schema with table and column details, snapshot push button. Diff Viewer: two-panel color-coded diff of added/removed/altered tables and columns with migration script preview. Review Detail: migration script with inline comment anchors, approval button, reviewer assignment.
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for active connection, current workspace, and local introspection cache. React Query for all Axum API calls with offline fallback to local SQLite cache for snapshot list.

security.auth_design: JWT issued by Axum on successful credential exchange. Stored in Tauri secure storage (not React state). Included in Authorization header for all API calls. 8-hour expiry with silent refresh.
security.secrets_management: Database connection passwords stored exclusively in OS keychain via Tauri keyring Rust plugin. Connection profiles stored in local SQLite contain only host, port, database name, and username - never password. Tauri security policy blocks webview JavaScript from calling keychain commands directly.
security.authorization_model: Workspace membership checked on every API endpoint. Snapshot visibility scoped to workspace. Review requests visible only to workspace members. Approver role required for review approval.

data.entities: team_workspaces (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, owner_user_id VARCHAR, created_at). workspace_members (id UUID PK, workspace_id FK, user_id VARCHAR, role ENUM member/approver/owner). schema_snapshots (id UUID PK, workspace_id FK, connection_label VARCHAR, db_engine ENUM postgres/mysql/sqlite, schema_json JSONB NOT NULL, schema_hash VARCHAR, captured_by VARCHAR, captured_at TIMESTAMPTZ). schema_diff_reports (id UUID PK, workspace_id FK, base_snapshot_id FK, head_snapshot_id FK, diff_json JSONB, computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). review_requests (id UUID PK, workspace_id FK, migration_script TEXT NOT NULL, title VARCHAR, author_user_id VARCHAR, base_snapshot_id FK, status ENUM open/approved/rejected/applied, created_at). review_comments (id UUID PK, review_id FK, author_user_id VARCHAR, line_number INT, comment_text TEXT, created_at). column_annotations (id UUID PK, snapshot_id FK, table_name VARCHAR, column_name VARCHAR, annotation_text TEXT, created_by VARCHAR).
data.schema_notes: schema_json in schema_snapshots stores the full normalized schema representation as JSONB enabling server-side diff computation. schema_hash allows deduplication of identical snapshots. Local SQLite mirrors schema_snapshots and schema_diff_reports for offline access.
data.migration_notes: Axum API uses sqlx with PostgreSQL for persistence. Local SQLite managed by the Tauri Rust process using rusqlite. Migrations applied at app startup for SQLite; via sqlx migrate for PostgreSQL.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate Axum API service behind ALB in private subnet (internal to VPC, accessible via VPN for remote developers). Desktop binaries built via GitHub Actions matrix (macOS, Windows, Linux) and published to GitHub Releases. Tauri updater checks GitHub Releases endpoint on app launch.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Rust cargo test, cargo clippy, cargo audit for dependency CVEs. Cross-compilation matrix for macOS arm64, Windows x64, Linux x64 via cross-rs. Tauri build and sign with Apple and Windows code signing certs stored in GitHub Secrets. Docker build for Axum API, Trivy scan, ECR push, ECS deploy.
devops.rollback_strategy: Desktop: previous version always downloadable from GitHub Releases; Tauri updater supports downgrade via manual version pin in config. API: ECS blue/green with ALB cutover. Database: sqlx migrations are additive-only.

ISS-001.title: Column rename detection missing from diff algorithm spec
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 diff spec treated renamed columns as drop-plus-add, generating migration scripts that would destroy column data. Rename detection via levenshtein distance or user-confirmed rename mapping is required.

ISS-002.title: Tauri webview JavaScript can access keychain commands
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan did not specify that Tauri allowlist should block webview JS from invoking keychain Tauri commands. Only the Rust backend process should call the keyring plugin.

focus_issues.title: Column rename detection missing from diff algorithm spec
focus_issues.detail: Naive drop-plus-add would destroy column data on renamed columns.

focus_issues.title: Tauri webview JavaScript can access keychain commands
focus_issues.detail: Tauri allowlist must block webview JS from invoking keyring plugin commands.

latest_recommendations: Add column rename detection to diff algorithm using similarity scoring with user confirmation for ambiguous cases
Restrict Tauri command allowlist so keyring commands are only callable from Rust backend, not webview JS

convergence_state.reason: Column rename detection gap produces data-destructive migrations; Tauri allowlist security gap unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Solid desktop architecture with good Tauri/Axum stack choice, but the diff algorithm has a data safety flaw and the Tauri security allowlist is not specified.
strengths: Credential isolation in OS keychain is well-designed
Offline capability for local introspection is correctly identified
Review workflow mirrors familiar PR patterns
concerns: Diff algorithm treats column renames as drop-plus-add, risking data loss in generated migrations
Tauri allowlist not specified, leaving webview JS able to call keychain commands
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Column rename detection required before migration script generation
recommendations: Implement column rename detection using name similarity scoring; surface ambiguous cases as user-confirmed renames in the diff UI
Explicitly configure Tauri allowlist to expose keyring commands only to Rust backend, not React webview

issue_updates.title: Column rename detection missing
issue_updates.detail: Diff section describes structural diff but does not address rename vs. drop-add disambiguation

issue_updates.title: Tauri allowlist not specified
issue_updates.detail: Security section does not mention Tauri command allowlist configuration

previous_plan.title: Schema Manager Desktop App v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Tauri 2.0 desktop application for database schema management with React frontend and Axum Rust API for team collaboration, supporting PostgreSQL introspection, schema snapshot sync, and migration script review workflows.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Tauri shell with React SPA for local operations; Axum API on ECS Fargate for team collaboration. Local PostgreSQL connections via Tauri Rust process. Schema snapshots stored as JSONB in PostgreSQL collaboration DB. OS keychain for connection credential storage.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: JWT auth for API. Connection passwords in OS keychain. TLS 1.3 in transit.
previous_plan.data_model: team_workspaces, schema_snapshots with schema JSONB, schema_diff_reports, review_requests, review_comments.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must close the column rename detection gap (data-safety issue in migration generation) and the Tauri allowlist security gap. The rename detection uses Levenshtein-based column name similarity scoring with a user-confirmation step for ambiguous pairs. The Tauri allowlist is now explicitly specified to restrict keyring commands to the Rust backend process only. Both fixes are architectural changes that touch the diff algorithm design and the Tauri security configuration.

fix_report.action_taken: Added rename detection phase to the schema diff algorithm: after computing added and removed column sets, pairs where name Levenshtein distance is under 3 and data types match are surfaced as candidate renames in the diff UI. The user confirms or rejects each candidate before the migration script is generated. Confirmed renames produce RENAME COLUMN statements instead of DROP plus ADD. Unconfirmed pairs remain as drop-add with a data loss warning in the UI.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Generated migration scripts preserve column data on renames, eliminating the data-destructive DROP-ADD pattern for the common case of column renaming.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit Tauri 2.0 capability configuration: keyring plugin commands (save_credential, get_credential, delete_credential) are defined as Rust-side commands only, not exposed in the allowlist to the React webview. Webview JS calls a Tauri command get_connection_password_for_test which invokes the keyring indirectly through a Rust handler that validates the request context before keychain access.
fix_report.expected_outcome: React webview JavaScript cannot directly invoke keychain operations; all keyring access is mediated by Rust handlers with validated call context.

target_output.title: Schema Manager Desktop App - Visual Schema Design and Migration Review Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A Tauri 2.0 desktop application with React 18 frontend and Rust Axum collaboration API that enables developers to introspect PostgreSQL schemas, generate visual ER diagrams, sync schema snapshots to team workspaces, and submit migration scripts for peer review with inline comments and approver sign-off. Connection passwords are stored exclusively in the OS keychain via Rust-side keyring access; the diff algorithm includes column rename detection to prevent data-destructive migration generation. The app supports offline introspection and local diff computation without API connectivity.
target_output.architecture_overview: Tauri 2.0 shell hosts a React 18 SPA for the desktop UI and a Rust sidecar process that manages local database connections, schema introspection via native PostgreSQL driver, local SQLite for connection profiles and offline snapshot cache, and OS keychain access via the keyring plugin. The sidecar exposes Tauri commands to the React frontend for local operations. A separate Axum REST API on AWS ECS Fargate handles team collaboration features: snapshot upload and versioning, server-side diff computation between remote snapshots, migration script review requests, inline comment threads, and approver sign-off. Desktop binaries are distributed via GitHub Releases with Tauri updater for automatic updates.
target_output.technology_stack: Desktop: Tauri 2.0 with Rust sidecar, React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, React Query. Rust deps: tokio-postgres for introspection, rusqlite for local SQLite, keyring crate for OS keychain, levenshtein crate for rename detection. API: Rust 1.77 with Axum, tower middleware, sqlx for PostgreSQL. Database: PostgreSQL 16 for collaboration API; SQLite embedded for local state. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate for Axum API, GitHub Releases for desktop binaries, GitHub Actions for cross-compilation matrix. Observability: Grafana plus Prometheus for API metrics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Connection Manager: add PostgreSQL connections with password stored in OS keychain; test connection from Rust sidecar; profiles persisted to local SQLite. Schema Explorer: introspect live schema via tokio-postgres; render ER diagram with tables, columns, data types, foreign keys, and indexes; push snapshot to team workspace. Diff Viewer: select two snapshots (local or workspace); compute diff showing added, removed, altered tables and columns; rename detection phase surfaces candidate column renames for user confirmation; generate migration SQL preview with RENAME COLUMN for confirmed renames and data-loss warning for drop-add pairs. Review Workflow: submit migration script as review request to workspace; reviewers leave inline comments anchored to script line numbers; single approver sign-off marks review as approved and ready to apply.
target_output.system_components: Tauri 2.0 shell process managing app lifecycle and update check via GitHub Releases endpoint. React 18 SPA rendered in webview with restricted capability allowlist. Rust sidecar process: schema introspector (tokio-postgres), local diff engine (levenshtein-based rename detection), SQLite manager (rusqlite), keychain accessor (keyring crate - Rust-only, not exposed to webview JS). Axum ECS Fargate API: workspace, snapshot, diff, review, comment, auth modules. PostgreSQL 16 RDS for collaboration data. Local SQLite database (per-user, stored in Tauri app data directory) for connection profiles and offline snapshot cache. ALB in private subnet (accessible via corporate VPN).
target_output.workflows: Schema Introspection and Snapshot Push: developer opens connection in app; Rust sidecar retrieves password from OS keychain (never exposed to React state); tokio-postgres driver connects and executes information_schema queries for tables, columns, constraints, and indexes; result normalized to schema_json; React renders ER diagram; developer clicks Push to Workspace; React calls Axum POST /v1/snapshots/:workspace_id with schema_json and connection label; server stores snapshot with schema_hash for deduplication and returns snapshot_id. Diff with Rename Detection: developer selects base and head snapshots; if both in local SQLite cache, diff computed client-side in Rust sidecar with levenshtein rename detection; dropped-added column pairs with name distance under 3 and matching types surfaced as rename candidates in confirmation modal; developer confirms or rejects each; confirmed renames produce RENAME COLUMN in generated migration SQL; rejected pairs produce DROP plus ADD with data-loss warning badge. Migration Review: developer submits migration script via Axum POST /v1/reviews; workspace members receive desktop OS notification via Tauri notification API; reviewer opens review detail in app, leaves inline comments on specific lines; approver clicks Approve; review status set to approved; author notified via desktop notification.
target_output.data_model: team_workspaces (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR NOT NULL, owner_user_id VARCHAR NOT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). workspace_members (id UUID PK, workspace_id UUID FK, user_id VARCHAR NOT NULL, role workspace_role_enum NOT NULL). schema_snapshots (id UUID PK, workspace_id UUID FK, connection_label VARCHAR, db_engine db_engine_enum NOT NULL, schema_json JSONB NOT NULL, schema_hash VARCHAR NOT NULL, captured_by VARCHAR, captured_at TIMESTAMPTZ; UNIQUE(workspace_id, schema_hash) for deduplication). schema_diff_reports (id UUID PK, workspace_id UUID FK, base_snapshot_id UUID FK, head_snapshot_id UUID FK, diff_json JSONB NOT NULL, rename_confirmations JSONB, computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). review_requests (id UUID PK, workspace_id UUID FK, title VARCHAR NOT NULL, migration_script TEXT NOT NULL, author_user_id VARCHAR, base_snapshot_id UUID FK, status review_status_enum NOT NULL DEFAULT open, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). review_comments (id UUID PK, review_id UUID FK, author_user_id VARCHAR, line_number INT, comment_text TEXT NOT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). column_annotations (id UUID PK, snapshot_id UUID FK, table_name VARCHAR, column_name VARCHAR, annotation_text TEXT, created_by VARCHAR). Local SQLite mirrors schema_snapshots (schema_json omitted) and review_requests list for offline access.
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/auth/token accepts username/password, returns JWT with 8-hour expiry. POST /v1/workspaces creates workspace, returns workspace_id. POST /v1/workspaces/:id/members adds member with role. POST /v1/snapshots/:workspace_id uploads schema_json, returns snapshot_id or existing ID on hash collision (409). GET /v1/snapshots/:workspace_id lists snapshots with captured_at and connection_label. GET /v1/diffs?base=:id&head=:id returns diff_json with rename candidates. POST /v1/reviews submits migration script for review. GET /v1/reviews/:workspace_id lists reviews with status. GET /v1/reviews/:id returns full review with comment threads. POST /v1/reviews/:id/comments adds inline comment. POST /v1/reviews/:id/approve requires approver role. All endpoints require Authorization JWT header.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Credential isolation: database passwords stored in OS keychain via Rust keyring crate in Tauri sidecar process; Tauri 2.0 capability config exposes no keyring commands to React webview JS; webview triggers connection test via a Rust-mediated command that fetches the credential internally without returning it to JS. JWT: issued by Axum on login, stored in Tauri secure storage plugin, injected into API requests by React Query interceptor, 8-hour expiry with silent refresh. Tauri allowlist: webview JS allowed commands explicitly enumerate only safe local operations (introspect_schema, compute_local_diff, list_connections) with keyring access scoped to Rust handlers only. TLS 1.3 on all API connections. Workspace scoping enforced on every Axum endpoint via middleware extracting workspace_id from JWT claims. Internal security baseline: schema data classified as internal, no customer PII in snapshots.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: cargo test, cargo clippy, cargo audit (CVE scan). Cross-compilation matrix: macOS arm64 (Apple silicon codesign), Windows x64 (EV certificate), Linux x64 (AppImage). Tauri build produces signed installers for each platform. Published to GitHub Releases as draft; promoted to stable on QA sign-off. Tauri updater checks GitHub Releases JSON endpoint on app launch. Axum API: Docker build, Trivy scan, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy on Fargate. Environments: dev (single AZ Fargate, shared RDS dev), staging, production (multi-AZ Fargate, RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium). DB migrations via sqlx-cli applied as ECS one-off task pre-deploy.
target_output.observability: Grafana plus Prometheus for Axum API: http_request_duration_seconds histogram by endpoint, snapshot_upload_count counter, diff_computation_duration_seconds histogram, review_requests_created counter, auth_failures counter. Desktop app: Tauri crash reporter sends anonymized error reports (no schema content) to a self-hosted Sentry instance. Alerts: Axum error rate > 1% for 5 minutes, diff computation p99 > 5 seconds, auth failure rate > 10/minute.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate 2 tasks at 0.5 vCPU / 1GB approximately $80/month. RDS PostgreSQL db.t3.medium Multi-AZ approximately $140/month. GitHub Actions build minutes for cross-platform matrix approximately $30/month. Total API infrastructure under $300/month. Desktop app distribution via GitHub Releases is zero marginal cost per download. Fargate auto-scales on CPU target 60% for larger teams.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): PostgreSQL introspection via tokio-postgres, OS keychain credential storage, schema snapshot push and sync, two-snapshot diff with column rename detection, migration script review and single-approver sign-off, JWT auth, ECS Fargate Axum API deployment, GitHub Releases desktop distribution for macOS and Windows. Phase 2 Engine Expansion (6 weeks): MySQL 8 and SQLite introspection adapters behind a common schema normalizer trait, engine-specific migration SQL generation. Phase 3 Enhancements (ongoing): AI-assisted migration script suggestions via OpenAI API, schema drift detection comparing latest snapshot to live DB on a schedule, GitOps export to Flyway or Liquibase format.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust edition 2021 with clippy deny warnings. cargo audit in CI for dependency CVEs. Tauri commands must be documented with allowed callers (Rust-only or webview). Column rename detection threshold (levenshtein distance < 3) is a configurable constant, not a magic number. Integration tests for diff algorithm cover: column add, column remove, column rename, table rename, type change, and index change scenarios. Review approval endpoint covered by role-enforcement integration tests. No schema JSONB content written to application logs.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Levenshtein-based rename detection produces false positives for columns with coincidentally similar names and same type. Mitigation: user confirmation step before rename is applied to migration; user can always reject to fall back to explicit drop-add. Risk: Local SQLite cache grows unbounded for users with many workspaces and snapshots. Mitigation: cache eviction policy deletes entries older than 30 days not accessed recently. Tradeoff: OS keychain over in-app encrypted storage provides better security but requires OS-level unlock on each app launch; acceptable for a developer tool used during work sessions. Tradeoff: Tauri over Electron reduces binary size from ~150MB to ~15MB but limits some npm ecosystem dependencies; all required packages have Rust equivalents.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed PostgreSQL-only for MVP with MySQL and SQLite in Phase 2 to bound scope. Confirmed Levenshtein distance threshold of 3 with user confirmation for rename detection, not automatic rename application. Confirmed OS keychain over encrypted SQLite for connection passwords. Confirmed GitHub Releases over a custom update server for binary distribution.

metadata.notes: Round 2 closes column rename detection data-safety gap and Tauri allowlist security gap; plan is solid with minor thinness in cost section.

profile.domain: Internal Platform Engineering

project_goal.value: Replace legacy file-based config management for a large monolith with REST endpoints for reading and writing configuration values scoped to service, environment, and namespace hierarchies, supporting atomic versioning, rollback, and change event webhooks
project_goal.rationale: Monolith reads configs from mounted files causing deploy-time restarts and no change history; API replaces this with a live config bus

target_users.value: Internal microservices consuming configuration at startup and runtime; platform operators managing config values; SRE team performing emergency rollbacks
target_users.rationale: Both machine and human consumers identified in discovery

project_class.rationale: Headless REST service consumed programmatically by internal services

capabilities.rationale: Core API surface, versioned config persistence, Keycloak auth, REST endpoints

complexity_level.rationale: Namespace hierarchy resolution and atomic versioning add logic complexity above simple CRUD

risk_level.rationale: Config values may include database URLs and API keys; misconfiguration can cause service-wide outage

data_sensitivity.rationale: Config namespaces contain internal endpoint URLs and connection strings

external_exposure.rationale: API is reachable only within the private Azure VNet

access_model.value: Service identity tokens via Keycloak client credentials grant; human operators use OIDC authorization code flow with namespace-scoped role assignments
access_model.rationale: Machine-to-machine and human operator access require separate grant types

feature_scope.value: Namespace hierarchy config reads with inheritance resolution; atomic versioned writes; per-namespace rollback to any previous version; webhook fanout to registered subscribers on config change; read-only audit log of all mutations
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set agreed in design review

mvp_scope.value: Config read with namespace inheritance, versioned write with rollback, Keycloak auth, webhook delivery for change events
